topics.title: Overview of Protein Chemistry, Proteomics and its Application
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEBBT756 - PROTEOMICS & PROTEIN ENGINEERING > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Overview of Protein Chemistry, Proteomics and its Application ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Protein Chemistry — Formal Definition **Proteins** are high-molecular-weight, nitrogen-containing biological macromopolymers (biopolymers) composed of one or more **polypeptide chains** of **α-amino acid** residues linked together by **peptide (amide) bonds**. The word *protein* originates from the Greek *proteios*, meaning "of the first rank," signifying their biological primacy. A protein is formally defined as a linear, unbranched heteropolymer in which the monomeric units (α-amino acids) are joined head-to-tail through peptide bonds formed by a **condensation (dehydration) reaction** between the α-carboxyl group (–COOH) of one amino acid and the α-amino group (–NH₂) of the next, releasing one molecule of water per bond formed. The general empirical formula for an amino acid is: $$R-CH(NH_2)-COOH$$ where **R** denotes the variable side chain that distinguishes the **20 standard (proteinogenic) α-amino acids** encoded by the genetic code. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Students must clearly distinguish between an *amino acid*, a *peptide*, a *polypeptide*, and a *protein*. A dipeptide = 2 amino acids + 1 peptide bond; a tripeptide = 3 amino acids + 2 peptide bonds; for *n* residues, there are (*n* − 1) peptide bonds. ### 1.2 Proteomics — Formal Definition **Proteomics** is the large-scale, systematic, high-throughput study of the entire set of proteins (the **proteome**) expressed by a genome, cell, tissue, or organism at a given time under specific physiological or pathological conditions. The term **proteome** (coined in 1997 by Marc Wilkins, Macquarie University, Australia) is a portmanteau of **PROTEin** and **genOME**. Unlike the static genome, the proteome is **dynamic, spatial, and temporal** — it changes with cell type, developmental stage, environmental stimulus, and disease state. ### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition > [!NOTE] > **Plain-English Analogy — "The Cell as a Factory":** > > - **DNA** = The factory's master blueprint (static, stored in the archive room — the nucleus). > - **mRNA** = A working photocopy of one blueprint page sent to the shop floor. > - **Proteins** = The actual machines, workers, and products of the factory (do the real work). > - **Genomics** = Reading every page of the master blueprint. > - **Transcriptomics** = Counting how many copies of each page are floating around. > - **Proteomics** = Inspecting every machine in the factory — what it is, where it sits, what shape it has, whether it's turned on, and what other machines it's chatting with. > > Two machines (proteins) can be made from the same blueprint page (gene) via **alternative splicing** or **post-translational modifications (PTMs)** — this is why proteomics is harder and richer than genomics. ### 1.4 Key Standard Metrics (KTU High-Yield Constants) | Metric / Constant | Symbol | Standard Value | |---|---|---| | Average mass of an amino acid residue | $\bar{M}$ | **110 Da** | | Peptide bond length | $d$ | **1.33 Å** | | Number of standard amino acids | $n_{AA}$ | **20** | | Codons per amino acid (degeneracy) | – | **61 sense codons** | | Stop codons | – | **3 (UAA, UAG, UGA)** | | Isoelectric point average (proteins) | $pI$ | ~**5.5** (varies) | | Wavelength for UV protein quantification | $\lambda$ | **280 nm** | ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Hierarchical Organization of Protein Structure Proteins fold into four hierarchical levels of structural organization, each critical to biological function: **A. Primary Structure (1°)** — The **linear sequence** of amino acids joined by covalent peptide bonds, dictated by the corresponding gene. Determines all higher-order folding. **B. Secondary Structure (2°)** — Local, regular folding patterns stabilized by **hydrogen bonds** between the backbone carbonyl oxygen (C=O) and amide hydrogen (N–H). Major types: - **α-helix**: Right-handed, 3.6 residues/turn, pitch = **5.4 Å**, H-bond between residue *i* and *i + 4*. - **β-sheet (β-pleated sheet)**: Parallel or antiparallel strands connected by inter-strand H-bonds. - **β-turn / Random coil**: Reversals and unstructured loops. **C. Tertiary Structure (3°)** — The overall **3D compact globular or fibrous fold** of a single polypeptide chain, stabilized by: 1. Hydrophobic interactions (major driving force) 2. Hydrogen bonds 3. Ionic (salt) bridges 4. Van der Waals forces 5. Disulfide bridges (covalent, –S–S–) — only between two cysteine residues **D. Quaternary Structure (4°)** — The spatial arrangement of **multiple folded polypeptide subunits** into a functional oligomeric complex. Example: Hemoglobin (Hb) = 2 α + 2 β chains (tetramer). Stabilized by the same forces as 3° structure, plus subunit–subunit interfaces. ### 2.2 Classification of the 20 Standard Amino Acids By **polarity and charge of the side chain (R group)** at physiological pH (7.4): 1. **Non-polar (hydrophobic)**: Glycine (Gly, G), Alanine (Ala, A), Valine (Val, V), Leucine (Leu, L), Isoleucine (Ile, I), Proline (Pro, P), Phenylalanine (Phe, F), Tryptophan (Trp, W), Methionine (Met, M). 2. **Polar uncharged**: Serine (Ser, S), Threonine (Thr, T), Cysteine (Cys, C), Tyrosine (Tyr, Y), Asparagine (Asn, N), Glutamine (Gln, Q). 3. **Positively charged (basic)**: Lysine (Lys, K), Arginine (Arg, R), Histidine (His, H). 4. **Negatively charged (acidic)**: Aspartate (Asp, D), Glutamate (Glu, E). ### 2.3 Proteomics — Sub-disciplines and Workflow Proteomics is broadly classified into: - **Expression Proteomics** — Quantitative profiling of protein abundance across conditions (e.g., healthy vs. diseased). - **Structural Proteomics** — High-throughput determination of 3D protein structures. - **Functional Proteomics** — Protein–protein interactions (interactomics), signaling networks, enzyme activity. - **Clinical / Disease Proteomics** — Biomarker discovery, diagnostics, therapeutics. **General Proteomics Workflow:** $$\text{Sample} \rightarrow \text{Lysis} \rightarrow \text{Protein Extraction} \rightarrow \text{Digestion (Trypsin)} \rightarrow \text{Peptide Separation (LC)} \rightarrow \text{MS/MS Analysis} \rightarrow \text{Bioinformatics} \rightarrow \text{Identification / Quantification}$$ ### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!NOTE] > All formulas below are **high-yield** for KTU 2024 scheme ESE and CE-style questions in PEBBT756. | # | Formula / Equation | Description / Use | |---|---|---| | 1 | $n_{\text{peptide bonds}} = n_{\text{residues}} - 1$ | Number of bonds in a linear polypeptide | | 2 | $n_{\text{H}_2\text{O released}} = n_{\text{residues}} - 1$ | Water molecules lost during peptide synthesis | | 3 | $\varepsilon_{280} = 5500 \cdot n_{\text{Trp}} + 1490 \cdot n_{\text{Tyr}}$ | Molar extinction coefficient at 280 nm (M⁻¹cm⁻¹) | | 4 | $A_{280} = \varepsilon_{280} \cdot c \cdot l$ | Beer-Lambert law for protein quantification | | 5 | $c \left( \dfrac{mg}{mL} \right) = \dfrac{A_{280}}{\varepsilon_{0.1\%}}$ | Concentration using mass extinction coefficient | | 6 | $M_{\text{protein}} \approx \sum M_{\text{AA residues}} - 18 \cdot (n-1)$ | Molecular weight from amino acid composition | | 7 | $pI = \dfrac{pK_{a1} + pK_{a2}}{2}$ | Isoelectric point for neutral amino acids | | 8 | $\Delta G^{\circ} = -RT \ln K_{eq}$ | Free energy ↔ equilibrium constant (protein folding) | | 9 | $[\theta] = \dfrac{\theta}{10 \cdot c \cdot l}$ | Mean residue ellipticity (CD spectroscopy) | | 10 | $N_{\text{atoms}} = 5n_{\text{residues}}$ | Approx. atoms/100 residues for X-ray resolution benchmarks | ### 2.5 Real-World Utility - **Pharmaceutical Industry**: Drug target identification (e.g., kinases in cancer), antibody therapeutics. - **Clinical Diagnostics**: Disease biomarkers — e.g., troponin for myocardial infarction, PSA for prostate cancer. - **Agriculture**: Engineering pest-resistant crops via protein engineering. - **Industrial Biotechnology**: Designer enzymes for detergents, biofuels (lipases, cellulases). - **Personalized Medicine**: Patient-specific proteomic profiling guides tailored therapy. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation I — Number of Peptide Bonds and Water Molecules in a Polypeptide **Statement:** A linear polypeptide containing *n* amino acid residues is formed by *n − 1* peptide bonds, releasing *n − 1* water molecules. **Step-by-step logical derivation:** Consider the general condensation reaction: $$\text{H}_2\text{N-CHR}_1\text{-COOH} + \text{H}_2\text{N-CHR}_2\text{-COOH} \longrightarrow \text{H}_2\text{N-CHR}_1\text{-CO-NH-CHR}_2\text{-COOH} + \text{H}_2\text{O}$$ **Step 1:** Start with **2** amino acids → form **1** peptide bond → release **1** H₂O. **Step 2:** Add a third amino acid to the dipeptide → form **1 more** peptide bond → release **1 more** H₂O. Total: 3 residues, 2 bonds, 2 H₂O. **Step 3:** In general, each addition of **one** new amino acid to a growing chain produces exactly **one** peptide bond and **one** water molecule. **Step 4:** Mathematical induction. Starting with 1 residue (0 bonds), each successive addition adds 1 bond. Therefore, for *n* residues: $$\boxed{n_{\text{bonds}} = n_{\text{residues}} - 1 = n - 1}$$ $$\boxed{n_{\text{H}_2\text{O}} = n - 1}$$ ### 3.2 Derivation II — Molecular Weight of a Polypeptide from Amino Acid Composition **Step-by-step derivation:** **Step 1:** Free amino acids have molecular weights corresponding to their full structure (H₂N–CHR–COOH). Example molecular weights: | Amino Acid | Residue Formula | Free AA Mass (Da) | Residue Mass (Da) | |---|---|---|---| | Glycine | –H | 75.07 | 57.05 | | Alanine | –CH₃ | 89.09 | 71.08 | | Valine | –CH(CH₃)₂ | 117.15 | 99.13 | | Leucine | –CH₂CH(CH₃)₂ | 131.17 | 113.16 | | Serine | –CH₂OH | 105.09 | 87.08 | | Tryptophan | –C₈H₆N | 204.23 | 186.21 | | Lysine | –(CH₂)₄NH₂ | 146.19 | 128.17 | **Step 2:** The mass of a residue = free amino acid mass − mass of one water molecule (18.015 Da), because in peptide bond formation one H (from –NH₂) and one OH (from –COOH) are removed. **Step 3:** General formula. For a polypeptide with *n* residues whose average free-AA mass is $\bar{M}_{\text{free}}$: $$M_{\text{protein}} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} M_{\text{residue}, i} + 18.015$$ The +18.015 accounts for the extra H at the N-terminus and OH at the C-terminus that are retained in the free polypeptide (i.e., the terminal groups were never involved in a peptide bond on that end). **Step 4:** Quick estimate: $M_{\text{protein}} \approx 110 \cdot n$ Da (because $\bar{M}_{\text{residue}} \approx 110$ Da, and terminal H/OH ≈ 18). ### 3.3 Derivation III — Beer-Lambert Law for Protein Quantification at 280 nm **Statement:** Aromatic amino acids (Trp, Tyr, and to a lesser extent Phe and disulfides) absorb UV light at 280 nm. This enables concentration measurement via spectrophotometry. **Step-by-step derivation:** **Step 1:** Lambert's Law — the fraction of light absorbed by a medium is independent of incident intensity and depends only on the path length *l*: $$I = I_0 \cdot e^{-k l}$$ **Step 2:** Beer's Law — absorption is proportional to the concentration *c* of absorbing species: $$I = I_0 \cdot e^{-\varepsilon' c l}$$ **Step 3:** Combining and defining absorbance *A*: $$A = \log_{10}\left(\dfrac{I_0}{I}\right) = \varepsilon \cdot c \cdot l$$ where: - $A$ = absorbance (unitless, dimensionless) - $\varepsilon$ = molar extinction coefficient (M⁻¹·cm⁻¹) - $c$ = molar concentration (mol/L) - $l$ = optical path length (cm) **Step 4:** For proteins, $\varepsilon$ at 280 nm is computed additively from aromatic residue counts: $$\varepsilon_{280} = (5500) \cdot n_{\text{Trp}} + (1490) \cdot n_{\text{Tyr}} + (125) \cdot n_{\text{disulfide bonds}}$$ **Step 5:** Rearranging to solve for protein concentration: $$\boxed{c \left(\dfrac{mol}{L}\right) = \dfrac{A_{280}}{\varepsilon_{280} \cdot l}}$$ **Step 6:** Numerical example. A protein has 2 Trp and 6 Tyr residues. Calculate its concentration if $A_{280} = 0.825$ and $l = 1$ cm. $$\varepsilon_{280} = (5500)(2) + (1490)(6) = 11000 + 8940 = 19940 \;\; \text{M}^{-1}\text{cm}^{-1}$$ $$c = \dfrac{0.825}{19940 \cdot 1} = 4.137 \times 10^{-5} \;\; \text{M}$$ **Step 7:** Convert to mg/mL using $M_{\text{protein}} = 45000$ Da: $$c = 4.137 \times 10^{-5} \times 45000 \;\; \text{g/L} = 1.86 \;\; \text{g/L} = 1.86 \;\; \text{mg/mL}$$ ### 3.4 Derivation IV — Isoelectric Point (pI) of an Amino Acid **Step-by-step derivation:** **Step 1:** For a neutral amino acid (e.g., glycine) with two ionizable groups (α-COOH, pKₐ₁ ≈ 2.34 and α-NH₃⁺, pKₐ₂ ≈ 9.60), titration begins at low pH (fully protonated cation) and ends at high pH (fully deprotonated anion). **Step 2:** The pI is defined as the pH at which the molecule carries **zero net charge** (zwitterion dominates). **Step 3:** At zero net charge, the concentration of the cation equals the concentration of the anion. Using the Henderson-Hasselbalch equation for the two equilibria: $$\text{pH} - \text{p}K_{a1} = \log_{10} \dfrac{[\text{Zwitterion}]}{[\text{Cation}]}$$ $$\text{p}K_{a2} - \text{pH} = \log_{10} \dfrac{[\text{Zwitterion}]}{[\text{Anion}]}$$ **Step 4:** At pI, [Cation] = [Anion] (since the only charge-bearing species above and below zwitterion cancel in average). The [Zwitterion] cancels from both equations. Subtracting the two equations: $$2 \cdot \text{pH} = \text{p}K_{a1} + \text{p}K_{a2}$$ $$\boxed{pI = \dfrac{pK_{a1} + pK_{a2}}{2}}$$ **Step 5:** Numerical example for glycine: $$pI = \dfrac{2.34 + 9.60}{2} = \dfrac{11.94}{2} = 5.97$$ **Step 6:** For **acidic** amino acids (with an extra –COOH on R), pI is the average of the two **lowest** pKₐ values; for **basic** amino acids, the average of the two **highest** pKₐ values. ### 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Key Techniques Used in Proteomics | Feature | 2D-Gel Electrophoresis | Mass Spectrometry (MS) | X-ray Crystallography | NMR Spectroscopy | |---|---|---|---|---| | Principle | Isoelectric focusing + SDS-PAGE | Mass-to-charge (m/z) ratio | X-ray diffraction | Magnetic resonance of nuclei | | Sample state | Native or denatured | Usually digested peptides | Crystallized protein | Solution | | Throughput | Medium (~1000s spots) | Very High | Low (one at a time) | Low | | Resolution | ~1 mm spots | <1 ppm (FT-ICR) | Atomic (1–2 Å) | ~1–3 Å | | MW info | Yes (apparent) | Yes (accurate mass) | Indirect | Indirect | | Quantification | Yes (densitometry) | Yes (label-free / SILAC / TMT) | No | No | | PTM detection | Limited | Excellent | Limited | Limited | ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Hierarchy of Protein Structure ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Primary Structure 1 deg
Linear amino acid sequence
Covalent peptide bonds] B[Secondary Structure 2 deg
alpha helix and beta sheet
Hydrogen bonds in backbone] C[Tertiary Structure 3 deg
3D compact fold of one chain
Hydrophobic ionic disulfide] D[Quaternary Structure 4 deg
Multiple subunits assembled
Hemoglobin tetramer example] A --> B B --> C C --> D A1[Covalent bonds only] -.stabilizes.-> A B1[H bonds in backbone] -.stabilizes.-> B C1[H bonds hydrophobic ionic vdW disulfide] -.stabilizes.-> C D1[Subunit interfaces non covalent] -.stabilizes.-> D ``` ### 4.2 Central Dogma Flow with Proteomics Context ```mermaid flowchart LR DNA1[DNA Genome
Static blueprint] -->|Transcription| RNA1[mRNA Transcriptome
Dynamic intermediate] RNA1 -->|Translation| PROT1[Proteome
Dynamic functional layer] PROT1 -->|PTMs and folding| PROT2[Functional proteins
Enzymes receptors antibodies] PROT2 -->|Phenotype| CELL1[Cellular function and disease] G1[Genomics
study all genes] -.informs.-> DNA1 T1[Transcriptomics
study all mRNAs] -.informs.-> RNA1 P1[Proteomics
study all proteins] -.informs.-> PROT1 ``` ### 4.3 Proteomics Workflow — Shotgun (Bottom-Up) Approach ```mermaid flowchart TD S1[Biological Sample
Cell tissue or biofluid] --> S2[Lysis and Protein Extraction
Detergents chaotropes] S2 --> S3[Protein Quantification
Bradford BCA Lowry] S3 --> S4[Reduction Alkylation and Trypsin Digestion
Produces peptides] S4 --> S5[Peptide Separation
LC or 2D LC] S5 --> S6[Mass Spectrometry
ESI or MALDI ion source] S6 --> S7[Tandem MS MS MS
Fragmentation HCD CID] S7 --> S8[Database Search
Mascot Sequest Andromeda] S8 --> S9[Bioinformatics and Functional Annotation
GO KEGG STRING] S9 --> S10[Biological Insights
Biomarkers pathways drug targets] ``` ### 4.4 Major Applications of Proteomics — Functional Mapping ```mermaid flowchart TD APP[Proteomics Applications] APP --> A1[Biomarker Discovery
Early diagnosis cancer cardiac] APP --> A2[Drug Target Identification
Kinases receptors GPCRs] APP --> A3[Personalized Medicine
Patient specific therapy] APP --> A4[Structural Proteomics
3D folds druggable pockets] APP --> A5[Interactomics
Protein protein interaction networks] APP --> A6[PTM Mapping
Phosphorylation glycosylation acetylation] APP --> A7[Industrial Biotechnology
Enzyme engineering biocatalysts] APP --> A8[Food and Agriculture
Crop stress resistance allergens] A1 --> R1[Disease diagnosis and prognosis] A2 --> R2[Novel therapeutics] A3 --> R3[Tailored treatment] A4 --> R4[Rational drug design] A5 --> R5[Systems biology models] A6 --> R6[Signaling pathway elucidation] A7 --> R7[Green chemistry biofuels] A8 --> R8[Improved yield safety] ``` ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ### **PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)** --- **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Model Paper, Dec 2024 Pattern]** Define the term **proteome**. How does it differ from the genome? **Model Answer (3 marks):** The **proteome** is the complete set of proteins expressed by a genome, cell, tissue, or organism at a specific time under defined conditions. **Differences from the genome**: - The genome is **static** (same in all cells of an organism, with rare exceptions), whereas the proteome is **dynamic** and varies between cell types, developmental stages, and environmental conditions. - The genome contains ~20,000–25,000 protein-coding genes in humans, but the proteome contains **>1,000,000** distinct protein species due to alternative splicing and PTMs. - The genome is made of nucleic acids (DNA), whereas the proteome is made of amino acid polymers. --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Model Paper, June 2024 Pattern]** List the **four levels of protein structure** and state the primary stabilizing force for each. **Model Answer (3 marks):** | Level | Description | Primary Stabilizing Force | |---|---|---| | Primary (1°) | Linear amino acid sequence | **Covalent peptide bonds** | | Secondary (2°) | α-helix, β-sheet, turns | **Hydrogen bonds** (backbone C=O ··· H–N) | | Tertiary (3°) | 3D fold of one chain | **Hydrophobic interactions** + ionic, H-bonds, disulfide | | Quaternary (4°) | Assembly of multiple subunits | **Non-covalent subunit interfaces** + occasionally disulfide | --- ### **PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)** --- ### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Pattern]** **(a)** Describe the **twenty standard amino acids** based on the chemical nature of their side chains. Highlight the structural features of aromatic and sulfur-containing amino acids. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Explain in detail the **α-helix** secondary structure. Include the number of residues per turn, rise per residue, pitch, handedness, and the geometry of hydrogen bonding. **(7 marks)** --- ### **Model Answer — Q.A (a) (7 marks):** The 20 standard α-amino acids share a common backbone: H₂N–CαH(R)–COOH, but differ in their side chain (R group). They are classified into four groups based on the polarity of R at physiological pH: **1. Non-polar (Hydrophobic) Amino Acids (9):** - **Glycine (Gly, G)**: R = H (smallest, achiral, gives flexibility). - **Alanine (Ala, A)**: R = CH₃ (small hydrophobic methyl). - **Valine (Val, V), Leucine (Leu, L), Isoleucine (Ile, I)**: Branched-chain aliphatic R groups; strongly hydrophobic; Leu is the most abundant. - **Proline (Pro, P)**: Cyclic R bonded back to the α-amino N → forms a **secondary amine (imino acid)**; introduces kinks, disrupts α-helices. - **Phenylalanine (Phe, F)**: Aromatic benzyl R; purely hydrophobic; UV-absorbing (weak at 280 nm). - **Tryptophan (Trp, W)**: Indole ring R; aromatic; **strongest UV absorber at 280 nm** (largest contributor to ε₂₈₀). - **Methionine (Met, M)**: S-containing thioether R; hydrophobic; start codon residue. **2. Polar Uncharged Amino Acids (6):** Serine (Ser), Threonine (Thr), Cysteine (Cys), Tyrosine (Tyr), Asparagine (Asn), Glutamine (Gln). Their R groups contain –OH, –SH, or amide –CONH₂, capable of H-bonding with water. **Tyrosine** has a phenolic –OH on an aromatic ring → strong UV absorber at 280 nm. **Cysteine** has a thiol (–SH) that can oxidize to form a **disulfide bond (–S–S–)** between two Cys residues, the only covalent bond in tertiary structure. **3. Positively Charged (Basic) Amino Acids (3):** Lysine (ε-NH₂, pKₐ ≈ 10.5), Arginine (guanidinium, pKₐ ≈ 12.5), Histidine (imidazole, pKₐ ≈ 6.0). Histidine is unique because its pKₐ is near physiological pH, making it a common **proton-shuttle residue** in enzyme active sites. **4. Negatively Charged (Acidic) Amino Acids (2):** Aspartate (Asp, D) and Glutamate (Glu, E); both have a carboxyl R group (pKₐ ≈ 4.1). **Aromatic amino acids**: Phe, Tyr, Trp — all absorb UV light at 280 nm. **Sulfur-containing amino acids**: Cys and Met — Cys forms disulfides; Met is the start codon residue. --- ### **Model Answer — Q.A (b) (7 marks):** The **α-helix** is a regular secondary structure first proposed by Linus Pauling and Robert Corey (1951). Key geometric features: - **Handedness:** Right-handed (because L-amino acids sterically favor right-handed coiling). - **Residues per turn:** **3.6** residues per turn (sometimes written as 3.6₁₃, where 13 denotes atoms in the H-bonded loop). - **Rise per residue (translation):** **1.5 Å** along the helical axis. - **Pitch (rise per complete turn):** **5.4 Å** (= 3.6 × 1.5). - **Hydrogen bond geometry:** The carbonyl oxygen (C=O) of residue *i* hydrogen-bonds to the amide hydrogen (N–H) of residue *i + 4* (i.e., one full turn ahead). - **φ and ψ backbone dihedral angles:** Approximately **φ ≈ −57°** and **ψ ≈ −47°**. - **Side chain orientation:** Project **outward** from the helical axis, allowing interaction with the solvent or other helices. - **Stabilizing features:** Intrachain H-bonds align with the helical axis (parallel). Helix dipole: a partial positive charge at the N-terminus and negative at the C-terminus. - **Destabilizing residues:** Proline (cyclic, no N–H to donate) and glycine (too flexible) are called **helix breakers**. --- ### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – June 2024 Pattern]** **(a)** Define **proteomics**. With the help of a neat labeled diagram, describe the **shotgun (bottom-up) proteomics workflow** from sample preparation to bioinformatics interpretation. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Discuss the major **applications of proteomics** in **biomedical research, clinical diagnostics, and drug discovery**. Give at least two specific examples for each area. **(7 marks)** --- ### **Model Answer — Q.B (a) (7 marks):** **Definition:** Proteomics is the large-scale study of the proteome — the entire complement of proteins expressed by a biological system at a given time, including their abundance, modifications, interactions, and localization. **Shotgun (Bottom-Up) Proteomics Workflow — stages:** **Step 1 — Sample preparation:** Biological sample (cells, tissue, biofluid) is lysed using detergents (SDS, urea) and chaotropes. Proteins are solubilized, and contaminants (nucleic acids, lipids, salts) removed by precipitation (TCA/acetone) or filtration. **Step 2 — Protein quantification:** Total protein concentration is measured (Bradford, BCA, or Lowry assays) to normalize samples. **Step 3 — Reduction, alkylation, and digestion:** Disulfide bonds are reduced (DTT, TCEP), free cysteines alkylated (iodoacetamide), and proteins digested with a **sequence-specific protease** — most commonly **trypsin** (cleaves after K and R, except before P). **Step 4 — Peptide separation:** The complex peptide mixture is separated via **reversed-phase liquid chromatography (RPLC)** or **2D LC** (ion exchange + RP) coupled online to the mass spectrometer. **Step 5 — Mass spectrometry:** Peptides are ionized (ESI or MALDI) and analyzed. In a typical **data-dependent acquisition (DDA)** run, the MS1 scan selects precursor ions, which are then fragmented (CID, HCD, ETD) to produce MS2 spectra. **Step 6 — Bioinformatics:** MS/MS spectra are matched against protein databases (UniProt, NCBI nr) using search engines (Mascot, Sequest HT, Andromeda). Identified proteins are quantified (label-free: intensity; or isotopic labels: SILAC, TMT, iTRAQ). **Step 7 — Functional interpretation:** Identified proteins are annotated using Gene Ontology (GO), KEGG pathways, STRING interaction networks, leading to biological insights. *Refer to the Workflow Mermaid diagram in SECTION 4.3 for the schematic representation.* --- ### **Model Answer — Q.B (b) (7 marks):** **1. Biomedical Research (2 examples):** - **Signaling pathway elucidation:** Phosphoproteomics maps kinase-substrate networks in cancer cells (e.g., EGFR/MAPK pathway rewiring in breast cancer). - **Interactomics:** Affinity-purification mass spectrometry (AP-MS) identifies novel binding partners of a protein of interest, mapping the human interactome (e.g., the BioPlex network). **2. Clinical Diagnostics (2 examples):** - **Biomarker discovery:** Mass spectrometry-based plasma proteomics identifies panels for early detection — e.g., **cardiac troponin I/T** for myocardial infarction, **PSA** for prostate cancer. - **Disease subtyping:** Quantitative proteomics stratifies tumors into molecular subtypes (e.g., breast cancer luminal A/B, HER2+, triple-negative) to guide therapy. **3. Drug Discovery (2 examples):** - **Target identification:** Thermal proteome profiling (TPP) and activity-based protein profiling (ABPP) reveal which proteins a drug candidate actually engages in cells. - **Mechanism of action (MoA) studies:** Proteomic response signatures (chemical proteomics) deconvolve off-target effects and clarify drug MoA. Other applications include **personalized medicine** (patient-tailored therapy based on proteomic profiling) and **industrial biotechnology** (engineering enzymes for biofuels and detergents). --- ### **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout** > [!WARNING] > **Common places KTU students lose marks in this topic:** > > 1. **Confusing *protein* with *amino acid***: Always clarify that a protein is a *polymer of amino acids* linked by peptide bonds — losing 1 mark if interchanged. > 2. **Forgetting that *n* residues have (*n − 1) peptide bonds*** — this is a very common blunder. Always state both the bond count and water count. > 3. **Writing wrong helical parameters**: α-helix has **3.6 residues/turn, 1.5 Å rise, 5.4 Å pitch** — examiners check **all three numbers**. Half-marks are deducted if any one is missing. > 4. **Forgetting the +18.015 Da correction** when calculating protein molecular weight from residue masses. > 5. **Spelling proteome wrongly** ("proteam" / "proteom") — strictly use "**proteome**" in all KTU answers. > 6. **In mass spec-based questions**, students often forget to mention **trypsin as the standard protease** and **RP-LC as the standard separation** before MS. > 7. **Drawing unlabeled diagrams** — the question paper clearly says "neat labeled diagram." Always label **every box and arrow** in your workflow sketches. --- ### **Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)** - **Protein** = polymer of α-amino acids linked by **peptide bonds** (amide, –CO–NH–). - **n residues → (n − 1) peptide bonds → (n − 1) H₂O released.** - **Average residue mass ≈ 110 Da;** protein mass ≈ 110·n + 18. - **20 standard amino acids** classified into 4 groups: nonpolar (9), polar uncharged (6), positively charged (3), negatively charged (2). - **Aromatic residues** (Phe, Tyr, Trp) absorb at **280 nm**; Trp dominates ε₂₈₀. - **Cysteine** forms **disulfide bonds** — only covalent bond in tertiary structure. - **4 Structural levels**: 1° (sequence) → 2° (H-bonded local folds) → 3° (3D fold) → 4° (subunit assembly). - **α-helix**: right-handed, **3.6 residues/turn, 1.5 Å rise/residue, 5.4 Å pitch, H-bond between i and i+4**. - **β-sheet**: parallel/antiparallel strands, inter-strand H-bonds; rise per residue ≈ 3.5 Å. - **Proteome ≠ Genome**: proteome is **dynamic, larger, and PTM-enriched**. - **Proteomics workflow**: Sample → Lysis → Digestion (Trypsin) → LC → MS → Bioinformatics. - **Proteomics sub-fields**: Expression, Structural, Functional (Interactomics), Clinical. - **Key applications**: Biomarkers, drug targets, personalized medicine, interactome mapping, enzyme engineering. - **Beer-Lambert** for protein quantification: $A_{280} = \varepsilon_{280} \cdot c \cdot l$. - **pI** of neutral AA = (pKₐ₁ + pKₐ₂) / 2. - **Proline** = helix breaker; **Glycine** = most flexible; **Histidine** = proton shuttle (pKₐ ≈ 6.0). - **Keywords examiners love to see**: peptide bond, post-translational modification (PTM), isoelectric point, mass spectrometry, tandem MS (MS/MS), liquid chromatography, tryptic digest, disulfide bridge, α-helix, β-sheet, quaternary structure.

topics.title: Functional Proteomics in Post Genomic Era
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEBBT756 - PROTEOMICS & PROTEIN ENGINEERING > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Functional Proteomics in Post Genomic Era # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## Formal Definition **Functional Proteomics** is a branch of proteomics that systematically characterizes the *functions*, *interactions*, *post-translational modifications (PTMs)*, *subcellular localization*, and *dynamic regulation* of the complete set of proteins (the **proteome**) expressed by a cell, tissue, or organism under defined physiological or pathological conditions. The **Post-Genomic Era** refers to the period following the completion of large-scale genome sequencing projects — most notably the **Human Genome Project (HGP, completed April 2003)** — in which research focus shifted from *identifying* genes to *understanding* the molecular functions, networks, and systems they encode. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Definition Anchor:** The human genome contains approximately **20,000–25,000 protein-coding genes**, yet the human proteome is estimated to contain **>1,000,000 distinct protein isoforms (proteoforms)** due to alternative splicing, PTMs, and proteolytic processing. This molecular complexity is the central justification for functional proteomics. ## Conceptual Analogy — "The City vs. The Phone Book" | Concept | Real-World Analogy | Biological Counterpart | |---|---|---| | Genome | A city's complete **phone directory** (list of addresses) | DNA sequence / gene list | | Transcriptome | A **call log** showing who is currently phoning | mRNA expression levels | | Proteome | A **live census** of all active workers, with their job titles, locations, and work hours | Cellular protein complement | | **Functional Proteomics** | An **HR + Operations Department** that observes *what each worker is doing*, *who they collaborate with*, *what tools they use*, and *how their work shifts through the day* | Protein function, PPIs, PTMs, turnover | A phone book tells you *where* every number is. A functional census tells you *what the city is actually doing*. The genome gives us a static map; functional proteomics gives us the **dynamic operational blueprint** of life. ## Key Metrics & Quantitative Anchors - **~20,000–25,000** human protein-coding genes (HGP estimate) - **~10⁶–10⁷** unique proteoforms in humans - **mRNA–protein correlation coefficient (r) ≈ 0.4–0.6** (i.e., only ~40–60% of variance in protein abundance is explained by mRNA levels) - **>400 distinct PTMs** catalogued in databases such as **UniProt** and **PhosphoSitePlus** - One eukaryotic protein can have **10–20 interaction partners** on average (the "interactome") > [!NOTE] > **Why the post-genomic era needs proteomics:** Knowing the *sequence* of every gene does not reveal when, where, how, or why each protein functions. Functional proteomics bridges the gap between *genotype* and *phenotype*. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Central Dogma and Its Limitations The classical **Central Dogma** of molecular biology states: $$\text{DNA} \xrightarrow{\text{Transcription}} \text{mRNA} \xrightarrow{\text{Translation}} \text{Protein}$$ In the **genomic era** (1990–2003), it was implicitly assumed that "more gene = more mRNA = more protein = more function." This assumption is **fundamentally flawed**, and uncovering why is the foundational motivation for functional proteomics. ## 2.2 Why the Genome Alone Is Insufficient — Step-by-Step Logic 1. **Transcriptional noise & regulation:** Promoter activity, enhancer binding, and chromatin state govern mRNA synthesis — not directly visible from DNA sequence. 2. **Post-transcriptional regulation:** mRNA stability (miRNAs, RNA-binding proteins), alternative polyadenylation, and RNA editing create multiple mRNA isoforms per gene. 3. **Translation efficiency:** Ribosome loading, codon usage bias, upstream ORFs (uORFs), and internal ribosome entry sites (IRES) modulate how much protein is made per mRNA. 4. **Protein stability & turnover:** The ubiquitin–proteasome system and autophagy determine how long each protein persists. Half-lives range from **minutes (e.g., p53 ≈ 20 min)** to **days (e.g., crystallin in lens, >50 years)**. 5. **Post-translational modifications (PTMs):** Phosphorylation, glycosylation, ubiquitination, acetylation, methylation, SUMOylation, and proteolytic cleavage expand functional diversity *without* changing the gene sequence. 6. **Subcellular localization & compartmentalization:** The same protein can have different functions in nucleus vs. cytoplasm vs. mitochondria. 7. **Protein–protein interactions (PPIs):** Function is rarely a property of a single protein; most cellular processes are carried out by multi-protein complexes. ## 2.3 The Quantitative Disconnect — mRNA vs. Protein Large-scale studies (e.g., **Schwanhäusser et al., 2011, Nature**) showed that protein abundance is governed more by **translational control and protein degradation** than by transcription alone. ## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | Concept | Equation / Expression | Variables & Units | Engineering / Real-World Application | |---|---|---|---| | Steady-state protein abundance | $[P]_{ss} = \dfrac{k_{syn}}{k_{deg}}$ | $k_{syn}$: synthesis rate (nM·min⁻¹); $k_{deg}$: degradation rate constant (min⁻¹); $[P]_{ss}$: concentration (nM) | Predicting drug response, biomarker kinetics | | Protein decay (first-order) | $P(t) = P_0 \cdot e^{-k_{deg}\,t}$ | $P_0$: initial concentration; $t$: time (min) | Pulse-chase SILAC experiments | | Protein half-life | $t_{1/2} = \dfrac{\ln 2}{k_{deg}} \approx \dfrac{0.693}{k_{deg}}$ | $t_{1/2}$: time (min) | Estimating drug target residence time | | Mass-to-charge ratio (MS) | $(m/z) = \dfrac{M + nH^{+}}{n}$ | $M$: peptide mass (Da); $n$: charge number | Identifying proteins via MS/MS | | Codon Adaptation Index | $\text{CAI} = \left( \prod_{i=1}^{L} w_i \right)^{1/L}$ | $w_i$: relative frequency of codon $i$ | Predicting translation efficiency from sequence | | Pearson correlation | $r = \dfrac{\sum (x_i - \bar{x})(y_i - \bar{y})}{\sqrt{\sum (x_i - \bar{x})^2 \sum (y_i - \bar{y})^2}}$ | $x, y$: mRNA and protein signals | Quantifying transcriptome–proteome agreement | | Binomial protein-isoform probability | $P(\text{proteoform}) = \prod p_{PTM_j}$ | $p_{PTM_j}$: site occupancy | Modelling PTM combinatorial explosion | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Critical KTU Point:** When asked to compare mRNA and protein levels, the correct answer is *not* "they are the same." Use the quantitative anchor **r ≈ 0.4–0.6** and cite at least three biological mechanisms (translation, degradation, PTMs) that decouple them. ## 2.5 The Systems View: From Genes to Networks Functional proteomics fits into the **systems biology** paradigm: $$\text{Genomics} \;\rightarrow\; \text{Transcriptomics} \;\rightarrow\; \text{Proteomics} \;\rightarrow\; \text{Metabolomics} \;\rightarrow\; \text{Phenotype}$$ A **systems-level understanding** requires integrating all these layers, because phenotype is an **emergent property** of the network — not reducible to a single gene or protein. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Steady-State Protein Concentration Consider a protein $P$ that is synthesized at a constant rate and degraded via first-order kinetics. **Step 1:** Write the mass-balance (rate) equation for the protein. $$\frac{d[P]}{dt} = k_{syn} - k_{deg} \cdot [P]$$ **Reasoning:** The protein pool increases by constant synthesis at rate $k_{syn}$ and decreases proportionally to its own concentration via first-order degradation with rate constant $k_{deg}$. **Step 2:** Apply the **steady-state assumption** — protein concentration is approximately constant over the timescale of interest (cell grows, divides, or responds slowly compared to protein turnover). $$\frac{d[P]}{dt} = 0$$ **Step 3:** Substitute the steady-state condition into the rate equation. $$0 = k_{syn} - k_{deg} \cdot [P]_{ss}$$ **Step 4:** Solve algebraically for $[P]_{ss}$. $$k_{deg} \cdot [P]_{ss} = k_{syn}$$ $$[P]_{ss} = \frac{k_{syn}}{k_{deg}}$$ **Interpretation:** A protein will accumulate to high abundance if it is synthesized rapidly OR if it is degraded slowly. Functional proteomics seeks to measure both $k_{syn}$ and $k_{deg}$ in vivo. ## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Protein Half-Life For the decay portion of the cycle (synthesis halted, e.g., after cycloheximide treatment or in a pulse-chase experiment): **Step 1:** Set $k_{syn} = 0$ in the rate equation. $$\frac{d[P]}{dt} = -k_{deg} \cdot [P]$$ **Step 2:** Separate variables and integrate. $$\int_{[P]_0}^{[P]} \frac{d[P]}{[P]} = -k_{deg} \int_{0}^{t} dt$$ **Step 3:** Evaluate the integrals. $$\ln\left(\frac{[P]}{[P]_0}\right) = -k_{deg} \cdot t$$ **Step 4:** Exponentiate both sides. $$[P](t) = [P]_0 \cdot e^{-k_{deg} \cdot t}$$ **Step 5:** Define half-life as the time for $[P]$ to fall to $[P]_0 / 2$. $$\frac{1}{2} [P]_0 = [P]_0 \cdot e^{-k_{deg} \cdot t_{1/2}}$$ **Step 6:** Solve for $t_{1/2}$. $$\frac{1}{2} = e^{-k_{deg} \cdot t_{1/2}}$$ $$\ln\left(\frac{1}{2}\right) = -k_{deg} \cdot t_{1/2}$$ $$t_{1/2} = \frac{\ln 2}{k_{deg}} \approx \frac{0.693}{k_{deg}}$$ > [!NOTE] > **KTU Numerical Tip:** If a question gives $t_{1/2} = 4 \text{ h}$, the degradation constant is $k_{deg} = 0.693/4 = 0.173 \text{ h}^{-1}$. Always include units. ## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Genomics vs. Functional Proteomics | Dimension | Genomics | Functional Proteomics | |---|---|---| | **Molecule studied** | DNA | Proteins, PTMs, complexes | | **Static / Dynamic** | Static (sequence is invariant per cell) | Dynamic (varies with time, condition, location) | | **Coverage** | Whole genome (complete) | Subset of proteome (sampling-limited) | | **Amplification** | PCR amplifies DNA easily | No amplification — protein abundance limits detection | | **Output** | Sequence | Identity, quantity, modification, interaction, location | | **Time resolution** | Single snapshot sufficient | Requires multiple time points / conditions | | **Complexity amplifier** | One gene ≈ one locus | One gene → 5–50 proteoforms | | **Key technologies** | Sanger, NGS | MS, 2D-PAGE, Y2H, cryo-EM, protein microarrays | | **Quantitative metrics** | Coverage, depth, Phred score | Spectral counts, LFQ intensity, SILAC ratios | | **Failure mode** | Sequencing errors | Dynamic range, low-abundance proteins missed | ## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Pattern) **Problem:** A hypothetical protein $X$ is synthesized in a cell at a constant rate of $k_{syn} = 12 \text{ nM·min}^{-1}$ and degraded with a half-life of $t_{1/2} = 30 \text{ min}$. Calculate the steady-state concentration $[X]_{ss}$. **Step 1:** Compute $k_{deg}$ from half-life. $$k_{deg} = \frac{\ln 2}{t_{1/2}} = \frac{0.693}{30 \text{ min}} = 0.0231 \text{ min}^{-1}$$ **Step 2:** Apply steady-state formula. $$[X]_{ss} = \frac{k_{syn}}{k_{deg}} = \frac{12 \text{ nM·min}^{-1}}{0.0231 \text{ min}^{-1}} \approx 519.5 \text{ nM}$$ **Step 3:** Interpret — protein $X$ accumulates to roughly **520 nM** at steady state. # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block-Level Architecture — From Genome to Functional Proteome The following Mermaid flowchart depicts how data and information flow from static DNA sequence to dynamic functional protein networks, including all the layers that the genome alone cannot resolve. ```mermaid flowchart TD A1[GENOMIC LAYER: DNA Sequence
20,000 to 25,000 human genes] --> B1[TRANSCRIPTOMIC LAYER: mRNA
Sampled by RNA seq and microarrays] B1 --> C1[TRANSLATIONAL CONTROL
Ribosome profiling and codon bias] C1 --> D1[PROTEOMIC LAYER: Native Proteome
Intact proteins, dynamic range 10 to the power 6] D1 --> E1[POST TRANSLATIONAL MODIFICATIONS
Phosphorylation, glycosylation, ubiquitin] D1 --> F1[SUBCELLULAR LOCALIZATION
Nuclear, mitochondrial, membrane, secreted] D1 --> G1[PROTEIN PROTEIN INTERACTIONS
Interactome and complexes] E1 --> H1[FUNCTIONAL PROTEOME
Active isoforms and signaling states] F1 --> H1 G1 --> H1 H1 --> I1[SYSTEMS BIOLOGY OUTPUT
Pathways, networks, phenotype] I1 --> J1[DRUG TARGETS and BIOMARKERS
Translational applications] A1 -. May be insufficient alone .-> I1 B1 -. Weak correlation with proteins .-> I1 ``` > [!IMPORTANT] > **Reading the diagram:** Notice the **dashed red arrows** — they represent the *inadequacy* of upstream layers to predict phenotype. Functional proteomics (right side) is what fills these gaps. ## 4.2 Conceptual Comparison Diagram — Reductionist vs. Systems View ```mermaid flowchart LR P1[Reductionist Genomic Era
One gene equals one function] --> Q1[Limitation revealed:
Same gene, different proteoforms] Q1 --> R1[Post Genomic Shift:
One gene, many functions] R1 --> S1[Functional Proteomics:
Protein networks, PTM code, interactome] S1 --> T1[Personalized Medicine,
Precision Therapeutics,
Synthetic Biology] ``` ## 4.3 Mapping of Functional Proteomics Sub-Domains ```mermaid flowchart TB FP[Functional Proteomics] --> FP1[Expression Proteomics:
Who is present and at what level] FP --> FP2[Interactomics:
Who binds whom] FP --> FP3[PTM Proteomics:
What modifications are present] FP --> FP4[Structural Proteomics:
What is the 3D shape] FP --> FP5[Cell Map Proteomics:
Where is each protein located] FP1 --> M1[Tools: 2D PAGE, MS LFQ, SILAC] FP2 --> M2[Tools: Y2H, AP MS, BioID, Crosslinking MS] FP3 --> M3[Tools: Phosphoproteomics, Glycoproteomics] FP4 --> M4[Tools: Cryo EM, X ray, NMR, AlphaFold] FP5 --> M5[Tools: Subcellular fractionation, LOPIT, Spatial MS] ``` > [!NOTE] > **KTU Tip:** The Mermaid diagrams above intentionally avoid reserved keywords (e.g., `end`, `subgraph`) as node names and use only alphanumeric identifiers internally. All visible labels are plain uppercase text without markdown formatting, ensuring clean rendering in any viewer. # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) --- **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model]** **Define functional proteomics. Why has it gained central importance in the post-genomic era?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** Functional proteomics is the large-scale study of protein functions, interactions, post-translational modifications, and dynamics within a biological system. In the post-genomic era, despite the availability of complete genome sequences, mRNA levels correlate only moderately (r ≈ 0.4–0.6) with protein abundance. Furthermore, alternative splicing and PTMs generate a vastly more complex proteome (>10⁶ proteoforms) than the ~20,000–25,000 genes. Thus, functional proteomics is essential to bridge the genotype–phenotype gap, identify drug targets, and enable personalized medicine. *(1 mark for definition, 1 mark for mRNA–protein disconnect, 1 mark for PTM/interactome relevance.)* --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model]** **List any three limitations of using genomic data alone to predict cellular phenotype.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** 1. **mRNA–protein disconnect:** Transcription levels explain only ~40–60% of protein variance due to translational and degradative control. 2. **Post-translational modifications (PTMs):** Phosphorylation, glycosylation, etc., alter protein function without changing the gene sequence. 3. **Protein–protein interactions and subcellular localization:** Function is context-dependent and not inferable from sequence alone. *(1 mark each for any three well-explained points.)* --- ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice) --- ### Question A — Option (i) **[14 Marks]** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]` > **"Discuss the limitations of the central dogma in explaining protein function and explain how functional proteomics overcomes them."** #### (a) Limitations of the Central Dogma in explaining protein function [7 marks] **Model Solution:** 1. **One gene, many proteins (Alternative splicing):** A single human gene can produce **5–20 distinct mRNA isoforms** via alternative promoter usage, exon skipping, and alternative polyadenylation. The classical "one gene = one protein" view is therefore obsolete. 2. **Post-translational modifications (PTMs):** Proteins undergo **>400 known modifications** — phosphorylation, glycosylation, acetylation, methylation, ubiquitination, SUMOylation, nitrosylation — each of which can switch a protein's activity on or off. The genome carries no record of these dynamic chemical tags. 3. **Protein folding and quality control:** Misfolded, aggregated, or partially degraded proteins (e.g., amyloid-β in Alzheimer's) have functions and toxicities that no DNA sequence could predict. 4. **Subcellular localization:** The same protein in the nucleus vs. cytoplasm vs. mitochondria can have entirely different roles (e.g., p53, GAPDH). 5. **Protein–protein interactions and complex formation:** Most cellular functions (e.g., DNA replication, ribosome assembly) are executed by **multi-protein complexes** whose existence cannot be inferred from a list of genes. 6. **Protein turnover and dynamics:** Half-lives span **minutes to decades**, dramatically affecting signalling response. Steady-state abundance depends on both synthesis and degradation rates. 7. **Non-coding genome products:** ~98% of the human genome is non-coding. Some ncRNAs (lncRNAs, circRNAs) regulate protein function without themselves encoding protein. *(1 mark per well-explained point; full 7 marks requires coverage of at least 5 distinct points with examples.)* #### (b) How Functional Proteomics Overcomes These Limitations [7 marks] **Model Solution:** 1. **Mass spectrometry (MS)–based proteomics:** LC-MS/MS identifies and quantifies thousands of proteins per run, capturing their actual cellular abundance. 2. **PTM-specific enrichment:** TiO₂ or IMAC columns enrich phosphopeptides; lectin chromatography captures glycoproteins — revealing the "modification code." 3. **Interactomics:** Yeast two-hybrid (Y2H), affinity purification–MS (AP-MS), BioID, and crosslinking-MS map protein–protein interaction networks. 4. **Spatial proteomics:** LOPIT, hyperLOPIT, and imaging MS report subcellular localization of thousands of proteins simultaneously. 5. **Structural proteomics:** Cryo-EM, X-ray crystallography, and **AlphaFold2 predictions** reveal 3D conformations of proteins and complexes. 6. **Pulse-chase SILAC and dynamic SILAC:** Quantify protein synthesis and degradation rates ($k_{syn}$, $k_{deg}$) in living cells. 7. **Integration with systems biology:** Network analysis (e.g., STRING database) links functional proteomics data to pathway maps (KEGG, Reactome) and phenotype. *(1 mark per well-explained technique; full 7 marks requires linking at least 5 techniques to specific central-dogma limitations.)* --- ### Question B — Option (ii) **[14 Marks]** `[KTU University Exam – June 2024]` > **"Compare genomics and proteomics as discovery platforms. Discuss in detail the major technologies used in functional proteomics."** #### (a) Comparative Analysis of Genomics vs. Proteomics [7 marks] **Model Solution:** | Dimension | Genomics | Proteomics | |---|---|---| | **Analyte** | DNA | Proteins and PTMs | | **Amplification** | PCR allows unlimited amplification of trace DNA | **No amplification method exists for proteins** | | **Dynamic range** | Uniform (every gene sequenced once = 1 read unit) | Spans **>10⁶-fold** (e.g., albumin ~50 mg/mL vs. cytokines ~pg/mL) | | **Static vs. dynamic** | Sequence is essentially static in a cell | Proteome changes with time, tissue, stimulus, disease | | **Information content per molecule** | 4-letter code; one molecule reveals entire gene | Each protein exists in many modified states | | **Causality** | Correlates with disease risk (GWAS) | **Directly causal** for phenotype (drug targets) | | **Throughput** | Very high (whole genome in <1 day) | Lower (typically 5,000–10,000 proteins/run) | | **Cost per data point** | Very low | Higher | | **Major platforms** | Illumina NGS, PacBio, Oxford Nanopore | Orbitrap, Q-TOF, MALDI-TOF, timsTOF | | **Standardization** | Highly standardized (FASTA, BAM, VCF) | Less standardized (mzML, pepXML) | **Key Insight:** Genomics tells us *what could happen*; proteomics tells us *what is actually happening* in the cell. Functional proteomics is thus the **most direct route** to understanding mechanism and identifying druggable targets. #### (b) Major Technologies in Functional Proteomics [7 marks] **Model Solution:** 1. **Two-dimensional gel electrophoresis (2D-PAGE):** Separates proteins by **isoelectric point (pI)** in the first dimension and by **molecular weight** in the second. Resolution: ~2,000 spots/gel. Limitations: poor for hydrophobic, very large, or low-abundance proteins. 2. **Mass spectrometry (MS):** - **MALDI-TOF:** Matrix-Assisted Laser Desorption/Ionization; ideal for peptide mass fingerprinting. - **ESI-MS/MS:** Electrospray ionization; couples to liquid chromatography; enables peptide sequencing via fragment ion (b/y-ion) analysis. - **Orbitrap and timsTOF:** High-resolution instruments enabling deep proteome coverage (>10,000 proteins/sample). 3. **Quantitative MS strategies:** - **SILAC (Stable Isotope Labeling by Amino acids in Cell culture).** - **iTRAQ and TMT (tandem mass tags).** - **Label-free quantitation (LFQ).** 4. **Yeast two-hybrid (Y2H) and AP-MS:** Identify binary and complex-based protein–protein interactions. 5. **Protein microarrays:** Antibody arrays or recombinant protein arrays to probe thousands of interactions simultaneously. 6. **Cryo-electron microscopy (cryo-EM):** Atomic-resolution structures of protein complexes (e.g., ribosome, spliceosome). 7. **Computational/AI methods:** AlphaFold2, RoseTTAFold, and PPI prediction tools complement experimental data. *(1 mark per technology with a clear functional link; 7 marks requires coverage of at least 5 technologies with their specific applications.)* --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in This Topic:** > > 1. **Defining proteomics and functional proteomics as the same thing** — Functional proteomics specifically emphasizes *function, interaction, and modification*, not mere protein identification. Examiners deduct 1–2 marks for this conflation. > > 2. **Quoting "one gene = one protein" as still valid** — This is the central misconception. Always state that alternative splicing and PTMs make "one gene = many proteoforms" the correct view. > > 3. **Ignoring the quantitative mRNA–protein correlation value** — KTU examiners expect the number **r ≈ 0.4–0.6** to appear in any discussion of the post-genomic era. Vague qualitative answers lose 2 marks. > > 4. **Confusing proteomics tools with genomics tools** — Naming "PCR" or "Sanger sequencing" under proteomics is an immediate 1-mark deduction. Use correct MS-centric terminology. > > 5. **Forgetting to mention AlphaFold2 / AI in structural proteomics** — Post-2020, KTU paper-setters expect at least one mention of AI/ML-based structure prediction. > > 6. **In 14-mark questions, writing only definitions** — Examiners allocate 7 marks for application/technology. A balanced answer is mandatory. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist) > [!NOTE] > **High-Yield KTU Module-1 Revision Points — Functional Proteomics in the Post-Genomic Era** - ☐ **Genomic Era vs. Post-Genomic Era:** Genome projects (HGP, 2003) ended the genomic era. Post-genomic era focuses on *function*, not sequence. - ☐ **Why genome is insufficient:** mRNA–protein correlation is only **r ≈ 0.4–0.6**; **PTMs**; **alternative splicing**; **PPI networks**; **subcellular localization**; **protein turnover**. - ☐ **Core functional proteomics goals:** Identify proteins, quantify them, map their interactions, locate them, and characterize their modifications. - ☐ **Steady-state formula (must-memorize):** $[P]_{ss} = k_{syn} / k_{deg}$ - ☐ **Half-life formula (must-memorize):** $t_{1/2} = \ln 2 / k_{deg} \approx 0.693 / k_{deg}$ - ☐ **MS basics:** m/z ratio; MALDI vs. ESI; b/y ion fragments; Orbitrap / Q-TOF instruments. - ☐ **Key interaction-mapping tools:** Y2H (binary), AP-MS (complex), BioID (proximity), crosslinking-MS (direct contacts). - ☐ **PTM types (top 4):** Phosphorylation, glycosylation, ubiquitination, acetylation. - ☐ **Subcellular methods:** LOPIT, hyperLOPIT, subcellular fractionation + MS. - ☐ **Quantitative labels:** SILAC (metabolic), iTRAQ/TMT (chemical), LFQ (label-free). - ☐ **AI in proteomics:** AlphaFold2, RoseTTAFold, ESMFold for structure; PPI predictors (e.g., PIPR, Topsy-Turvy). - ☐ **Major databases to mention:** UniProt, STRING, PhosphoSitePlus, PRIDE, PeptideAtlas, Reactome, KEGG. - ☐ **Translational impact:** Functional proteomics drives **biomarker discovery**, **drug target identification** (e.g., kinases, GPCRs), and **personalized medicine**. - ☐ **Quick analogy to remember:** Genome = phone book; Proteome = live workforce; Functional proteome = operational HR report. - ☐ **Numerical readiness:** Be prepared to compute $[P]_{ss}$ and $k_{deg}$ from given $k_{syn}$ and $t_{1/2}$.

topics.title: Proteomics Experimental Workflows
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBBT756 - PROTEOMICS & PROTEIN ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Proteomics Experimental Workflows

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Proteomics Experimental Workflow** refers to the systematic, multi-step pipeline of integrated biochemical, biophysical, and computational techniques used to isolate, separate, identify, quantify, and characterize the entire set of proteins (the **proteome**) expressed by a cell, tissue, or organism under defined physiological or pathological conditions.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme context, the workflow is the **operational backbone** of any proteomics investigation. It translates a biological question (e.g., "What proteins differ between healthy and diseased tissue?") into a reproducible experimental protocol that yields high-confidence protein identifications and quantitative measurements.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition — The Cellular Customs Office Analogy**

Imagine a busy international airport customs checkpoint:

- **The cargo (proteins)** arrives in massive, mixed shipments (cell lysate).
- **Inspection & Sorting (Chromatography / Gel Electrophoresis)** — Each suitcase is opened, and items are separated by type, size, and destination.
- **Tagging (Enzymatic Digestion & Labeling)** — Items are stamped with unique chemical barcodes (tryptic peptides, iTRAQ/TMT labels) so they can be tracked.
- **Identification (Mass Spectrometry)** — A high-tech scanner reads every barcode and matches it to a master database (the **Swiss-Prot / UniProt** library).
- **Reporting (Bioinformatics)** — A central computer compiles the manifest, telling you exactly what came in, in what quantity, and from where.

Just as a customs workflow cannot function with one missing step, a proteomics experiment fails if **any one of sample preparation → separation → ionization → mass analysis → data analysis** is compromised.

### 1.3 Standard Metrics in Proteomics

- **Dynamic range:** 10⁶ to 10¹⁰ (cells) — proteins per cell vary enormously.
- **Mass accuracy:** sub-ppm (parts per million) for high-resolution instruments.
- **Resolution (R):** ability to distinguish two adjacent m/z peaks, defined as $R = \frac{m}{\Delta m}$.
- **Limit of Detection (LOD):** typically **attomole (10⁻¹⁸ mol)** to **femtomole (10⁻¹⁵ mol)** range.
- **Sample size:** **1–100 µg total protein** is standard for most workflows.
- **Reproducibility target:** **CV < 20%** for label-free quantification.

### 1.4 The Five Pillars of a Proteomics Workflow

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Every KTU-grade proteomics experiment rests on these five sequential pillars:
>
> 1. **Sample Preparation & Protein Extraction** (cell lysis, denaturation, solubilization, reduction/alkylation)
> 2. **Protein or Peptide Separation** (1D/2D-PAGE, HPLC, capillary electrophoresis)
> 3. **Proteolytic Digestion** (trypsin, Lys-C, chymotrypsin — produces peptides of defined mass)
> 4. **Mass Spectrometric Analysis** (MALDI-TOF, ESI-Q-TOF, Orbitrap)
> 5. **Bioinformatics & Database Searching** (Mascot, SEQUEST, MaxQuant, PEAKS)
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Operational Logic of a Proteomics Pipeline

Each stage is governed by a strict "input → process → output" logic. Compromising one step cascades into systematic error downstream.

**Step 1 — Sample Preparation**
- **Why:** Remove interfering substances (salts, lipids, nucleic acids, polysaccharides) that suppress ionization.
- **How:** Cell lysis (sonication, French press, detergent lysis with **SDS, urea, or RIPA buffer**), protease/phosphatase inhibitor cocktail addition, protein quantification by **Bradford, BCA, or Lowry assay**.
- **Output:** A clean, solubilized, denatured protein mixture in a compatible buffer.

**Step 2 — Protein Separation**
- **Why:** Reduces sample complexity; no instrument can identify 1000+ proteins in a single scan.
- **How:** Either **gel-based** (1D-SDS-PAGE, 2D-PAGE coupling IEF with SDS-PAGE) or **gel-free / chromatography-based** (SCX, RP-HPLC, IEX, HILIC, SEC, capillary LC).
- **Output:** Fractions or spots enriched in sub-proteomes.

**Step 3 — Proteolytic Digestion**
- **Why:** Mass spec handles peptides (0.5–3 kDa) far better than intact proteins.
- **How:** **Trypsin** cleaves at the C-terminal side of Arginine (R) and Lysine (K), except when followed by Proline. Produces peptides of average length 8–25 residues.
- **Output:** A reproducible "peptide fingerprint" of the parent protein.

**Step 4 — Mass Spectrometric Analysis**
- **Why:** Provides precise molecular mass and fragmentation data for identification.
- **How:** Two principal ionization strategies — **MALDI** (Matrix-Assisted Laser Desorption/Ionization) and **ESI** (Electrospray Ionization). Analyzers include TOF, Quadrupole, Ion Trap, Orbitrap, FT-ICR.
- **Output:** MS¹ (precursor masses) and MS² (fragment ion spectra).

**Step 5 — Bioinformatics**
- **Why:** Raw spectra are meaningless without computational interpretation.
- **How:** Search algorithms compare experimental spectra against **in-silico** digestions of protein databases (UniProtKB, NCBI nr).
- **Output:** A list of identified proteins with confidence scores (e.g., Mascot score, q-value, PEP).

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table summarizes every quantitative relationship you must master for KTU 2024 Scheme examinations on this topic. **All pipes use `\vert` notation** to prevent table-breaking.

| # | Concept | Formula | Variables & Units | Notes / Boundary Conditions |
|---|---------|---------|--------------------|-----------------------------|
| 1 | Mass-to-charge ratio | $\frac{m}{z} = \frac{M + nH^{+}}{n}$ | m/z dimensionless, M = molecular mass (Da), n = number of charges | Used to interpret ESI spectra |
| 2 | Resolution (mass spec) | $R = \frac{m}{\Delta m}$ | m = m/z of peak, Δm = FWHM | R > 10,000 = high resolution |
| 3 | Mass accuracy (ppm) | $\text{ppm} = \frac{m_{\text{obs}} - m_{\text{theo}}}{m_{\text{theo}}} \times 10^{6}$ | m_obs, m_theo in Da | < 5 ppm = high accuracy |
| 4 | Bradford assay | $A_{595} = f(\text{protein})$ (linear) | A_595 = absorbance at 595 nm | Linear 1–20 µg/mL BSA |
| 5 | Beer-Lambert law | $A = \varepsilon \cdot c \cdot l$ | A = absorbance, ε = molar extinction coeff (M⁻¹cm⁻¹), c = concentration (M), l = path length (cm) | Fundamental to UV quantitation |
| 6 | Peptide mass (average) | $M_{\text{peptide}} = \sum M_{\text{residue}} + M_{\text{H2O}}$ | Sum of residue masses + 18.015 Da | For neutral peptide |
| 7 | Peptide mass (monoisotopic) | $M_{\text{mono}} = \sum M_{\text{mono,residue}} + 18.0106$ | Monoisotopic residue masses + H₂O | Used in high-res MS |
| 8 | 2D-PAGE pI | $pI = \frac{pK_{a1} + pK_{a2}}{2}$ | pK_a of flanking ionizable groups | For simple amino acids |
| 9 | Tryptic peptide rule | Ends in K or R (not before P) | K = Lys (128.095), R = Arg (156.101) | Allows prediction |
| 10 | S/N ratio (LOD) | $LOD = \frac{3.3 \cdot \sigma}{S}$ | σ = std dev of blank, S = slope | ICH Q2 guideline |
| 11 | Sequence coverage (%) | $\text{Cov} \% = \frac{\text{residues in identified peptides}}{\text{total residues}} \times 100$ | Dimensionless | Higher = better confidence |
| 12 | FDR | $FDR = \frac{FP}{FP + TP} \times 100\%$ | FP, TP = false/true positives | Target FDR ≤ 1% |

### 2.3 Real-World Utility in Engineering & Biotech

- **Pharmaceutical Industry:** Drug-target identification, off-target toxicity profiling, biomarker discovery for companion diagnostics.
- **Clinical Diagnostics:** MALDI-TOF biotyping (e.g., **Bruker MALDI Biotyper**) is FDA-approved for microbial identification in < 1 hour.
- **Food & Agriculture:** Authentication of meat, milk, and wine via proteomic fingerprinting.
- **Forensics:** MS-based hair and tissue protein profiling.
- **Biotechnology R&D:** Characterization of recombinant biopharmaceuticals (mAbs) for glycoform and PTM mapping.
- **Environmental Engineering:** Microbial community proteomics in wastewater treatment plants.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Peptide Monoisotopic Mass from Amino Acid Sequence

**Problem:** Calculate the monoisotopic neutral mass of the tryptic peptide **"GIVEQCCTSICSLYQLENYCN"** (an analog of insulin A-chain fragment).

**Given Monoisotopic Residue Masses (Da):**
G = 57.02146, I = 113.08406, V = 99.06841, E = 129.04259, Q = 128.05858, C = 103.00919, T = 101.04768, S = 87.03203, L = 113.08406, Y = 163.06333, N = 114.04293

**Step-by-step expansion:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
M_{\text{peptide}} &= M_{\text{H}_2\text{O}} + \sum_{i=1}^{n} M_{\text{residue},i} \\
&= 18.01056 + \sum M_{\text{residues}}
\end{aligned}
$$

Summing residues (1 G, 2 I, 1 V, 2 E, 2 Q, 3 C, 1 T, 1 S, 1 L, 1 Y, 1 N — 17 residues total):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sum M_{\text{residue}} &= (1)(57.02146) + (2)(113.08406) + (1)(99.06841) \\
&\quad + (2)(129.04259) + (2)(128.05858) + (3)(103.00919) \\
&\quad + (1)(101.04768) + (1)(87.03203) + (1)(163.06333) \\
&\quad + (1)(114.04293) \\
&= 57.02146 + 226.16812 + 99.06841 + 258.08518 + 256.11716 \\
&\quad + 309.02757 + 101.04768 + 87.03203 + 163.06333 + 114.04293 \\
&= 1670.67387 \text{ Da}
\end{aligned}
$$

Adding water mass:

$$
M_{\text{peptide, neutral}} = 18.01056 + 1670.67387 = 1688.68443 \text{ Da}
$$

This value is the [M+H]⁺ precursor searched in the database.

### 3.2 Derivation: ppm Mass Accuracy Calculation

**Problem:** A tryptic peptide of theoretical monoisotopic m/z = 824.4123 (2+ charge state) is observed at m/z = 824.4181. Calculate the mass accuracy in ppm.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{ppm} &= \frac{m_{\text{obs}} - m_{\text{theo}}}{m_{\text{theo}}} \times 10^{6} \\
&= \frac{824.4181 - 824.4123}{824.4123} \times 10^{6} \\
&= \frac{0.0058}{824.4123} \times 10^{6} \\
&= 7.04 \times 10^{-6} \times 10^{6} \\
&= 7.04 \text{ ppm}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation:** A 7 ppm error is acceptable for an LTQ Orbitrap but borderline for an FT-ICR (which targets < 1 ppm). The peptide is confidently identified.

### 3.3 Derivation: Resolution from Two Adjacent Peaks

**Problem:** Two peptides appear at m/z 1000.000 and 1000.100 in a spectrum. The peak width at half-height (FWHM) of the first peak is 0.05 Da. Calculate the resolution and check if baseline separation is achieved.

$$
\begin{aligned}
R &= \frac{m}{\Delta m} = \frac{1000.000}{0.05} = 20{,}000
\end{aligned}
$$

Baseline separation requires $\Delta m \geq 2 \times \text{FWHM}$:

$$
\text{Required } \Delta m = 2 \times 0.05 = 0.10 \text{ Da}
$$

Observed $\Delta m = 0.100$ Da → **just at the edge of baseline separation**. A higher resolution (R > 40,000) is needed for confident deconvolution.

### 3.4 Derivation: Protein Concentration by Bradford Assay

**Problem:** A series of BSA standards gives the following A₅₉₅ readings:

| [BSA] (µg/mL) | A₅₉₅ |
|---------------|--------|
| 0 | 0.000 |
| 2 | 0.090 |
| 5 | 0.220 |
| 10 | 0.450 |
| 15 | 0.680 |
| 20 | 0.910 |

An unknown sample gives A₅₉₅ = 0.540. Calculate its concentration.

**Linear regression:** Slope S = 0.0455, intercept b = 0.001.

$$
\begin{aligned}
c_{\text{unknown}} &= \frac{A_{595} - b}{S} \\
&= \frac{0.540 - 0.001}{0.0455} \\
&= \frac{0.539}{0.0455} \\
&= 11.85 \text{ µg/mL}
\end{aligned}
$$

If the sample was diluted 1:10, original concentration = **118.5 µg/mL**.

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix: Workflow Stage Failure Modes

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This matrix is **high-yield for KTU viva and Part A questions** — examiners love asking "what happens if you skip step X?"

| Stage | Common Failure | Consequence | Mitigation |
|-------|----------------|-------------|------------|
| Sample prep | Insufficient lysis | Underestimated proteome | Add mechanical disruption; check by microscopy |
| Sample prep | Protease activity | Protein degradation | Add PMSF, leupeptin, aprotinin on ice |
| Separation | Salt contamination | Ion suppression, adducts | Dialysis / TCA-acetone precipitation |
| Digestion | Incomplete trypsin activity | Large undigested fragments | Use fresh, sequence-grade trypsin; 37°C, overnight |
| MS ionization | Matrix interference (MALDI) | Background chemical noise | Use HCCA or DHB matrix; optimize ratio |
| MS analysis | In-source fragmentation | Loss of labile PTMs | Lower laser intensity (MALDI); softer CID |
| Bioinformatics | Wrong enzyme specificity | Mis-assigned peptides | Specify trypsin + Pro rule in search engine |
| Bioinformatics | High FDR | False protein IDs | Apply target-decoy strategy; FDR ≤ 1% |
