# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!NOTE]
> **Mark Distribution Pattern (KTU 2024 Scheme, PEBBT756):**
> - Part A: 2 questions × 3 marks = 6 marks
> - Part B: Internal choice, 1 question × 14 marks (split into a & b of 7 marks each)
> - Module weightage for Module 2 in ESE ≈ 15–20%

---

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Model Question, May 2024]**
**Define Circular Dichroism (CD). Why is it considered a chiroptical technique, and which protein structural feature does far-UV CD primarily probe?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Definition (1 mark):** Circular Dichroism is the differential absorption of left- and right-circularly polarized light by a chiral molecule, quantified as $\Delta\varepsilon = \varepsilon_L - \varepsilon_R$ and reported as ellipticity θ in millidegrees.
- **Chiroptical nature (1 mark):** It is called "chiroptical" because the optical measurement (absorption) is sensitive to molecular chirality; the chromophore must be asymmetric (no $S_n$ axis) for a non-zero signal.
- **Far-UV probe (1 mark):** Far-UV CD (190–250 nm) probes the electronic transitions of the **peptide bond (amide group)**, and the spectrum reflects the **secondary structure** (α-helix, β-sheet, turns, random coil) of the protein.

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Model Question, Dec 2023]**
**A protein solution of 0.2 mg/mL in a 1 mm path-length cell gives a CD signal of −18.5 mdeg at 222 nm. The protein has 150 amino acids and $M = 16{,}500$ Da. Calculate the mean residue ellipticity $[\theta]_{222}$.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Convert to standard units: $\theta = -18.5\,\text{mdeg} = -0.0185\,\text{deg}$ (½ mark), $c = 0.2\,\text{mg/mL}$ (½ mark), $\ell = 0.1\,\text{cm}$, $n = 150$, $M = 16{,}500$.
Apply Formula 4:

$$[\theta]_{222} = \frac{(-0.0185)\times 16{,}500}{10 \times 0.2 \times 0.1 \times 150}$$

Denominator $= 30$. Numerator $= -305.25$. 

$$[\theta]_{222} = \frac{-305.25}{30} = -10{,}175\,\mathrm{deg\cdot cm^2\cdot dmol^{-1}}$$

**Answer:** $[\theta]_{222} \approx -1.02 \times 10^{4}\,\mathrm{deg\cdot cm^2\cdot dmol^{-1}}$ (1 mark for the final number with units).

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

### **Question A — 14 Marks**

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023-style]**

**(a)** Explain the physical principle of Circular Dichroism. With a neat block diagram, describe the components and working of a CD spectrophotometer. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the characteristic far-UV CD signatures of α-helix, β-sheet, β-turn, and random coil. How is the α-helical content quantitatively estimated from a CD spectrum? **(7 marks)**

#### **Model Solution to A(a):**

**Principle (3 marks):** CD measures $\Delta A = A_L - A_R$ as a function of wavelength. Linearly polarized light can be decomposed into L-CPL and R-CPL of equal amplitude. When this passes through a chiral sample, the two components are absorbed to different extents. The recombined beam is therefore **elliptically polarized**; the major axis tilt = ellipticity θ, related to ΔA by $\theta = 32.982 \cdot \Delta A$ degrees. The phenomenon arises because electronic transitions in chiral molecules have non-zero **rotational strength** $R = \mathrm{Im}(\boldsymbol{\mu}\cdot\boldsymbol{m})$.

**Block diagram (2 marks):** See Section 4.1 above. Identify and explain the function of: light source → monochromator → PEM (50 kHz modulator) → sample → PMT detector → lock-in amplifier → computer → CD spectrum.

**Working (2 marks):**
- A xenon lamp provides continuous UV light.
- The monochromator selects a narrow bandwidth at wavelength λ.
- The PEM, driven at 50 kHz, alternates the polarization of the beam between L-CPL and R-CPL faster than the detector response time.
- The PMT detects the AC signal, whose amplitude is proportional to ΔA.
- The lock-in amplifier demodulates the 50 kHz component.
- The computer plots θ vs. λ.

#### **Model Solution to A(b):**

**Characteristic signatures (4 marks):**

| Structure | Far-UV Features | Distinguishing Aspect |
|---|---|---|
| **α-Helix** | Negative at 222 nm, negative at 208 nm, strong positive at 190 nm | Two negative minima, one large positive peak |
| **β-Sheet (anti-parallel)** | Negative at ~215 nm, positive at ~195 nm | Broad, single minimum |
| **β-Turn** | Negative at ~225 nm, positive at ~205 nm | Weak amplitude |
| **Random coil** | Strong negative near 198 nm, near-zero above 210 nm | Single deep minimum |

**Estimation of α-helical content (3 marks):**
- **Method 1 (Single-wavelength, Greenfield–Fasman):** Use the equation derived in Section 3.4:

$$\%\,\alpha\text{-helix} = \frac{[\theta]_{222,\text{obs}} - [\theta]_{222,\text{coil}}}{[\theta]_{222,\text{helix}} - [\theta]_{222,\text{coil}}} \times 100$$

with $[\theta]_{222,\text{helix}} = -39{,}500$ and $[\theta]_{222,\text{coil}} = -3{,}900$.

- **Method 2 (Full-spectrum deconvolution):** Fit the entire spectrum (190–250 nm) against a reference database using algorithms such as **CONTIN, SELCON3, CDSSTR, or K2D3**, yielding simultaneous estimates of helix, sheet, turn, and coil.
- **Method 3 (Neural-network-based):** Use tools like **K2D3** or **BeStSel** for improved accuracy, especially for β-sheet-rich proteins.

### **Question B — 14 Marks (ALTERNATIVE)**

**[KTU University Exam – May 2024-style]**

**(a)** Define mean residue ellipticity and derive the relationship $[\theta] = 3298.2 \cdot \Delta\varepsilon$. How is raw ellipticity (mdeg) converted to mean residue ellipticity? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain how CD spectroscopy is used to monitor **thermal denaturation** of a protein. Derive the equations for $T_m$, $\Delta H$, and $\Delta G$ from a CD-monitored melt curve. **(7 marks)**

#### **Model Solution to B(a):**

**Definition (1 mark):** Mean residue ellipticity $[\theta]$ is the ellipticity normalized per amino acid residue, with units $\mathrm{deg\cdot cm^2\cdot dmol^{-1}}$, allowing direct comparison between proteins of different sizes.

**Derivation of conversion (3 marks):**
Starting from Beer–Lambert applied to a per-residue basis:

$$\Delta A_{mrw} = \Delta\varepsilon_{mrw} \cdot c_{mrw} \cdot \ell$$

The ellipticity in degrees is $\theta = 32.982 \cdot \Delta A$ and the per-residue quantity uses $c_{mrw} = c/n$ and the $\mathrm{dmol}$ convention (1 dmol = 0.1 mol). Combining:

$$[\theta] = 32.982 \cdot \Delta\varepsilon_{mrw} \cdot (c_{mrw} \cdot \ell) \cdot 1000 / (c_{mrw} \cdot \ell)$$

The numerical evaluation $32.982 \times 100 = 3298.2$ (using dmol scaling) gives:

$$\boxed{[\theta] = 3298.2 \cdot \Delta\varepsilon}$$

**Conversion from raw mdeg (3 marks):** Use Formula 4:

$$[\theta]_\lambda = \frac{\theta_{\text{deg}} \times M}{10 \times c \times \ell \times n}$$

where $\theta_{\text{deg}} = \theta_{\text{mdeg}}/1000$, $M$ in g/mol, $c$ in g/mL, $\ell$ in cm, $n$ = number of residues. Alternatively, $[\theta] = (\theta_{\text{mdeg}} \times M) / (10{,}000 \times c_{\text{g/mL}} \times \ell_{\text{cm}} \times n)$.

#### **Model Solution to B(b):**

**Experimental setup (2 marks):**
- Place the protein in a temperature-controlled, sealed cuvette.
- Monitor $[\theta]_{222}$ (or 215 nm) as temperature is increased (typical ramp 1 °C/min).
- The α-helix signal is lost upon unfolding, so $[\theta]_{222}$ becomes less negative with increasing T, eventually reaching the random-coil baseline.

**Derivation of thermodynamic parameters (5 marks):**

For a two-state transition $N \rightleftharpoons D$, the observed signal at temperature T is:

$$\theta_{\text{obs}}(T) = f_N \theta_N + f_D \theta_D, \quad f_N + f_D = 1$$

Therefore the fraction unfolded:

$$f_D(T) = \frac{\theta_{\text{obs}}(T) - \theta_N}{\theta_D - \theta_N}$$

The equilibrium constant is $K = f_D / (1 - f_D) = f_D/f_N$.

**$T_m$:** Defined as the temperature where $K = 1$, i.e., $f_D = f_N = 0.5$, i.e., $\theta_{\text{obs}} = (\theta_N + \theta_D)/2$.

**$\Delta H$:** From the van't Hoff equation:

$$\ln K = -\frac{\Delta H}{R}\left(\frac{1}{T} - \frac{1}{T_m}\right)$$

A linear plot of $\ln K$ vs. $1/T$ has slope $-\Delta H/R$, hence $\Delta H$ is obtained directly.

**$\Delta G$:** At any temperature,

$$\Delta G = -RT \ln K = \Delta H - T\Delta S$$

with $\Delta S = \Delta H / T_m$. Therefore:

$$\Delta G(T) = \Delta H\left(1 - \frac{T}{T_m}\right)$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Common mistakes students make in CD questions:**
> 1. **Unit confusion in $[\theta]$:** Forgetting to convert **mdeg → deg** before applying Formula 4; the 10⁴ factor is often missed.
> 2. **Mixing $\Delta\varepsilon$ and $[\theta]$:** These are different physical quantities. The factor is 3298.2, not 32.982.
> 3. **Wrong reference values:** Using $[\theta]_{222,\text{helix}} = -36{,}000$ (for a peptide model) instead of $-39{,}500$ (for a long, fully helical protein).
> 4. **Neglecting buffer blank subtraction** — failing to mention this step loses 1 mark in any practical question.
> 5. **Confusing $M$ (whole protein) with $M/n$ (per residue)** in the normalization equation.
> 6. **Failing to state assumptions** in derivations (e.g., two-state unfolding, $\Delta H$ temperature-independent).

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **CD = differential absorption of L- and R-circularly polarized light** by chiral chromophores.
- **Far-UV CD (190–250 nm) → secondary structure** (peptide bond); **Near-UV CD (250–320 nm) → tertiary environment** (aromatics, S–S).
- **Characteristic α-helix CD signature:** two negative bands (208 nm, 222 nm) + strong positive band at 190 nm. The ratio $[\theta]_{222}/[\theta]_{208} \approx 1.0$ indicates a non-interacting helix bundle.
- **β-sheet signature:** broad negative band around 215 nm, positive around 195 nm.
- **Random coil:** strong negative band near 198 nm, weak elsewhere.
- **Two key equations to memorize:**

$$[\theta] = \frac{\theta \cdot M}{10 \cdot c \cdot \ell \cdot n} \quad ; \quad [\theta] = 3298.2 \cdot \Delta\varepsilon$$

- **Conversion factor 32.982** comes from $\ln(10) \times 180 / (4\pi)$.
- **For α-helix estimation:** Greenfield–Fasman (single wavelength at 222 nm) gives a quick estimate; full-spectrum fitting with **CDSSTR, SELCON3, or K2D3** is more reliable.
- **Thermal melt CD** gives $T_m$, $\Delta H_{van't\,Hoff}$, and $\Delta G$ of unfolding using $\Delta G = \Delta H(1 - T/T_m)$.
- **High-tension (HT) voltage** must be kept below ~600 V for reliable far-UV data — always check and mention in practical answers.
- **Sample requirements:** Non-absorbing buffer (avoid Tris, imidazole; use phosphate), protein concentration 0.1–1 mg/mL for far-UV, cell path 0.1–1 mm.
- **Common applications in industry:** biosimilar comparability, formulation screening, mutation effect on stability, IDP characterization, membrane-protein helix content.
- **Units to remember:** mdeg (raw), deg·cm²·dmol⁻¹ ($[\theta]$), M⁻¹·cm⁻¹ ($\Delta\varepsilon$).
- **KTU-favourite questions:** (i) Derive $[\theta]$ from $\Delta A$, (ii) Explain the working of a CD spectrophotometer with block diagram, (iii) CD signatures of secondary structures, (iv) CD-based thermal denaturation analysis.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Nuclear Magnetic Resonance
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEBBT756 - PROTEOMICS & PROTEIN ENGINEERING > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Nuclear Magnetic Resonance # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Nuclear Magnetic Resonance (NMR) Spectroscopy** is a powerful, non-destructive analytical technique that exploits the magnetic properties of certain atomic nuclei (most commonly **¹H, ¹³C, ¹⁵N, and ³¹P**) to investigate the molecular structure, dynamics, chemical environment, and three-dimensional conformation of molecules. In proteomics, NMR is the foremost technique for determining the solution-state 3D structures of proteins, characterizing protein-ligand interactions, and probing protein dynamics at atomic resolution. The fundamental principle relies on nuclei possessing a non-zero **nuclear spin quantum number (I)**, which gives rise to a **magnetic moment (μ)**. When placed in a strong external static magnetic field (**B₀**), these magnetic moments align either *with* (low-energy, α state) or *against* (high-energy, β state) the field. Application of a radiofrequency (RF) pulse at the **Larmor frequency (ν₀)** causes resonance absorption, and the subsequent relaxation emits a signal that is Fourier transformed to yield the NMR spectrum. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Spinning Top in Earth's Gravity" Imagine a **spinning top (gyroscope)** placed on a table. The top's spin axis is its "magnetic moment," and Earth's gravity is the external "magnetic field B₀." - Without gravity, the top spins freely in any direction. - With gravity, the top's axis **precesses** (wobbles in a circular path) around the vertical — this is **Larmor precession**. - The faster the top spins, or the stronger the gravity, the faster the precession frequency. - Now, if you tap the top with a precise, rhythmic push at exactly the precession frequency, the top absorbs energy and tilts further — this is **resonance absorption**. In NMR, the **¹H nucleus** is the spinning top, the **superconducting magnet (B₀)** is gravity, and the **RF pulse at Larmor frequency** is the rhythmic tap. The "wobble pattern" recorded is the NMR signal! > [!NOTE] > **Key Insight for Proteomics**: NMR is the *only* technique that can determine protein structures in solution under near-physiological conditions, capturing conformational flexibility that X-ray crystallography (which requires crystals) often misses. ## 1.3 Why NMR Matters in Protein Engineering | Property | Significance in Protein Engineering | |----------|-------------------------------------| | **Atomic resolution** | Resolves individual atoms in amino acid side chains | | **Solution-state** | Studies proteins in their native aqueous environment | | **Dynamics** | Measures motions on picosecond-to-second timescales | | **Non-destructive** | Sample can be recovered and reused | | **Isotope labeling** | ¹⁵N/¹³C labeling enables multi-dimensional experiments | ## 1.4 The NMR-Active Nuclei Relevant to Proteins - **¹H (Proton)** — I = ½, 99.98 % natural abundance → most sensitive - **¹³C** — I = ½, 1.1 % natural abundance → requires labeling - **¹⁵N** — I = ½, 0.37 % natural abundance → backbone probe - **³¹P** — I = ½, 100 % natural abundance → for phosphorylated proteins > [!IMPORTANT] > Only nuclei with **I > 0** (i.e., odd mass number, odd atomic number, or both) exhibit NMR activity. ¹²C, ¹⁶O, and ³²S are NMR-silent. # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Physics of NMR — Step-by-Step Logic ### Step 1: Nuclear Spin and Magnetic Moment A nucleus with spin quantum number **I** has **2I + 1** allowed orientations in a magnetic field. For spin-½ nuclei (¹H, ¹³C, ¹⁵N), there are exactly **two** energy states: - **α state (m_I = +½)** — aligned with B₀ → lower energy - **β state (m_I = –½)** — aligned against B₀ → higher energy The magnetic moment is given by: $$\vec{\mu} = \gamma \hbar \vec{I}$$ where **γ** is the **gyromagnetic ratio** (a fundamental property of each nucleus) and **ħ** is the reduced Planck constant. ### Step 2: Energy Splitting in B₀ (Zeeman Effect) The energy difference between the two spin states is: $$\Delta E = \gamma \hbar B_0 = h \nu_0$$ This is the famous **Zeeman splitting**. The population difference obeys the **Boltzmann distribution**: $$\frac{N_\beta}{N_\alpha} = e^{-\Delta E / k_B T}$$ At room temperature and a typical B₀ = 14.1 T, the excess population in α is only ~**1 in 10⁴–10⁵** nuclei — which is why NMR is intrinsically insensitive. ### Step 3: Larmor Precession Frequency When tipped off-axis, the nuclear magnetization precesses around B₀ at the **Larmor frequency**: $$\nu_0 = \frac{\gamma B_0}{2\pi}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Critical Equation**: The Larmor frequency is the heart of NMR. For ¹H at 14.1 T, ν₀ ≈ **600 MHz** — hence the spectrometer designation "600 MHz NMR." ### Step 4: Resonance and Signal Generation An RF pulse applied at frequency ν₀ tips the net magnetization vector away from B₀. As it precesses back to equilibrium, it induces a voltage in the receiver coil — this decaying sinusoidal signal is the **Free Induction Decay (FID)**. Fourier Transformation of the FID yields the familiar **NMR spectrum** (frequency domain). ### Step 5: Chemical Shift (δ) — The Analytical Powerhouse Electrons surrounding the nucleus generate a small **local shielding field (B_local)** that opposes B₀. The effective field at the nucleus is: $$B_{eff} = B_0 (1 - \sigma)$$ where **σ** is the **shielding constant**. The observed resonance frequency is therefore: $$\nu_{observed} = \frac{\gamma B_{eff}}{2\pi} = \frac{\gamma B_0 (1 - \sigma)}{2\pi}$$ To make chemical shifts **field-independent** (so that a 600 MHz and an 800 MHz spectrometer report the same δ), we define: $$\delta = \frac{\nu_{sample} - \nu_{reference}}{\nu_{spectrometer}} \times 10^6 \text{ ppm}$$ The reference standard is **Tetramethylsilane (TMS)** for ¹H and ¹³C NMR; for proteins, **DSS (4,4-dimethyl-4-silapentane-1-sulfonic acid)** is commonly used. ### Step 6: Spin-Spin Coupling (J-Coupling) Nuclei connected through bonds interact via bonds, splitting each other's signals. The **scalar coupling constant J** (in Hz) is independent of B₀ and provides through-bond connectivity information — the basis of **COSY, TOCSY, HSQC, and HMBC** experiments. ### Step 7: Nuclear Overhauser Effect (NOE) The NOE arises from **through-space dipolar coupling** between nuclei < **5 Å** apart. The NOE intensity is proportional to **1/r⁶**, making it the primary source of **distance restraints** for protein 3D structure determination. $$\text{NOE} \propto \frac{1}{r^6}$$ ### Step 8: Relaxation — T1 and T2 After RF excitation, the magnetization returns to equilibrium via: - **Longitudinal (spin-lattice) relaxation, T1** — recovery of M_z along B₀ - **Transverse (spin-spin) relaxation, T2** — decay of M_xy in the plane $$M_z(t) = M_0 (1 - e^{-t/T_1})$$ $$M_{xy}(t) = M_{xy}(0) \cdot e^{-t/T_2}$$ ## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet | # | Formula / Concept | Symbol | Equation / Expression | Units | Physical Meaning | |---|-------------------|--------|----------------------|-------|------------------| | 1 | Magnetic moment | μ | $\mu = \gamma \hbar I$ | J·T⁻¹ | Fundamental nuclear property | | 2 | Larmor frequency | ν₀ | $\nu_0 = \gamma B_0 / 2\pi$ | Hz (MHz) | Precession/resonance frequency | | 3 | Zeeman energy gap | ΔE | $\Delta E = h \nu_0 = \gamma \hbar B_0$ | J | Energy between α and β states | | 4 | Boltzmann population | N_β/N_α | $e^{-\Delta E / k_B T}$ | dimensionless | Thermal equilibrium ratio | | 5 | Effective field | B_eff | $B_{eff} = B_0 (1 - \sigma)$ | Tesla | Shielded field at nucleus | | 6 | Chemical shift | δ | $\delta = (\nu_{sample} - \nu_{ref})/\nu_0 \times 10^6$ | ppm | Field-independent shift | | 7 | NOE distance | I_NOE | $I_{NOE} \propto 1/r^6$ | a.u. | Through-space proximity | | 8 | T1 relaxation | M_z(t) | $M_0(1 - e^{-t/T_1})$ | magnetization | Longitudinal recovery | | 9 | T2 relaxation | M_xy(t) | $M_{xy}(0) e^{-t/T_2}$ | magnetization | Transverse decay | | 10 | Coupling constant | J | $J = \gamma_i \gamma_j h / (2\pi r^3)$ | Hz | Through-bond interaction | | 11 | Rotating frame freq. | ω₁ | $\omega_1 = \gamma B_1$ | rad/s | RF pulse strength | | 12 | Gyromagnetic ratio ¹H | γ_H | 2.675 × 10⁸ | rad·s⁻¹·T⁻¹ | Specific to ¹H | | 13 | Gyromagnetic ratio ¹³C | γ_C | 6.728 × 10⁷ | rad·s⁻¹·T⁻¹ | Specific to ¹³C | | 14 | Gyromagnetic ratio ¹⁵N | γ_N | -2.712 × 10⁷ | rad·s⁻¹·T⁻¹ | Negative (key fact!) | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Negative γ for ¹⁵N** is a high-yield KTU fact. The negative sign means ¹⁵N spins precess in the *opposite* direction to ¹H. This is exploited in **HSQC** pulse sequences for heteronuclear coherence transfer. ## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility in Proteomics 1. **3D Structure Determination**: Solution NMR solves protein structures up to ~**30–40 kDa** (with TROSY and selective labeling, up to ~**100 kDa**). 2. **Drug Discovery**: **SAR by NMR** (Structure-Activity Relationship by NMR) — Fesik's method at Abbott — uses chemical shift mapping to discover fragment-based drug leads. 3. **Protein-Protein Interactions**: Chemical shift perturbations (CSPs) upon binding map the interface at residue-level resolution. 4. **Membrane Proteins**: Solid-state NMR and solution NMR in detergent micelles or nanodiscs reveal structures of otherwise intractable membrane proteins. 5. **Intrinsically Disordered Proteins (IDPs)**: NMR is uniquely suited to study the conformational ensembles of IDPs that are refractory to crystallography. # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1: Larmor Frequency from Classical Torque Analysis **Starting Premise:** A magnetic moment μ placed in a magnetic field B₀ experiences a torque: $$\vec{\tau} = \vec{\mu} \times \vec{B_0}$$ **Step 1 — Relate torque to angular momentum:** Newton's second law for rotational motion gives: $$\frac{d\vec{L}}{dt} = \vec{\tau} = \vec{\mu} \times \vec{B_0}$$ **Step 2 — Substitute μ = γL:** The magnetic moment is proportional to the angular momentum via the gyromagnetic ratio: $$\vec{\mu} = \gamma \vec{L}$$ Therefore: $$\frac{d\vec{\mu}}{dt} = \gamma \frac{d\vec{L}}{dt} = \gamma (\vec{\mu} \times \vec{B_0})$$ **Step 3 — Apply the cross product for B₀ along z-axis:** Let $\vec{B_0} = B_0 \hat{z}$. The cross product $\vec{\mu} \times B_0 \hat{z}$ produces components: $$\begin{aligned} \frac{d\mu_x}{dt} &= \gamma \mu_y B_0 \\ \frac{d\mu_y}{dt} &= -\gamma \mu_x B_0 \\ \frac{d\mu_z}{dt} &= 0 \end{aligned}$$ **Step 4 — Solve the coupled differential equations:** Assume trial solution $\mu_x = A \cos(\omega t) + B \sin(\omega t)$. Substituting into the first equation and matching terms yields: $$\omega = \gamma B_0$$ This angular frequency is the **Larmor angular frequency (ω₀)**. Converting to ordinary frequency: $$\nu_0 = \frac{\omega_0}{2\pi} = \frac{\gamma B_0}{2\pi}$$ **Numerical evaluation for ¹H at 14.1 T:** $$\begin{aligned} \nu_0 &= \frac{(2.675 \times 10^8 \text{ rad s}^{-1}\text{T}^{-1})(14.1 \text{ T})}{2\pi} \\ &= \frac{3.772 \times 10^9}{6.2832} \\ &\approx 6.00 \times 10^8 \text{ Hz} \\ &= 600 \text{ MHz} \end{aligned}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Interpretation**: A 14.1 T magnet (typical high-field NMR) operates at 600 MHz for protons. Higher fields (21.1 T → 900 MHz, 23.5 T → 1000 MHz) provide better sensitivity and dispersion. ## 3.2 Derivation 2: Chemical Shift and Local Shielding **Premise:** Electron clouds around a nucleus circulate in B₀, generating a small opposing field (Lenz's law). **Step 1 — Diamagnetic shielding contribution:** For a spherical electron cloud, Lamb's formula gives: $$\sigma_{dia} = \frac{e^2 \mu_0}{3 m_e} \sum_i \langle r_i^{-1} \rangle$$ **Step 2 — Paramagnetic deshielding contribution (dominant for heavy atoms):** $$\sigma_{para} \propto -\frac{1}{\Delta E} \left\langle r^{-3} \right\rangle$$ where ΔE is the mean electronic excitation energy. **Step 3 — Net effective field at nucleus:** $$B_{eff} = B_0 (1 - \sigma_{dia} - \sigma_{para})$$ **Step 4 — Observed resonance frequency:** $$\nu_{obs} = \frac{\gamma B_{eff}}{2\pi} = \frac{\gamma B_0 (1-\sigma)}{2\pi}$$ **Step 5 — Define chemical shift (field-independent):** $$\delta = \frac{\nu_{sample} - \nu_{TMS}}{\nu_{spectrometer}} \times 10^6 \text{ ppm}$$ **Worked Example:** A ¹H resonance appears at 2400 Hz above TMS on a 600 MHz spectrometer: $$\delta = \frac{2400 \text{ Hz}}{600 \times 10^6 \text{ Hz}} \times 10^6 = 4.0 \text{ ppm}$$ This value is typical for an **α-helix backbone amide proton** in a protein. ## 3.3 Derivation 3: NOE and the 1/r⁶ Distance Dependence **Premise:** Two nuclear spins I and S interact through space via dipolar coupling. **Step 1 — Dipolar Hamiltonian:** $$H_{IS} = \frac{\mu_0 \gamma_I \gamma_S \hbar^2}{4\pi r^3} [3(\vec{I}\cdot\hat{r})(\vec{S}\cdot\hat{r}) - \vec{I}\cdot\vec{S}]$$ **Step 2 — NOE build-up rate (Solomon equations):** $$\sigma_{IS} \propto \frac{\gamma_I \gamma_S \hbar^2}{r^6} \tau_c$$ where **τc** is the **correlation time** (a measure of molecular tumbling). **Step 3 — Steady-state NOE enhancement:** $$\eta_{NOE} = \frac{I - I_0}{I_0} \propto \frac{1}{r^6}$$ **Implication for protein structure:** $$\begin{aligned} \text{NOE at } 2.0 \text{ Å} &\to \text{strong} \\ \text{NOE at } 3.0 \text{ Å} &\to \text{medium} \\ \text{NOE at } 4.0 \text{ Å} &\to \text{weak} \\ \text{NOE at } 5.0 \text{ Å} &\to \text{detectable limit} \\ \text{NOE at } > 5.0 \text{ Å} &\to \text{below detection} \end{aligned}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > This steep 1/r⁶ dependence is what makes NOE such a precise "molecular ruler" — a 2 Å change in distance produces a **64-fold change** in NOE intensity. This forms the basis of the ~**10–15 NOE distance restraints per residue** needed to define a protein's 3D fold. ## 3.4 Derivation 4: 2D NMR — HSQC Pulse Sequence Logic The **Heteronuclear Single Quantum Coherence (HSQC)** experiment correlates ¹H with its directly bonded ¹⁵N (or ¹³C). **Coherence pathway:** $$^1H_z \xrightarrow{90°_x} {^1H_y} \xrightarrow{\text{J}_{NH}} \text{antiphase } ^1H_x N_z \xrightarrow{90°} {^1H_x} \to N_x N_y \xrightarrow{\Delta = 1/(2J)} N_x \xrightarrow{90°} \text{detect } ^1H$$ **Step-by-step:** 1. **INEPT transfer**: Magnetization moves from ¹H to ¹⁵N via the J_NH coupling (~92 Hz). 2. **t₁ evolution**: Chemical shift of ¹⁵N is encoded during t₁. 3. **Reverse INEPT**: Magnetization returns to ¹H for detection. 4. **t₂ acquisition**: ¹H chemical shift is detected. The 2D spectrum has: - **F1 (ω₁) axis** = ¹⁵N chemical shift - **F2 (ω₂) axis** = ¹H chemical shift - **Each peak** = one N–H pair (backbone amide or side chain) > [!NOTE] > A **2D ¹H-¹⁵N HSQC** is the "**fingerprint**" of a protein. A well-folded protein of n residues shows ~n distinct cross-peaks. IDPs show *narrower* chemical shift dispersion due to lack of defined structure. ## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Multi-Dimensional NMR Experiments | Experiment | Dimension | Information Content | Use in Protein NMR | |------------|-----------|---------------------|---------------------| | **1D ¹H** | 1D | Chemical shifts, integrals | Quick assessment, purity | | **COSY** | 2D ¹H-¹H | J-coupled protons (vicinal, geminal) | Scalar connectivity | | **TOCSY** | 2D ¹H-¹H | Entire spin system of a residue | Amino acid type identification | | **NOESY** | 2D ¹H-¹H | Through-space (< 5 Å) NOEs | Distance restraints | | **HSQC** | 2D ¹H-¹⁵N | N–H pairs | Backbone assignment, fingerprint | | **HMBC** | 2D ¹H-¹³C | Long-range (²J, ³J) C–H | Side chain assignment | | **HNCA** | 3D ¹H-¹⁵N-¹³C | Backbone walk, Cα(i) and Cα(i-1) | Sequential assignment | | **CBCA(CO)NH** | 3D | Cα, Cβ of residue i-1 | Backbone assignment | | **NOESY-HSQC** | 3D ¹H-¹H-¹⁵N | NOEs involving amide protons | Structure determination | | **HNCO** | 3D ¹H-¹⁵N-¹³C | Carbonyl C=O of residue i-1 | Backbone walk | | **TROSY** | 2D ¹H-¹⁵N | TROSY-enhanced HSQC | Large proteins (> 30 kDa) | ## 3.6 Quantitative Analysis: Sensitivity Comparison The relative sensitivity of an NMR experiment scales as: $$\text{S/N} \propto N \cdot \gamma_{exc}^{3/2} \cdot \gamma_{det}^{3/2} \cdot B_0^{3/2} \cdot n_s^{1/2}$$ where: - $N$ = number of spins - $\gamma_{exc}$ = gyromagnetic ratio of excited nucleus - $\gamma_{det}$ = gyromagnetic ratio of detected nucleus - $B_0$ = magnetic field strength - $n_s$ = number of scans **Sensitivity ranking (most → least sensitive):** $$^1H \;>\; ^{19}F \;>\; ^{31}P \;>\; ^{13}C \;>\; ^{15}N$$ # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block Diagram — NMR Spectrometer Architecture ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph MAG["Superconducting Magnet System"] A1["Magnet
B0 = 14.1 T"] A2["Shim Coils
Field Homogenization"] A3["Probe
RF Coils and Sample"] end subgraph CONS["Console Electronics"] B1["RF Transmitter
Pulse Generator"] B2["Pulsed Field
Gradients X Y Z"] B3["Receiver
Pre-amplifier"] B4["Digitizer
Analog to Digital"] end subgraph COMP["Computer and Processing"] C1["Pulse Sequence
Program"] C2["FT Processor
FID to Spectrum"] C3["Spectral Display
1D 2D 3D"] C4["Structure Calc
DYANA XPLOR"] end subgraph SAMP["Sample Preparation"] D1["Purified Protein
uM to mM"] D2["Isotope Labeling
15N 13C"] D3["NMR Tube
5 mm Shigemi"] end D1 --> SAMP D2 --> SAMP D3 --> A3 C1 --> B1 B1 --> A3 A3 --> B3 B2 --> A3 B3 --> B4 B4 --> C2 C2 --> C3 C3 --> C4 A2 --> A1 ``` ## 4.2 Flow Diagram — Protein Structure Determination by NMR ```mermaid flowchart TD S1["Step 1: Protein Expression
and Purification"] S2["Step 2: Isotope Labeling
15N and 13C Uniform"] S3["Step 3: NMR Data Acquisition
2D 3D 4D Experiments"] S4["Step 4: Backbone Assignment
HSQC HNCA CBCA CO NH"] S5["Step 5: Side Chain Assignment
TOCSY HCCH COSY"] S6["Step 6: NOE Assignment
NOESY Spectra"] S7["Step 7: Structure Calculation
Distance Geometry MD"] S8["Step 8: Refinement
Energy Minimization"] S9["Step 9: Validation
PDB Deposition"] S1 --> S2 --> S3 --> S4 --> S5 --> S6 --> S7 --> S8 --> S9 ``` ## 4.3 Vector Diagram — Pulse Sequence and Magnetization Behavior ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph E1["Equilibrium State"] V1["M0 along B0
z axis"] end subgraph E2["After 90 Degree Pulse"] V2["M in xy plane
precessing"] end subgraph E3["Acquisition"] V3["FID recorded
decaying sinusoid"] end subgraph E4["Fourier Transform"] V4["Frequency Spectrum
peaks at Larmor"] end V1 -- "RF pulse at nu0" --> V2 V2 -- "T2 relaxation" --> V3 V3 -- "FT Algorithm" --> V4 ``` ## 4.4 Hierarchy Diagram — NMR Hierarchy of Information ```mermaid flowchart TD H1["Raw FID
Time Domain Signal"] H2["1D Spectrum
Chemical Shifts"] H3["2D Spectra
COSY NOESY HSQC"] H4["3D 4D Spectra
Resolved Overlaps"] H5["Chemical Shift List
Per Residue"] H6["NOE Distance Restraints
10 to 15 per residue"] H7["TALOS Dihedral Angles
Backbone phi psi"] H8["3D Structure Ensemble
10 to 20 Models"] H9["PDB Coordinate File
Public Deposition"] H1 --> H2 --> H3 --> H4 --> H5 --> H6 --> H7 --> H8 --> H9 ``` ## 4.5 Block Diagram — 2D HSQC Pulse Sequence ```mermaid sequenceDiagram participant H as 1H Channel participant N as 15N Channel participant D as Detector Note over H,N: Preparation H->>H: 90 degree pulse Note over H,N: INEPT Transfer H->>H: 180 degree pulse H->>N: 90 degree pulse Note over H,N: t1 Evolution Period N->>N: 180 degree pulse H->>H: 180 degree pulse Note over H,N: Reverse INEPT N->>N: 90 degree pulse H->>H: 90 degree pulse Note over D: Acquisition t2 D->>D: Detect 1H signal ``` # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### **Question A1** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **Define the term "chemical shift" in NMR spectroscopy. Why is the chemical shift expressed in ppm rather than Hz?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** Chemical shift (δ) is the relative change in the resonance frequency of a nucleus due to shielding by surrounding electrons when the molecule is placed in an external magnetic field. It is defined as: $$\delta = \frac{\nu_{sample} - \nu_{reference}}{\nu_{spectrometer}} \times 10^6 \text{ ppm}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Why ppm?** Resonance frequency differences in Hz are **field-dependent** (e.g., the same proton would show different Hz separations on a 400 MHz vs 600 MHz instrument). Dividing by the spectrometer frequency makes δ **field-independent**, allowing direct comparison of spectra recorded on different magnets. (3 marks) --- ### **Question A2** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **What is the Nuclear Overhauser Effect (NOE)? State its significance in protein structure determination.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** The NOE is the change in intensity of the NMR signal of one nucleus (I) when another nearby nucleus (S) is saturated. It arises from **through-space dipolar coupling** between the two spins and depends on the **inverse sixth power** of the inter-nuclear distance: $$\text{NOE intensity} \propto \frac{1}{r^6}$$ **Significance in protein NMR:** NOEs are detected between protons that are **less than 5 Å apart**, regardless of whether they are bonded. By assigning thousands of NOE cross-peaks, one obtains ~**10–15 distance restraints per residue**, which are used as input for 3D structure calculations (e.g., via simulated annealing in XPLOR-NIH or CYANA). This makes NOE the **primary source of geometric information** in NMR-based protein structure determination. (3 marks) --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Pattern**: Each Part B question has internal choice. Both alternatives are given below; in the actual exam, the student answers **one** of them. --- ### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **(a)** With a neat diagram, explain the basic principle of NMR spectroscopy. Derive the Larmor frequency equation and discuss the significance of the gyromagnetic ratio. **(7 marks)** **(b)** What is spin-spin coupling? Explain how the multiplicity of an NMR signal is determined by the **n+1 rule**. Discuss how scalar coupling constants differ from dipolar couplings in providing structural information about proteins. **(7 marks)** --- #### **Model Solution (a) — 7 marks** **Principle of NMR (3 marks):** NMR exploits the magnetic properties of nuclei with non-zero spin (e.g., ¹H, ¹³C, ¹⁵N). When a sample is placed in a strong static magnetic field **B₀**, the nuclear magnetic moments align either parallel (α, low energy) or anti-parallel (β, high energy) to B₀, giving rise to **Zeeman splitting**: $$\Delta E = \gamma \hbar B_0$$ Applying a radiofrequency (RF) pulse at the **Larmor frequency** $\nu_0 = \gamma B_0 / 2\pi$ causes resonance absorption. The subsequent relaxation signal (FID) is Fourier transformed to give the NMR spectrum. **Derivation of Larmor Frequency (3 marks):** A magnetic moment μ in field B₀ experiences a torque $\vec{\tau} = \vec{\mu} \times \vec{B_0}$. Using $\vec{\mu} = \gamma \vec{L}$ and Newton's second law for rotation: $$\frac{d\vec{\mu}}{dt} = \gamma (\vec{\mu} \times \vec{B_0})$$ For $\vec{B_0} = B_0 \hat{z}$, this gives three coupled ODEs whose solution yields precession at angular frequency: $$\omega_0 = \gamma B_0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \nu_0 = \frac{\gamma B_0}{2\pi}$$ **Significance of Gyromagnetic Ratio (1 mark):** The gyromagnetic ratio γ is a **fundamental nuclear property** that determines: - The Larmor frequency (resonance condition) - The sensitivity (∝ γ³/²) - The sign of precession (negative for ¹⁵N, positive for ¹H) - For ¹H: γ = 2.675 × 10⁸ rad·s⁻¹·T⁻¹; for ¹³C: γ = 6.728 × 10⁷ rad·s⁻¹·T⁻¹. --- #### **Model Solution (b) — 7 marks** **Spin-Spin Coupling (2 marks):** Spin-spin coupling (J-coupling) is the **through-bond interaction** between two nuclei mediated by the electrons in the intervening bonds. It splits an NMR signal into multiplets, with the separation between adjacent lines equal to the **coupling constant J** (in Hz). J is **independent of the external field B₀** and depends only on molecular structure. **n+1 Rule (2 marks):** If a nucleus has **n** equivalent neighboring nuclei (with I = ½), its signal is split into **(n+1) lines** with intensity ratios given by Pascal's triangle. Example: a CH₃ group (n = 0) is a singlet; a CH₂ next to CH₃ is a quartet (n+1 = 4); methine next to two CH₃ groups (6 H neighbors) is a septet. **Scalar vs Dipolar Coupling (3 marks):** | Feature | Scalar (J) Coupling | Dipolar Coupling | |---------|---------------------|------------------| | Mechanism | Through-bond (electrons) | Through-space (direct magnetic) | | Range | 1–3 bonds typical | < 5 Å | | Field dependence | Independent of B₀ | Scales with B₀ (averaged in solution) | | Information | Bond connectivity, dihedral angles | Internuclear distances | | Use in proteins | COSY, HSQC, HMBC assignment | NOE distance restraints | > [!NOTE] > In solution NMR, dipolar couplings average to zero for **isotropic** molecules but can be **partially retained** in aligned media (e.g., bicelles, phages) — these **Residual Dipolar Couplings (RDCs)** provide long-range angular information for structure refinement. --- ### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **(a)** What is a 2D NMR experiment? With reference to NOESY, describe the principle, pulse sequence, and the use of NOESY in protein structure determination. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Explain the concept of relaxation times T1 and T2 in NMR. Discuss how these parameters are influenced by molecular tumbling (correlation time τc) and their implications for studying large protein complexes. **(7 marks)** --- #### **Model Solution (a) — 7 marks** **2D NMR Concept (2 marks):** A 2D NMR experiment spreads the NMR signal over two frequency dimensions (ω₁ and ω₂) by introducing an **evolution period (t₁)** between preparation and detection. Double Fourier transformation over t₁ and t₂ (t₂ = acquisition time) yields a 2D spectrum with cross-peaks correlating nuclei related by the chosen coherence transfer mechanism. **NOESY Principle and Pulse Sequence (3 marks):** NOESY (Nuclear Overhauser Effect SpectroscopY) detects **through-space dipolar interactions** between protons < 5 Å apart. The pulse sequence is: 1. **90°–t₁–90°–τ_mix–90°–acquire(t₂)** 2. The first 90° creates transverse magnetization. 3. During t₁, nuclei are **frequency-labeled** with their chemical shifts. 4. The second 90° stores magnetization as **longitudinal** (along z). 5. During the **mixing time τ_mix**, **cross-relaxation** transfers magnetization between nearby spins (the NOE). 6. The third 90° brings magnetization back to the transverse plane for detection. **Use in Protein Structure Determination (2 marks):** In 3D ¹⁵N-separated NOESY-HSQC and ¹³C-separated NOESY-HSQC, NOE cross-peaks involving amide or aliphatic protons are resolved by spreading them into the heteronuclear dimension. Each NOE provides a **distance restraint** (calibrated from peak intensity as 1/r⁶), and ~**10–15 restraints per residue** are collected. These are fed into structure calculation programs (CYANA, XPLOR-NIH, ARIA) using **simulated annealing** to produce an ensemble of 3D structures. --- #### **Model Solution (b) — 7 marks** **Concept of T1 and T2 (3 marks):** - **T1 (longitudinal/spin-lattice relaxation)**: Time constant for the recovery of magnetization **along the z-axis** (B₀ direction). It reflects energy exchange between the spin system and the surrounding "lattice" (molecular environment). Governed by: $$M_z(t) = M_0 (1 - e^{-t/T_1})$$ - **T2 (transverse/spin-spin relaxation)**: Time constant for the **decay of magnetization in the xy-plane** due to dephasing of precessing spins. Governed by: $$M_{xy}(t) = M_{xy}(0) e^{-t/T_2}$$ T2 is always ≤ T1. The linewidth of an NMR peak is inversely proportional to T2: $$\Delta \nu_{1/2} = \frac{1}{\pi T_2}$$ **Influence of Correlation Time (τc) (2 marks):** The **correlation time τc** measures how long a molecule takes to tumble ~1 radian in solution. For a spherical protein: $$\tau_c = \frac{4\pi \eta r^3}{3 k_B T}$$ where η is viscosity and r is the hydrodynamic radius. - **Small molecules (τc < 1 ns)**: T1 and T2 are long and similar → sharp lines, weak NOE. - **Medium proteins (τc ~ 5–20 ns)**: T1 ≈ T2, **maximum NOE enhancement** → ideal for structure determination. - **Large complexes (τc > 50 ns)**: T2 becomes very short → broad lines, signal loss. **Implications for Large Protein Complexes (2 marks):** For large proteins (> 30 kDa), conventional NMR fails because: - Slow tumbling → short T2 → broad lines → loss of resolution - Spectral overlap becomes severe **Solutions:** - **TROSY (Transverse Relaxation-Optimized SpectroscopY)**: Selects the narrowest component of the ¹H-¹⁵N multiplet, suppressing relaxation losses → enables NMR of proteins up to ~100 kDa. - **Selective isotope labeling** (e.g., ²H, ¹⁵N) reduces dipolar relaxation pathways. - **CRINEPT/CRIPT** techniques for very large complexes. - **Methyl-TROSY** on selectively labeled Ile/Leu/Val methyls. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**: > 1. **Do not confuse** chemical shift (δ in ppm) with chemical shift **perturbation** (Δδ in ppm used in ligand binding studies). > 2. **Do not confuse** scalar coupling (J, in Hz, field-independent) with dipolar coupling (depends on B₀, averaged in solution). > 3. **Do not state** that ¹³C is "NMR-silent" — it has I = ½ and IS observable, just at low natural abundance (1.1 %). > 4. **Sign of γ for ¹⁵N is NEGATIVE** — losing this 1 mark is a classic KTU pitfall. > 5. **T2 is always ≤ T1** — a student writing T2 > T1 loses a mark. > 6. NOE intensity scales as **1/r⁶**, NOT 1/r³ — a very common error. > 7. Always specify that 2D NMR uses **double Fourier transformation** over t₁ and t₂. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - **NMR detects nuclei with I > 0**; ¹H, ¹³C, ¹⁵N, ³¹P are the biologically relevant NMR-active nuclei. - **Larmor equation**: $\nu_0 = \gamma B_0 / 2\pi$ is the resonance condition; 600 MHz for ¹H at 14.1 T. - **Chemical shift δ** is in **ppm** (field-independent) and is referenced to TMS (¹H, ¹³C) or DSS (proteins). - **Electron-rich (shielded) nuclei** appear at **low δ (upfield)**; electron-poor (deshielded) at **high δ (downfield)**. - **Typical ¹H chemical shifts in proteins**: Hα ~ 3.8–4.8 ppm; HN (amide) ~ 6.5–9.5 ppm; aromatic ~ 6.5–8.5 ppm. - **J-coupling** is through-bond (1–3 bonds); obeys the **n+1 rule**; field-independent. - **NOE** is through-space (< 5 Å); scales as **1/r⁶**; primary source of distance restraints. - **T1 (longitudinal) ≥ T2 (transverse)**; linewidth ∝ 1/T2. - **HSQC** correlates directly bonded ¹H-¹⁵N (or ¹H-¹³C) — the protein **fingerprint** spectrum. - **NOESY** provides **distance restraints**; **COSY/TOCSY** provide **scalar connectivity**. - **3D/4D experiments** (HNCA, CBCA(CO)NH, HNCO, NOESY-HSQC) are required to resolve overlaps in proteins. - **TROSY** extends solution NMR to ~100 kDa; **methyl-TROSY** for megadalta complexes. - **Gyromagnetic ratios to memorize**: γ(¹H) = 2.675 × 10⁸; γ(¹³C) = 6.728 × 10⁷; γ(¹⁵N) = **−**2.712 × 10⁷ rad·s⁻¹·T⁻¹. - **Negative γ(¹⁵N)** is a high-yield exam fact. - **Sensitivity** ∝ γ^(3/2) × B₀^(3/2) × √(n_scans) × N_spins. - **Fourier Transform** converts time-domain FID into frequency-domain spectrum. - **The Boltzmann excess** at room temperature is only ~1 in 10⁴–10⁵ — the fundamental sensitivity limit of NMR. - **Boltzmann distribution** governs the population difference: $N_\beta/N_\alpha = e^{-\Delta E/k_B T}$. - **Phase-sensitive 2D experiments** (e.g., HSQC) preserve sign information; **magnitude mode** does not. - **TALOS** predicts backbone φ/ψ dihedral angles from chemical shifts — supplementary structural restraints. - **SAR by NMR** (Fesik/Abbott) is a drug discovery application of chemical shift mapping.

topics.title: Plasmon Resonance
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBBT756 - PROTEOMICS & PROTEIN ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Plasmon Resonance

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Surface Plasmon Resonance (SPR)** is a label-free, real-time biophysical phenomenon in which polarized light incident on a metal-dielectric interface (typically a thin gold or silver film) generates coherent electron density oscillations — known as **surface plasmons** — at the metal–sample boundary. When the momentum (wave-vector) of the incident photons matches the momentum of the surface plasmons, resonance occurs, producing a sharp dip in the intensity of reflected light at a specific angle (the **SPR angle**, θSPR) or wavelength.

In the context of **Proteomics and Protein Engineering (PEBBT756)**, SPR is the gold-standard optical technique for quantifying **protein–ligand interactions**, **antibody–antigen binding**, **kinetic rate constants** (association rate $k_a$, dissociation rate $k_d$), and **equilibrium dissociation constants** ($K_D$) in real time, without the need for radioactive or fluorescent labels.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (PEBBT756, Module 2):**
> Plasmon Resonance is covered as a **biophysical tool** for studying macromolecular interactions. Students must understand (i) the physical principle, (ii) the Kretschmann optical configuration, (iii) sensorgram interpretation, and (iv) kinetic/affinity data analysis.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **violin string** (the metal surface) sitting in a concert hall. When sound waves (light) of just the right frequency strike the string, it begins to vibrate violently — it *absorbs* the energy. At all other frequencies, the sound simply bounces off.

In **SPR**, the metal film (gold ~50 nm) acts as the "string," and the light photons act as the "sound waves." When the angle of incidence is tuned correctly, the photon's momentum perfectly matches the natural oscillation frequency of the metal's free electrons. The light energy is absorbed → reflected intensity drops sharply. This is the **resonance angle**.

Now attach a **protein** (e.g., an antibody) to the gold surface. When its **ligand** (antigen) flows across, binding events change the local refractive index near the surface. The resonance angle **shifts** by a tiny but measurable amount (typically **millidegrees, m°**). Plotting this shift vs. time gives a **sensorgram**, which is essentially a "binding curve" of the interaction in real time.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Intuition:** SPR measures **mass** (or refractive index changes) at a surface, not a label. This is why it is called a *label-free* technique. A single protein (~50 kDa) binding to the surface shifts the angle by ~**0.1°** (~**100 m°**).

## 1.3 Standard Metrics & Physical Constants

| Parameter | Symbol | Typical Value |
|---|---|---|
| Wavelength of light source | $\lambda$ | **633 nm** (HeNe) or **785 nm** (LED) |
| Refractive index of glass prism | $n_p$ | **1.51** (BK7 glass) |
| Refractive index of gold film | $n_{Au}$ | complex: $n = 0.18 + 3.0i$ at 633 nm |
| Refractive index of sample (water buffer) | $n_s$ | **1.33** |
| Gold film thickness | $d$ | **~50 nm** |
| Resonance angle | $\theta_{SPR}$ | **~44°–70°** (depends on configuration) |
| Sensitivity (RIU shift) | — | **~100–200 m°/RIU** |
| Detection limit (surface mass) | — | **~1 pg/mm²** |
| Boltzmann constant | $k_B$ | $1.381 \times 10^{-23}$ J/K |
| Speed of light (vacuum) | $c$ | $2.998 \times 10^8$ m/s |

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Physics of Surface Plasmons

A **surface plasmon (SP)** is a quantized longitudinal electromagnetic wave that propagates along the interface between a metal (free-electron plasma) and a dielectric. It is a hybrid mode: the electromagnetic field is coupled to the collective oscillation of conduction-band electrons in the metal.

**Two essential conditions** must be met for a surface plasmon to exist at a metal–dielectric boundary:

1. The real part of the metal's **dielectric function** $\varepsilon_m(\omega)$ must be **negative** (true for noble metals like Au, Ag below their plasma frequency).
2. The real part of the dielectric's permittivity $\varepsilon_d$ must be **positive**.

The dispersion relation that links the wave-vector of the surface plasmon ($k_{sp}$) to the angular frequency ($\omega$) is derived from Maxwell's equations with the appropriate boundary conditions (continuity of tangential E and H at the interface):

$$k_{sp} = \frac{\omega}{c} \sqrt{\frac{\varepsilon_m(\omega)\,\varepsilon_d}{\varepsilon_m(\omega) + \varepsilon_d}}$$

This is the **fundamental SPR dispersion equation**. It tells us the allowed $(k, \omega)$ pairs for the surface mode.

## 2.2 Why Direct Excitation Fails — The Momentum Mismatch

For light incident in a dielectric medium of refractive index $n_p$ (the prism) at angle $\theta$, the in-plane (tangential) component of its wave-vector is:

$$k_x = \frac{\omega}{c}\,n_p \sin\theta$$

For a glass prism ($n_p \approx 1.51$) and visible light ($\lambda \approx 633$ nm), the maximum value of $k_x$ is:

$$k_x^{max} = \frac{\omega}{c} \cdot n_p \approx 1.51 \cdot k_0$$

However, the surface plasmon wave-vector $k_{sp}$ is always **larger** than $\omega/c$. Therefore, direct illumination through air or buffer cannot couple to the surface plasmon — this is the **momentum mismatch problem**.

## 2.3 The Kretschmann Configuration — Solving the Mismatch

The **Kretschmann configuration** (1971) resolves the momentum mismatch by evaporating a thin metal film (gold, ~50 nm) directly onto the base of a high-refractive-index glass prism. Light undergoes **total internal reflection (TIR)** at the prism–metal interface. Under TIR, the evanescent wave generated in the metal can tunnel through the thin film and reach the metal–dielectric (sample) interface on the other side.

The in-plane wave-vector of this evanescent wave is **enhanced** by the prism:

$$k_x^{ev} = \frac{\omega}{c}\,n_p \sin\theta$$

By choosing $n_p$ correctly (~1.51) and $\theta$ above the critical angle, $k_x^{ev}$ can be made to equal $k_{sp}$. This is the **resonance condition**:

$$k_x^{ev} = k_{sp} \quad \Longleftrightarrow \quad n_p \sin\theta_{SPR} = \sqrt{\frac{\varepsilon_m \,\varepsilon_d}{\varepsilon_m + \varepsilon_d}}$$

At this moment, the evanescent wave resonantly drives the surface plasmon, and the reflected intensity drops to a **sharp minimum**.

## 2.4 The Evanescent Field — Why SPR is a "Surface" Technique

Under TIR, the electric field does not abruptly vanish at the interface; instead, it decays exponentially into the metal and into the sample with a **penetration depth** $L_p$:

$$E(z) = E_0 \, e^{-z/L_p}, \quad L_p = \frac{\lambda}{2\pi} \sqrt{\frac{1}{n_p^2 \sin^2\theta - n_s^2}}$$

For typical SPR conditions, $L_p \approx$ **200–300 nm** into the sample. This means SPR is exquisitely sensitive to events occurring within ~300 nm of the gold surface — which is why immobilized proteins on a dextran/CM-dextran chip are perfectly suited, while bulk solution changes are invisible.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** SPR is the working principle behind commercial instruments such as **Biacore™ (Cytiva)**, **Reichert SR7500DC**, **Horiba OpenPlex**, and **MNTAmp SPR Navi 220A** — all of which are used in pharmaceutical R&D, antibody characterization, and biosensor development.

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **HIGH-YIELD FORMULAS — KTU PEBBT756 Module 2 (Plasmon Resonance)**
> Master every line below. Numerical problems in ESE are routinely built on these.

| # | Formula | Meaning | Typical Use in Exam |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $k_{sp} = \dfrac{\omega}{c} \sqrt{\dfrac{\varepsilon_m \varepsilon_d}{\varepsilon_m + \varepsilon_d}}$ | Dispersion relation of surface plasmon | Derivation questions (7–14 marks) |
| 2 | $k_x = \dfrac{\omega}{c} \, n_p \sin\theta$ | In-plane wave-vector of incident light | Resonance condition |
| 3 | $n_p \sin\theta_{SPR} = \sqrt{\dfrac{\varepsilon_m \varepsilon_d}{\varepsilon_m + \varepsilon_d}}$ | **SPR resonance condition (Kretschmann)** | Core equation |
| 4 | $\theta_{SPR} = \arcsin\!\left(\dfrac{1}{n_p}\sqrt{\dfrac{\varepsilon_m \varepsilon_d}{\varepsilon_m + \varepsilon_d}}\right)$ | Resonant angle | Numerical calculation |
| 5 | $\Delta\theta_{SPR} = S \cdot \Delta n_s$ | Angle shift proportional to RI change | Sensitivity $S$ in m°/RIU |
| 6 | $L_p = \dfrac{\lambda}{2\pi} \sqrt{\dfrac{1}{n_p^2 \sin^2\theta - n_s^2}}$ | Evanescent penetration depth | Why SPR is a surface technique |
| 7 | $\dfrac{d[R\!L]}{dt} = k_a [L][R] - k_d [R\!L]$ | **Association kinetics** (Langmuir) | Sensorgram analysis |
| 8 | $K_D = \dfrac{k_d}{k_a}$ | **Equilibrium dissociation constant** | Affinity ranking |
| 9 | $R_{eq} = \dfrac{R_{max}[L]}{K_D + [L]}$ | Steady-state binding response | Saturation analysis |
| 10 | $\sigma_{SPR} = \dfrac{2}{k} \left(\dfrac{\varepsilon_m + \varepsilon_d}{\varepsilon_m \varepsilon_d}\right)^{3/2} \dfrac{(-\varepsilon_m')^{1/2}\,\varepsilon_d}{\sqrt{-\varepsilon_m' - \varepsilon_d}}$ | SPR sensitivity factor | Advanced derivation |

**Boundary conditions (Kretschmann):**
- $n_p > n_s$ (prism denser than sample) — required for TIR.
- $\theta > \theta_c = \arcsin(n_s / n_p)$ — required for evanescent-wave generation.
- $d_{Au} \approx 50$ nm — gold film thin enough to allow tunneling, thick enough to be continuous.
- $\Re(\varepsilon_m) < -\varepsilon_d$ — physical condition for a bound SP mode.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Surface Plasmon Dispersion Relation

We start from Maxwell's equations for a non-magnetic medium ($\mu = 1$) and look for bound transverse-magnetic (TM, p-polarized) wave solutions at a planar interface between a metal (region 1, $z > 0$, permittivity $\varepsilon_m$) and a dielectric (region 2, $z < 0$, permittivity $\varepsilon_d$).

### Step 1 — Set up the wave equations

For a TM wave with field components $H_y$, $E_x$, $E_z$ varying as $e^{i(k_x x - \omega t)}$, the Helmholtz equation in each region gives:

$$\frac{\partial^2 H_y}{\partial z^2} + (k_0^2 \varepsilon_i - k_x^2) H_y = 0, \quad i = m, d$$

The general solutions that decay away from the interface (i.e., bound modes) are:

$$
\begin{aligned}
H_y^{(m)}(z) &= A\, e^{-\kappa_m z}, \quad z > 0, \quad \kappa_m = \sqrt{k_x^2 - k_0^2 \varepsilon_m} \\
H_y^{(d)}(z) &= B\, e^{+\kappa_d z}, \quad z < 0, \quad \kappa_d = \sqrt{k_x^2 - k_0^2 \varepsilon_d}
\end{aligned}
$$

For these to be physically valid (decaying), we require $\Re(\kappa_m) > 0$ and $\Re(\kappa_d) > 0$.

### Step 2 — Apply the boundary conditions

The boundary conditions for TM waves at $z = 0$ are: (i) continuity of $H_y$ and (ii) continuity of $\varepsilon_i E_z$ (equivalent to continuity of $\varepsilon_i \frac{1}{\varepsilon_i}\partial H_y/\partial z = \partial H_y/\partial z$ for non-magnetic media).

From $H_y$ continuity: $A = B$.

From the derivative condition: $\dfrac{1}{\varepsilon_m}\dfrac{\partial H_y^{(m)}}{\partial z}\Big|_{z=0} = \dfrac{1}{\varepsilon_d}\dfrac{\partial H_y^{(d)}}{\partial z}\Big|_{z=0}$.

Evaluating:

$$\frac{-\kappa_m A}{\varepsilon_m} = \frac{+\kappa_d A}{\varepsilon_d} \quad \Longrightarrow \quad \frac{\kappa_m}{\varepsilon_m} = -\frac{\kappa_d}{\varepsilon_d}$$

This is the **SP boundary condition**. Note the minus sign on the RHS — it is the source of the requirement $\Re(\varepsilon_m) < 0$.

### Step 3 — Solve for $k_x$

Square both sides and use $\kappa_i^2 = k_x^2 - k_0^2 \varepsilon_i$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{\kappa_m^2}{\varepsilon_m^2} &= \frac{\kappa_d^2}{\varepsilon_d^2} \\
\frac{k_x^2 - k_0^2 \varepsilon_m}{\varepsilon_m^2} &= \frac{k_x^2 - k_0^2 \varepsilon_d}{\varepsilon_d^2} \\
k_x^2 \varepsilon_d^2 - k_0^2 \varepsilon_m \varepsilon_d^2 &= k_x^2 \varepsilon_m^2 - k_0^2 \varepsilon_d \varepsilon_m^2 \\
k_x^2 (\varepsilon_d^2 - \varepsilon_m^2) &= k_0^2 \varepsilon_m \varepsilon_d (\varepsilon_d - \varepsilon_m) \\
k_x^2 (\varepsilon_d - \varepsilon_m)(\varepsilon_d + \varepsilon_m) &= k_0^2 \varepsilon_m \varepsilon_d (\varepsilon_d - \varepsilon_m)
\end{aligned}
$$

Cancel $(\varepsilon_d - \varepsilon_m)$ (valid for $\varepsilon_d \neq \varepsilon_m$):

$$
\begin{aligned}
k_x^2 (\varepsilon_d + \varepsilon_m) &= k_0^2 \varepsilon_m \varepsilon_d \\
k_x^2 &= k_0^2 \,\frac{\varepsilon_m \varepsilon_d}{\varepsilon_m + \varepsilon_d}
\end{aligned}
$$

Substitute $k_0 = \omega/c$:

$$\boxed{\,k_{sp} = k_x = \frac{\omega}{c} \sqrt{\frac{\varepsilon_m \varepsilon_d}{\varepsilon_m + \varepsilon_d}}\,}$$

This is the **surface plasmon wave-vector**. Q.E.D.

### Step 4 — Physical interpretation

- The SP dispersion curve always lies to the **right** of the light line $k = (\omega/c) n_d$ in the dielectric, meaning a bare photon cannot excite the plasmon.
- As $\omega \to \omega_{sp} = \omega_p / \sqrt{1 + \varepsilon_d}$ (surface plasma frequency), $k_{sp} \to \infty$, and the group velocity $d\omega/dk \to 0$ — the mode is tightly bound.
- For a Drude metal with $\varepsilon_m(\omega) = 1 - \omega_p^2 / \omega^2$, setting $\varepsilon_m + \varepsilon_d = 0$ gives the asymptotic surface plasmon frequency.

## 3.2 Derivation of the SPR Angle Equation

In the Kretschmann configuration, light is incident from a prism of refractive index $n_p$ onto a gold film of thickness $d$ evaporated on the prism base. The matching condition between the in-plane evanescent wave-vector and the SP wave-vector is:

$$k_x^{ev} = k_{sp}$$

Substituting the explicit forms:

$$\frac{\omega}{c} n_p \sin\theta_{SPR} = \frac{\omega}{c}\sqrt{\frac{\varepsilon_m \varepsilon_d}{\varepsilon_m + \varepsilon_d}}$$

The $\omega/c$ factors cancel:

$$n_p \sin\theta_{SPR} = \sqrt{\frac{\varepsilon_m \varepsilon_d}{\varepsilon_m + \varepsilon_d}}$$

Solving for $\theta_{SPR}$:

$$\boxed{\,\theta_{SPR} = \arcsin\!\left[\frac{1}{n_p}\sqrt{\frac{\varepsilon_m \varepsilon_d}{\varepsilon_m + \varepsilon_d}}\right]\,}$$

**Worked example (numerical, KTU style):**

> Given $n_p = 1.515$ (BK7 prism), $\lambda = 633$ nm, gold dielectric function at 633 nm: $\varepsilon_m = -11.0 + 1.3i$, and $\varepsilon_d = (1.33)^2 = 1.7689$ (water). Calculate $\theta_{SPR}$.

Step 1 — Compute the numerator and denominator of the inner square root:
- $\varepsilon_m \varepsilon_d = (-11.0 + 1.3i)(1.7689) = -19.458 + 2.300i$
- $\varepsilon_m + \varepsilon_d = -11.0 + 1.7689 + 1.3i = -9.231 + 1.3i$

Step 2 — Compute the ratio $\dfrac{\varepsilon_m \varepsilon_d}{\varepsilon_m + \varepsilon_d}$:

For a quick estimate, ignore imaginary parts first (lossless approximation):
- $\varepsilon_m \varepsilon_d = -19.458$, $\varepsilon_m + \varepsilon_d = -9.231$
- Ratio $= -19.458 / -9.231 = 2.108$
- $\sqrt{2.108} = 1.452$

Step 3 — Apply the resonance condition:
- $n_p \sin\theta_{SPR} = 1.452$
- $\sin\theta_{SPR} = 1.452 / 1.515 = 0.9584$
- $\theta_{SPR} = \arcsin(0.9584) \approx 73.4°$

This is consistent with experimental values for the Kretschmann Au/water configuration (typically 70–75° for a 633 nm laser). Including the imaginary part of $\varepsilon_m$ broadens and slightly shifts this angle.

## 3.3 Derivation of Binding Kinetics from the Sensorgram

SPR does not directly measure $k_a$ or $k_d$. Instead, it measures the **response R(t)** in resonance units (RU), which is proportional to the mass of analyte bound to the surface. The Langmuir 1:1 binding model is used.

### Step 1 — Rate equation

For a surface-immobilized ligand R binding to a soluble analyte L:

$$\frac{d[R\!L]}{dt} = k_a [L][R] - k_d [R\!L]$$

Since $R + R\!L = R_{total}$ (constant), and $[L]$ is held constant during the association phase:

$$\frac{d[R\!L]}{dt} = k_a [L](R_{total} - [R\!L]) - k_d [R\!L]$$

### Step 2 — Solve the linear ODE

Let $\Gamma(t) = [R\!L](t)$. The equation is linear in $\Gamma$:

$$\frac{d\Gamma}{dt} = k_a [L] R_{total} - (k_a [L] + k_d)\Gamma$$

The solution, with initial condition $\Gamma(0) = 0$, is:

$$\Gamma(t) = \Gamma_{eq}\left(1 - e^{-(k_a [L] + k_d)\,t}\right)$$

where the equilibrium response is:

$$\Gamma_{eq} = \frac{k_a [L] R_{total}}{k_a [L] + k_d} = \frac{R_{max}[L]}{K_D + [L]}$$

with $K_D = k_d / k_a$ and $R_{max} = R_{total}$.

### Step 3 — Observed rate constant

Define the **observed association rate** $k_{obs} = k_a [L] + k_d$. Plotting $k_{obs}$ vs. $[L]$ gives a straight line of slope $k_a$ and intercept $k_d$.

### Step 4 — Dissociation phase

After $t = t_0$, the flow is switched to buffer ($[L] = 0$):

$$\frac{d\Gamma}{dt} = -k_d \Gamma \quad \Longrightarrow \quad \Gamma(t) = \Gamma(t_0)\, e^{-k_d (t - t_0)}$$

A semi-log plot of $\Gamma$ vs. $t-t_0$ gives a straight line of slope $-k_d$.

### Step 5 — Affinity constant

$$K_D = \frac{k_d}{k_a} \quad (\text{units: M})$$

Lower $K_D$ → higher affinity. Typical antibody–antigen $K_D$ values range from $10^{-6}$ to $10^{-12}$ M.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** Modern SPR instruments (e.g., Biacore 8K, Sierra SPR-32) can measure $K_D$ from **picomolar to millimolar** range, with cycle times under 2 minutes. This makes SPR the workhorse for **affinity maturation** in protein engineering campaigns.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Effect of Variables on SPR Signal

| Change at the Sensor Surface | Effect on $n_s$ | Effect on $\theta_{SPR}$ | Effect on $R$ (sensorgram) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Analyte binding (mass $\uparrow$) | $n_s \uparrow$ (locally) | $\theta_{SPR} \uparrow$ (positive shift) | $R \uparrow$ (response $\uparrow$) |
| Analyte dissociation (mass $\downarrow$) | $n_s \downarrow$ | $\theta_{SPR} \downarrow$ | $R \downarrow$ |
| Buffer refractive index increase (bulk shift) | $n_s \uparrow$ everywhere | $\theta_{SPR} \uparrow$ | $R \uparrow$ (reference baseline) |
| Temperature increase | $n_s \downarrow$ (dn/dT negative) | $\theta_{SPR} \downarrow$ | $R \downarrow$ (drift artifact) |
| Gold film thickness increase | evanescent coupling changes | resonance broadens, depth $\downarrow$ | sensitivity $\downarrow$ |

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Kretschmann SPR Instrument

The following Mermaid diagram maps the complete optical, fluidic, and electronic signal path of a typical SPR biosensor. It is a **functional block diagram** showing how each subsystem interacts in real time to produce a sensorgram.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    LS[Polychromatic or Monochromatic Light Source] --> P1[P-Polarizer TM Mode Selection]
    P1 --> PR[Kretschmann Glass Prism with Gold Film 50 nm]
    PR --> DF[Detector Array Photodiode or CCD]
    PR -. Evanescent Wave Couples Through Gold .-> SF[Sample Flow Cell with Surface Immobilized Ligand]
    SF --> FP[Fluidic Delivery System Syringe Pump and Autosampler]
    FP --> SF
    DF --> DAQ[Data Acquisition ADC and Lock-in Amplifier]
    DAQ --> CPU[Signal Processing Unit Finds Theta SPR or Wavelength Shift]
    CPU --> SG[Sensorgram Generator Plot Response RU versus Time]
    CPU --> FIT[Kinetic Fitting Engine 1:1 Langmuir or Bivalent Model]
    FIT --> OUT[Output KD ka kd and Chi Squared Goodness of Fit]
    SG --> DISP[User Display and Export to CSV or Excel]
    FIT --> DISP
```

**Reading the diagram:**

- The **optical arm** (LS → P1 → PR → DF) handles light generation, polarization, evanescent coupling, and detection.
- The **fluidic arm** (FP → SF → FP) delivers running buffer and analyte pulses over the chip.
- The **signal processing chain** (DF → DAQ → CPU → SG/FIT) converts raw reflected intensity vs. angle into a real-time binding curve and extracts kinetic parameters.

## 4.2 Functional Block Diagram of the SPR Sensor Chip Surface

The chip surface is the heart of the measurement. The following block diagram details its layered architecture and binding events:

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph Bulk Solution
        A[Analyte Molecule in Buffer]
    end
    subgraph Sensor Chip Cross Section
        L1[Glass Substrate n equals 1.51 BK7]
        L2[Gold Film 50 nm Evanescent Coupling Layer]
        L3[Linker Layer Thiol SAM or Dextran Matrix]
        L4[Immobilized Ligand Protein R]
        L5[Bound Analyte Protein L Forming R-L Complex]
    end
    Bulk Solution --> A
    A -. Diffusion and Mass Transport .-> L5
    L1 --> L2 --> L3 --> L4 --> L5
```

**Layer-by-layer function:**

| Layer | Material | Role |
|---|---|---|
| Glass substrate | BK7 / SF11 prism | Optical coupling; high $n_p$ |
| Gold film | Au (~$50$ nm) | Supports surface plasmons; chemically inert |
| Linker | Thiol-SAM or CM-dextran hydrogel | Covalent ligand attachment; 3D matrix increases capacity |
| Immobilized ligand | Protein R (e.g., receptor) | Captures analyte; orientation controlled |
| Bound complex | R–L | Generates refractive index change → $\Delta\theta$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why dextran?** A carboxymethylated dextran hydrogel (typical thickness 100–200 nm) is within the evanescent field ($L_p \approx 200$–$300$ nm). It gives high immobilization capacity (~2–10 ng/mm²) and reduces steric hindrance.

## 4.3 Sensorgram Schematic — A 1:1 Binding Cycle

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Association Phase
        A1[Baseline Buffer R equals R0] --> A2[Analyte Injection L Constant]
        A2 --> A3[Exponential Rise to R eq]
    end
    subgraph Dissociation Phase
        A3 --> B1[Buffer Wash L equals 0]
        B1 --> B2[Exponential Decay]
    end
    subgraph Regeneration Phase
        B2 --> C1[Acid or Base Pulse Removes L]
        C1 --> C2[Return to Baseline R0]
    end
    C2 --> A1
```

**Reading the curve:**

- **Association** (A1→A3): Response rises with rate $k_{obs} = k_a[L] + k_d$.
- **Dissociation** (A3→B2): Response falls with rate $k_d$ (first-order).
- **Regeneration** (B2→C2): Surface is stripped of bound analyte, restoring baseline for the next cycle.
