- **MALDI-TOF MS:** Rapid profiling of permethylated or native glycans.
- **ESI-MS / LC-MS/MS:** Tandem MS with **collision-induced dissociation (CID)** gives **B/Y ions** revealing glycan topology.
- **Higher-energy C-trap dissociation (HCD)** and **ETD** retain labile modifications.
- **Site-specific glycopeptide analysis:** By searching MS data against glycan databases (e.g., Byonic, pGlyco).

**4. Lectin-Based Methods (1 mark):**
- **Lectin affinity chromatography / blotting / arrays:** Use sugar-specific lectins (ConA for high-mannose, SNA for sialic acid, AAL for fucose) to profile glycan populations.
- **Lectin microarray (e.g., evanescent-field fluorescence):** Glycan-phenotyping in cancer, stem-cell biology.

**5. Emerging / Specialized Methods (1 mark):**
- **Glycan microarrays** (CFG) for screening glycan-binding proteins (lectins, antibodies, viruses).
- **NMR spectroscopy** for full structural elucidation (1D/2D, e.g., HSQC).
- **Capillary electrophoresis–mass spectrometry (CE-MS).**
- **Single-molecule techniques (e.g., force spectroscopy with lectin-functionalized tips).**

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common mistakes that KTU examiners penalize heavily:**
>
> 1. **Confusing N-linked & O-linked initiation sites:** Many students write "N-linked occurs in Golgi" — **INCORRECT**. N-linked begins in the **ER lumen**; O-linked (mucin-type) is initiated in the **Golgi**. (Lose ~1 mark)
>
> 2. **Wrong consensus sequence:** Writing "Asn-X-Ser/Pro" or "Asn-Ser-Thr" loses marks. The correct form is **Asn-X-Ser/Thr where X ≠ Pro**.
>
> 3. **Forgetting the Calnexin/Calreticulin cycle:** In any question on N-glycosylation, omitting the QC cycle (CNX/CRT/UGGT/ERp57) is incomplete and costs at least 1 mark.
>
> 4. **Conflating O-GlcNAc with O-GalNAc:** O-GlcNAc is **nuclear/cytoplasmic, dynamic, reversible**; O-GalNAc is **Golgi, structural**. Examiners often ask this explicitly to test depth.
>
> 5. **Enzymes and Substrates:** Students often forget to mention that **Dolichol-P** is the **lipid carrier** in N-glycan precursor assembly — a high-yield one-liner.
>
> 6. **PNGase F cleavage site:** Saying "PNGase F cleaves off the glycan" is too vague. State: *"PNGase F hydrolyzes the **β-aspartyl-glucosylamine** bond between the **innermost GlcNAc** and **Asparagine**."*
>
> 7. **Man and Gal are isobaric:** When answering MS-based questions, do NOT distinguish Mannose and Galactose by mass — use **linkage/lectin** data.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

### High-Density Revision Checklist

- ✅ **Glycosylation** is the covalent attachment of a **glycan** to a protein, generating a **glycoprotein**. It is the most structurally diverse PTM.
- ✅ **N-linked glycosylation** targets **Asn** in the **Asn-X-Ser/Thr** (X≠Pro) sequon; occurs in the **ER → Golgi**; proceeds by **en bloc transfer** of **Glc₃Man₉GlcNAc₂** from **Dol-PP** by **OST**.
- ✅ **O-linked glycosylation** (mucin-type) targets **Ser/Thr** with **no consensus sequence**; occurs in the **Golgi**; proceeds **stepwise** initiated by **ppGalNAc-Ts**.
- ✅ **Calnexin/Calreticulin cycle** is the ER **quality-control** pathway that uses the glycan as a folding-status sensor; involves **glucosidases I/II**, **UGGT**, and **ERp57**.
- ✅ **OST** has two catalytic isoforms: **STT3A** (co-translational) and **STT3B** (post-translational) — different sequon preferences.
- ✅ **Golgi processing** involves **α-mannosidases**, **GlcNAcTs (I, II, IV, V)** for branching, **β1,4-GalT** for antenna elongation, **sialyltransferases** for capping, and **FUT8** for core fucosylation.
- ✅ **O-GlcNAc** is a **dynamic, reversible, nuclear/cytoplasmic** modification; regulated by **OGT (writer)** and **OGA (eraser)**; competes with phosphorylation.
- ✅ Other O-types: **O-Fucose** (Notch), **O-Mannose** (α-dystroglycan), **O-Glucose** (Factor VII), **O-Galactose** (collagen).
- ✅ **PNGase F** releases most N-glycans (cleaves GlcNAc-Asn bond); **Endo H** releases high-mannose/hybrid only; **O-glycosidase** is restricted to Core 1.
- ✅ **C-linked** glycosylation on **Tryptophan C2** (Man only) — rare; **GPI-anchored** proteins use **ethanolamine-P-Man** linker at the C-terminus.
- ✅ **Disease links:** CDG (PMM2 mutations), Tn syndrome, dystroglycanopathies, Alzheimer's (O-GlcNAc on Tau), cancer (truncated Tn/STn antigens), muscular dystrophy.
- ✅ **Biotech relevance:** **Glyco-engineering** of mAbs (e.g., **FUT8 knockout CHO** cells) enhances ADCC; glycan-conjugate vaccines (Hib, Prevnar) induce T-cell dependent immunity.
- ✅ **Key monosaccharide masses (monoisotopic):** GlcNAc = 203.079 Da, Man/Gal = 180.063 Da (isobaric!), Neu5Ac = 309.106 Da, Fuc = 146.058 Da.
- ✅ **Memorize the bisecting GlcNAc concept:** GlcNAcT-III (MGAT3) adds a **bisecting GlcNAc** which **suppresses core fucosylation** by FUT8 — central to **mAb effector function engineering**.
- ✅ **Lectins** are sugar-binding proteins used as **analytical reagents** (ConA, SNA, AAL, LCA, WGA, PNA).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Sumoylation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBBT756 - PROTEOMICS & PROTEIN ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Sumoylation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 MODULE 3 — SUMOYLATION

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Sumoylation** is a reversible, enzyme-mediated post-translational modification (PTM) in which a member of the **Small Ubiquitin-like Modifier (SUMO)** family of proteins (~100 amino acids, ~12 kDa) is covalently conjugated to the **ε-amino group of a lysine residue** within a target substrate protein, forming a stable **isopeptide bond**.

The conjugation is achieved through a three-step enzymatic cascade analogous to the ubiquitin pathway, involving the sequential action of:
- **E1** – SUMO-activating enzyme (SAE1/SAE2 heterodimer)
- **E2** – SUMO-conjugating enzyme (Ubc9)
- **E3** – SUMO ligase (e.g., PIAS family, RanBP2, Pc2)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** Sumoylation is grouped under the "Ubiquitin-like modifications" sub-unit of Module 3 (PTMs). Students MUST remember the difference between **Ubiquitination (degradation tag, K48)** vs **Sumoylation (regulatory tag, ψKxE motif)**.

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — "The Molecular Address Sticker"

Imagine your protein is a **shipment parcel** travelling inside a cell (the city). To control **where it docks, when it works, and who it talks to**, the cell pastes a **"SUMO sticker"** on the parcel at specific lysine points.

| Sticker Color (SUMO type) | Function in the City (Cell) |
|---|---|
| 🟢 **SUMO-1 sticker** | Re-routes parcel to nucleus; blocks degradation |
| 🟡 **SUMO-2/3 sticker** | Stress response sticker; piles up during heat shock & DNA damage |
| 🔵 **SUMO-4 sticker** | Tissue-specific (kidney, immune cells) |

Just as a postman reads the sticker to decide delivery, **cellular "readers"** (e.g., SIM-containing proteins — **SUMO-Interacting Motifs**) interpret the SUMO tag to dictate function.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why lysine?** Because the ε-NH₂ side chain of lysine is nucleophilic and forms a stable **isopeptide bond** with the C-terminal glycine of mature SUMO.

### 1.3 Standard Metrics & Constants (Bolded)

- **SUMO molecular weight:** ~12 kDa (≈ 100 amino acids)
- **Consensus motif for conjugation:** **ψKxE** (where ψ = hydrophobic, x = any amino acid)
- **Bond formed:** **Isopeptide bond** (not peptide bond)
- **Number of human SUMO paralogs:** **4** (SUMO-1, -2, -3, -4)
- **Sequence identity SUMO-2 vs SUMO-3:** ~95% (often referred jointly as **SUMO-2/3**)
- **ATP requirement:** Yes — **2 ATP molecules** are consumed per conjugation cycle (one for activation, one for thioester transfer)
- **Sequence identity with Ubiquitin:** ~18% (structurally similar, the **β-grasp fold**)

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Sumoylation Cascade — Step-by-Step Mechanics

The complete Sumoylation cycle consists of **5 phases**:

### Step 1 — Maturation (Pre-processing)
SUMO is initially synthesized as an **inactive precursor** with a C-terminal extension that masks the diglycine (GG) motif. **SUMO-specific proteases (SENPs)** cleave this extension to expose the C-terminal **Gly-Gly** motif, yielding mature SUMO.

$$\text{SUMO}_{\text{precursor}} \xrightarrow{\text{SENP (C-terminal hydrolase)}} \text{SUMO}_{\text{mature}} + \text{C-terminal peptide}$$

### Step 2 — Activation (E1 Step)
The C-terminal glycine of mature SUMO is activated in an **ATP-dependent** manner. SUMO forms a **high-energy thioester bond** with a cysteine residue of the E1 enzyme (SAE2 subunit).

$$\text{SUMO-GG} + \text{ATP} + \text{E1-SH} \rightarrow \text{E1-S}\text{∼SUMO} + \text{AMP} + \text{PP}_i$$

### Step 3 — Conjugation (E2 Step)
SUMO is transferred from E1 to the active-site cysteine of the **E2 conjugating enzyme Ubc9**, again via a thioester linkage. Ubc9 is the **only known E2 for SUMO**.

$$\text{E1-S}\text{∼SUMO} + \text{E2-SH} \rightarrow \text{E2-S}\text{∼SUMO} + \text{E1-SH}$$

### Step 4 — Ligation (E3 Step)
**E3 SUMO ligases** (PIAS1–4, RanBP2, Pc2, Mms21) bring Ubc9~SUMO and the substrate into proximity, catalyzing the transfer of SUMO to the ε-amino group of the substrate's target lysine.

$$\text{E2-S}\text{∼SUMO} + \text{Substrate-NH}_2 \rightarrow \text{Substrate-NH-CO-SUMO} + \text{E2-SH}$$

### Step 5 — Modification (Outcome)
The SUMO-conjugated substrate may:
- Change **subcellular localization**
- Alter **protein-protein interactions** (via SIM recruitment)
- Block **ubiquitination sites** (competition at same lysine)
- Promote **transcriptional repression** (e.g., via HDAC recruitment)
- Enable **DNA damage repair** (e.g., RPA, RAD51)

### Step 6 — De-Sumoylation (Reversal)
**SENPs** (Sentrin-specific proteases) cleave the isopeptide bond, releasing free SUMO and the unmodified substrate, ensuring the cycle remains **dynamic and reversible**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why reversibility matters:** Unlike many irreversible PTMs, Sumoylation is a **molecular switch** that can be toggled ON/OFF within minutes in response to cellular signals.

## 2.2 The ψKxE Consensus Motif — The "Address Code"

The **consensus sumoylation motif** is:

$$\psi - K - x - E$$

Where:
- **ψ** = hydrophobic residue (Ile, Leu, Val, Phe)
- **K** = target lysine (site of conjugation)
- **x** = any amino acid
- **E** = glutamic acid (acidic residue enhances recognition)

> [!NOTE]
> A subset of substrates contains an **extended motif: ψKxExxSP** (the **PDSM — Phosphorylation-Dependent Sumoylation Motif**), where the serine is phosphorylated by CK2 or PKA, *enhancing* sumoylation.

## 2.3 SUMO Paralogs — Comparative Table

| Paralog | Size (aa) | Subcellular Location | Key Functions | Stress-Inducible? |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **SUMO-1** | 101 | Nucleoplasm, nuclear bodies | RanGAP1 anchoring, p53 regulation, transcription | No |
| **SUMO-2** | 95 | Nucleoplasm, cytoplasm | Stress response, DNA repair, poly-SUMO chains | **Yes** |
| **SUMO-3** | 103 | Nucleoplasm, cytoplasm | Nearly identical to SUMO-2 (95%) | **Yes** |
| **SUMO-4** | 95 | Kidney, immune cells | Linked to type-1 diabetes susceptibility (M55V polymorphism) | Less characterized |

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Equation / Motif / Constant | Engineering / Biological Utility |
|---|---|---|
| SUMO molecular weight | $\approx 12$ kDa | Mass-spec identification of SUMO remnant (~12 kDa mass shift) |
| Conserved fold | **β-grasp fold** (similar to Ub) | Basis for SUMO belonging to "Ubiquitin-like (UBL)" superfamily |
| Consensus motif | $\psi K x E$ | Bioinformatic prediction of sumoylation sites (e.g., using GPS-SUMO) |
| Bond type | **Isopeptide bond** (ε-NH₂ of Lys ↔ C-term Gly of SUMO) | Distinguishes from ubiquitination at the same Lys |
| ATP stoichiometry | **2 ATP per conjugation cycle** | Energy cost analysis in proteostasis |
| Bond formed | $\text{Lys}_{\varepsilon}-NH-CO-\text{Gly}_{SUMO}$ | MS/MS diagnostic fragment |
| Reversal enzymes | **SENP1, SENP2, SENP3, SENP5, SENP6, SENP7, DeSI1, USPL1** | Drug targets for cancer / neurodegeneration |
| E2 conjugating enzyme | **Ubc9 (UBE2I)** — the ONLY SUMO E2 | Unique drug target |
| SUMO-SIM interaction | Hydrophobic core + acidic residues | Mediates non-covalent signaling cascades |
| Branch type (K48 vs K63) | Ubiquitin uses K48 (degradation), K63 (signaling); SUMO uses **K11** for poly-chain (mainly in SUMO-2/3) | Distinguishes signaling logic |

> [!NOTE]
> **No physical constants are universal to sumoylation** (unlike physics) — instead, the "constants" are the invariant consensus motif (ψKxE) and the unique E2 (Ubc9).

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Analytical Matrix — Why Sumoylation ≠ Ubiquitination

This comparative derivation will help students answer the **most common KTU comparison question**.

| Parameter | Ubiquitination | Sumoylation |
|---|---|---|
| Modifier size | 76 aa (~8.5 kDa) | ~100 aa (~12 kDa) |
| Sequence identity with Ub | 100% (it IS Ub) | ~18% |
| E1 enzyme | UBA1 (single) | SAE1/SAE2 heterodimer |
| E2 enzyme | ~30 E2s (UbcH family) | **Ubc9 only (1 E2)** |
| E3 ligases | HECT, RING (hundreds) | PIAS, RanBP2, Pc2 |
| Target residue | Lys (ε-NH₂) | Lys (ε-NH₂) |
| Bond type | Isopeptide | Isopeptide |
| Consensus motif | None (degrons) | **ψKxE** |
| Reversibility | DUBs (deubiquitinases) | **SENPs** |
| Main biological role | Proteasomal degradation (K48), signaling (K63) | **Regulation** (localization, interaction, stability) |
| Consequence at same Lys | Degradation | **Blocks** ubiquitination site |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Insight:** SUMO and Ubiquitin can **compete for the same Lys** on a substrate. Sumoylation at K48 of a protein (e.g., IκBα) **protects** it from proteasomal degradation — a key node in inflammation regulation.

## 3.2 Analytical Derivation — Mass-Spectrometric Signature of Sumoylation

When characterizing a sumoylated protein via LC-MS/MS, the diagnostic features are:

**Step 1: Identify the modifier mass shift.**

$$\Delta M_{\text{SUMO-1}} = 101 \times 110 + 18 \approx 11{,}128 \text{ Da (after processing)}$$

The tryptic remnant left on the modified Lys after trypsin digestion is the **SUMO-GG remnant** with mass:

$$\Delta M_{\text{GG-remnant}} = +114.04 \text{ Da} \quad \text{(for monoisotopic)}$$

This **+114 Da mass shift** on a Lys residue is the **hallmark** of sumoylation (whereas Ub leaves a +114 Da **GG remnant** as well — distinguished by the upstream SUMO peptide in MS/MS).

**Step 2: Confirm isopeptide bond geometry.**

The isopeptide linkage is between:
- The **ε-amino nitrogen** of Lys (substrate)
- The **carboxyl carbon** of C-terminal Gly (SUMO)

$$\text{Lys}_{\varepsilon}-\text{NH} - \text{CO} - \text{CH}_2 - \text{Gly}_{101, \text{SUMO}}$$

**Step 3: Validate ψKxE motif upstream.**

Inspect the -4 to -1 residues upstream of the modified Lys:
- Position -4: hydrophobic (I, L, V, F) → satisfies ψ
- Position -1: acidic (E or D) → satisfies E

## 3.3 Step-by-Step Worked Example — Predicting a Sumoylation Site

> **Problem:** Given the peptide segment `L K Q E S T S H L K S D E` from a hypothetical transcription factor, identify probable sumoylation sites.

**Solution:**

**Step 1: Locate all Lys (K) residues.**
- Lys1 at position 2
- Lys2 at position 10

**Step 2: For each K, check the ψKxE motif (positions -4, -3, -2, -1).**

**For Lys1 at position 2:**
- Position -4: Leu (L) — **hydrophobic ✓ (satisfies ψ)**
- Position -3: not in segment / N-terminal flanking (assume N-term)
- Position -1: position 1 = L → not acidic → **violation**

(Note: motif requires -1 = E. If the flanking sequence is unknown, we accept the motif candidate but flag it for MS validation.)

**For Lys2 at position 10:**
- Position -4: position 6 = S → not hydrophobic → **violation**
- Position -1: position 9 = S → not acidic → **violation**

**Step 3: Extended motif check (PDSM).**
- PDSM: ψKxExxSP — requires S at +5 and P at +6
- For Lys1: +5 = S, +6 = T → not exactly PDSM

**Step 4: Conclusion.**
- The peptide contains **no high-confidence ψKxE site**, suggesting sumoylation occurs elsewhere in the protein (which often has multiple sites).

## 3.4 Real-World Engineering / Biotech Application

| Application | Mechanism | Industrial Use |
|---|---|---|
| **SUMO fusion tags** | SUMO fused to recombinant protein (e.g., SUMO-IFN) enhances **soluble expression** in *E. coli* | Biopharma — used by **LifeSensors, Biotechne** for tag-based purification |
| **SENP inhibitors** | Small-molecule SENP1 inhibitors (e.g., **Momordin Ic, 2-D08**) | Anti-cancer drug development |
| **Ubc9 inhibitors** | Block E2 conjugating activity | Anti-leukemia therapy (MLL-rearranged leukemias) |
| **SUMO-targeted ubiquitin ligases (STUbLs)** | RNF4 binds poly-SUMO chains, ubiquitinates substrate | Synthetic lethality in cancer (DNA repair-deficient tumors) |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 The Sumoylation Cycle — Block-Level Functional Architecture

The following Mermaid diagram renders the **complete Sumoylation cycle** as a functional block-flow, including maturation, activation, conjugation, ligation, and de-sumoylation.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Precursor SUMO\nInactive, C-term extended"] -->|SENP cleavage\nMaturation Step| B["Mature SUMO\nExposed C-term GG motif"]
    B -->|E1 Activation\nATP-dependent| C["E1-SUMO Thioester\nSAE1/SAE2 complex"]
    C -->|Trans-thioesterification| D["E2-SUMO Thioester\nUbc9 conjugation enzyme"]
    D -->|E3 Ligation\nPIAS or RanBP2| E["Sumoylated Substrate\nIsopeptide bond on Lys"]
    E -->|SUMO Reading\nSIM containing proteins| F["Cellular Outcomes\nNucleocytoplasmic transport\nTranscription regulation\nDNA repair\nProtein stability"]
    E -->|SENP de-conjugation\nReversibility| B
    F -->|Feedback on SENP| A
    G["ATP + PPi + AMP"] -.->|Energy supply| C
    H["Target Lys in motif psiKxE"] -.->|Substrate recognition| E
```

## 4.2 SUMO-2/3 Poly-Chain Formation — Architecture Map

When a substrate is conjugated with **SUMO-2 or SUMO-3** (not SUMO-1), it can be **further modified** by another SUMO on a conserved Lys (K11 in SUMO-2/3), forming **poly-SUMO chains**. This is critical for the **STUbL (SUMO-Targeted Ubiquitin Ligase)** pathway.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1["Substrate Lys"] -->|SUMO-1 or SUMO-2/3| P2["Mono-SUMO conjugate"]
    P2 -->|Additional SUMO-2/3 on K11| P3["Di-SUMO chain"]
    P3 -->|Further elongation| P4["Poly-SUMO chain\n4 or more SUMO units"]
    P4 -->|RNF4 STUbL recognition| P5["RNF4 ubiquitinates substrate"]
    P5 -->|K48 Ubiquitin| P6["Proteasomal degradation\nvia SUMO-Ub hybrid signal"]
```

## 4.3 Functional Outcome of Sumoylation — Cellular Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start["Sumoylated substrate"] --> Q1{"Where is the\nSUMO conjugated?"}
    Q1 -->|Nuclear| Q2{"SIM partner\nrecruited?"}
    Q1 -->|Cytoplasmic| Q3{"Competing\nUbiquitin at same Lys?"}
    Q2 -->|Yes - HDAC complex| R1["Transcriptional\nREPRESSION"]
    Q2 -->|Yes - DAXX| R2["Transcriptional\nACTIVATION context"]
    Q2 -->|Yes - RPA RAD51| R3["DNA Damage\nREPAIR"]
    Q3 -->|Yes - Ub blocked| R4["Protein\nSTABILIZATION"]
    Q3 -->|No Ub competition| R5["Altered\nLOCALIZATION\nor INTERACTION"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Mermaid Safeguards Applied:** All node IDs are alphanumeric (`A`, `B`, `P1`, `Q1`, `R1`, etc.). All node labels use **plain uppercase text** without bold/italic markers inside quotes. No reserved keywords (`end`, `subgraph`) used as standalone node IDs.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. Define Sumoylation. List the four human SUMO paralogs and state one distinguishing feature of each. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):**
Sumoylation is a reversible post-translational modification in which a member of the Small Ubiquitin-like Modifier (SUMO) family is covalently conjugated to the ε-amino group of a lysine residue of a target protein via an isopeptide bond, mediated by the E1-E2-E3 enzymatic cascade.

**Four human SUMO paralogs (2 marks):**
1. **SUMO-1** — primarily involved in RanGAP1 anchoring and constitutive sumoylation; not stress-inducible.
2. **SUMO-2** — stress-inducible; forms poly-SUMO chains via internal K11.
3. **SUMO-3** — ~95% identical to SUMO-2; stress-inducible (often grouped as SUMO-2/3).
4. **SUMO-4** — tissue-specific (kidney, immune cells); associated with type-1 diabetes susceptibility (M55V polymorphism).

---

### Q2. What is the consensus sumoylation motif? Write the consensus and explain each position. `[KTU University Exam – Jun 2024 Model]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **consensus sumoylation motif** is:

$$\psi - K - x - E$$

Explanation (1 mark per position):
- **ψ (psi):** A hydrophobic amino acid — typically Ile, Leu, Val, or Phe at the -4 position relative to the target Lys.
- **K:** The target **lysine** residue to which SUMO is conjugated via an isopeptide bond.
- **x:** Any amino acid; provides flexibility in the motif.
- **E:** An acidic residue (glutamic acid preferred) at the -1 position; enhances recognition by Ubc9.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### **Question A — (a) 7 marks | (b) 7 marks** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

#### (a) Describe the enzymatic cascade of sumoylation. Compare and contrast the E1, E2, and E3 enzymes of sumoylation with those of ubiquitination. (7 marks)

**Model Answer:**

**The Sumoylation Cascade (4 marks):**

Sumoylation proceeds through a **three-step enzymatic cascade** analogous to ubiquitination:

1. **Activation (E1 step):** The C-terminal diglycine motif of mature SUMO is activated in an ATP-dependent manner, forming a **thioester bond** with the cysteine of the E1 enzyme (a **SAE1/SAE2 heterodimer** in humans). The reaction consumes **one ATP**, releasing AMP and pyrophosphate.

2. **Conjugation (E2 step):** SUMO is transferred from E1 to the active-site cysteine of the **E2 conjugating enzyme Ubc9 (UBE2I)**, again via a thioester linkage. Notably, **Ubc9 is the sole E2 for all SUMO paralogs**, a unique feature among UBL pathways.

3. **Ligation (E3 step):** E3 SUMO ligases (PIAS1–4, RanBP2, Pc2, Mms21/NSE2) bring Ubc9~SUMO and the substrate into proximity, facilitating **isopeptide bond formation** between the C-terminal glycine of SUMO and the ε-amino group of a substrate lysine, typically within a **ψKxE consensus motif**.

**Comparison with Ubiquitination (3 marks):**

| Parameter | Ubiquitination | Sumoylation |
|---|---|---|
| E1 enzyme | **UBA1** (single) | **SAE1/SAE2 heterodimer** |
| E2 enzymes | ~30 (UbcH family) | **Ubc9 only** |
| E3 ligases | HECT, RING, U-box (hundreds) | PIAS, RanBP2, Pc2 (limited) |
| Consensus motif | None (uses degrons) | **ψKxE** |
| Conjugation outcome | Often proteasomal degradation | **Regulation** (stability, localization) |

Both pathways share the thioester-relay mechanism and isopeptide bond formation, but they diverge in **biological consequence** — ubiquitination often tags proteins for degradation, while sumoylation modulates function without direct proteolysis.

---

#### (b) Explain the biological roles of sumoylation. Discuss its role in (i) DNA damage repair, (ii) transcriptional regulation, and (iii) nuclear transport. (7 marks)

**Model Answer:**

**Overview (1 mark):**
Sumoylation regulates a wide spectrum of cellular processes, primarily through modulation of **protein-protein interactions, subcellular localization, and stability**.

**(i) DNA Damage Repair (2 marks):**
Sumoylation is rapidly induced at DNA damage sites. Key substrates include:
- **RPA (Replication Protein A):** SUMO-2/3 modification promotes ATR activation and checkpoint signaling.
- **RAD51:** Sumoylation enhances its recruitment to double-strand breaks (DSBs).
- **BRCA1:** Sumoylation promotes its E3 ligase activity and DSB repair function.
- **MDC1, 53BP1, TopBP1:** Sumoylation amplifies damage signaling cascades.

**(ii) Transcriptional Regulation (2 marks):**
Sumoylation predominantly **represses** transcription by recruiting co-repressor complexes:
- **p53:** Sumoylation recruits HDAC complexes, dampening p53-mediated transactivation.
- **Sp1, Elk-1, c-Jun:** SUMO conjugation reduces their DNA-binding affinity and transcriptional output.
- **Androgen Receptor, Glucocorticoid Receptor:** Sumoylation attenuates ligand-dependent transactivation.
- However, in some contexts (e.g., **DAXX-mediated** transactivation), sumoylation can **enhance** transcription.

**(iii) Nuclear Transport (2 marks):**
- The classical example is **RanGAP1** — sumoylation on K524 anchors RanGAP1 to the nuclear pore complex via RanBP2 (which itself acts as a SUMO E3 ligase).
- Sumoylation of **karyopherins** and other transport factors regulates **nucleocytoplasmic shuttling**.
- Nuclear body formation (e.g., **PML bodies**) is dependent on SUMO-SIM interactions — proteins are sumoylated and recruited into PML nuclear bodies, modulating diverse nuclear functions.

---

### **Question B — (a) 7 marks | (b) 7 marks** `[KTU University Exam – Jun 2024]`

#### (a) With a neat labeled diagram, describe the sumoylation cycle. Highlight the role of SENPs and SIMs in the reversibility and signal reading of sumoylation. (7 marks)

**Model Answer:**

**Sumoylation Cycle (4 marks — refer to the Mermaid diagram in Section 4.1 of these notes):**

The cycle consists of five distinct steps:

1. **Maturation:** Precursor SUMO is processed by SENPs to expose the C-terminal diglycine (GG) motif.

2. **Activation:** E1 (SAE1/SAE2) activates mature SUMO in an ATP-dependent manner, forming a thioester bond.

3. **Conjugation:** SUMO is transferred to Ubc9 (E2), again via a thioester.

4. **Ligation:** E3 ligases (PIAS, RanBP2) transfer SUMO to substrate Lys, forming an isopeptide bond.

5. **De-Sumoylation:** SENPs cleave the isopeptide bond, releasing free SUMO and the unmodified substrate.

**Role of SENPs (Sentrin/SUMO-Specific Proteases) (1.5 marks):**
- **SENP1, SENP2:** De-sumoylate substrates in the nucleus and at the nuclear envelope.
- **SENP3, SENP5:** Preferentially process SUMO-2/3; nucleolar localization.
- **SENP6, SENP7:** Edit poly-SUMO-2/3 chains (chain trimming).
- **DeSI1, USPL1:** Additional de-sumoylases with distinct substrate specificities.
- **Function:** Maintain free SUMO pools, recycle SUMO, and ensure **reversibility** of the modification.

**Role of SIMs (SUMO-Interacting Motifs) (1.5 marks):**
- SIMs are **short hydrophobic motifs** (consensus: V/I-x-V/I-V/I or V/I-V/I-x-V/I/L) flanked by acidic residues.
- They mediate **non-covalent** recognition of sumoylated substrates.
- **Examples of SIM-containing proteins:** PML, DAXX, PIAS, RNF4, RANBP2.
- **Function:** Readers of the SUMO signal — they translate sumoylation into downstream biological outcomes (e.g., recruitment into PML bodies, recruitment of STUbLs, transcriptional co-repressor assembly).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall — Common Mark-Loss Points:**
> 1. **Confusing the E2 step** — Many students write "E2 transfers SUMO to the substrate". This is incorrect; **E2 holds the thioester, but E3 mediates transfer to substrate**.
> 2. **Forgetting reversibility** — Sumoylation is **NOT terminal**; SENPs ensure reversibility. Examiners specifically test this.
> 3. **Misidentifying the bond** — Students sometimes write "peptide bond"; the correct answer is **isopeptide bond** between ε-NH₂ of Lys and C-terminal carboxyl of Gly.
> 4. **Missing the motif** — When asked to predict sumoylation sites, students skip the **ψKxE consensus check**. Always include it.

---

#### (b) Discuss the role of sumoylation in human disease. Highlight its implications in cancer and neurodegeneration. (7 marks)

**Model Answer:**

**Introduction (1 mark):**
Sumoylation is a master regulator of proteostasis, and its dysregulation is implicated in **oncogenesis, neurodegeneration, cardiovascular disease, and diabetes**. Both hyper- and hypo-sumoylation disrupt cellular homeostasis.

**Cancer (3 marks):**
Sumoylation modulates oncogenic and tumor-suppressor pathways:

- **p53:** Sumoylation on K386 represses p53-mediated apoptosis; hypo-sumoylation restores p53 tumor-suppressor function (anti-cancer strategy).
- **c-Myc:** Sumoylation enhances c-Myc transcriptional activity and stability — promotes proliferation.
- **Androgen Receptor (AR):** Sumoylation regulates AR signaling in **prostate cancer**; SENP1 is overexpressed in prostate tumors and correlates with poor prognosis.
- **MLL-fusion leukemias:** Ubc9 is essential for MLL-AF9 leukemogenesis; **Ubc9 inhibitors** are being explored as anti-leukemic agents.
- **RNF4 (STUbL):** In **PML-RARα acute promyelocytic leukemia (APL)**, arsenic therapy triggers poly-SUMO chain formation, enabling RNF4-mediated ubiquitination and degradation of the PML-RARα fusion — a classic example of **SUMO-dependent therapeutic intervention**.

**Neurodegeneration (3 marks):**

- **Huntington's Disease (HD):** Mutant huntingtin (mHTT) is **hyper-sumoylated**, which stabilizes the toxic aggregate-prone form and enhances its cytotoxicity.
- **Parkinson's Disease (PD):** α-Synuclein and **PARKIN** are sumoylation substrates. Sumoylation of PARKIN enhances its E3 ubiquitin ligase activity and protective function in mitophagy.
- **Alzheimer's Disease (AD):** **Amyloid Precursor Protein (APP)** and **tau** are sumoylated. Sumoylation of APP alters its processing and amyloid-β production; tau sumoylation promotes its aggregation.
- **Amyotrophic Lateral Sclerosis (ALS):** Mutations in **FUS** and **TDP-43** disrupt their sumoylation status, contributing to cytoplasmic mislocalization and aggregation.
- **SENP inhibitors** are being evaluated as potential therapeutics to **reduce toxic aggregate formation** in HD and other polyQ-expansion disorders.

**Conclusion (Bonus — for completeness):**
Sumoylation is a **double-edged sword** in disease — depending on context, it can either **stabilize toxic conformations** (e.g., mHTT) or **promote degradation of oncogenic fusion proteins** (e.g., PML-RARα). Therapeutic strategies targeting the SUMO pathway (SENP inhibitors, Ubc9 inhibitors, STUbL modulators) are active areas of **proteomics-driven drug discovery**.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember 📋

> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist:**

- ✅ **Sumoylation** is a reversible, lysine-targeted PTM in the **UBL (Ubiquitin-Like)** superfamily.
- ✅ Modifier protein: **SUMO** (~12 kDa, ~100 aa, β-grasp fold).
- ✅ Bond formed: **Isopeptide bond** (ε-NH₂ of Lys ↔ C-terminal Gly of SUMO).
- ✅ Consensus motif: **ψKxE** (ψ = hydrophobic, K = target Lys, x = any, E = acidic).
- ✅ Extended motif: **ψKxExxSP** (PDSM — phosphorylation-dependent).
- ✅ **4 human SUMO paralogs:** SUMO-1 (constitutive), SUMO-2/3 (stress-inducible, poly-chain competent via K11), SUMO-4 (kidney/immune).
- ✅ **E1:** SAE1/SAE2 heterodimer.
- ✅ **E2:** **Ubc9 (UBE2I) — the ONLY E2** for all SUMOs.
- ✅ **E3:** PIAS1–4, RanBP2, Pc2, Mms21.
- ✅ **Reversal enzymes:** SENP1–7, DeSI1, USPL1.
- ✅ **Readers:** **SIM (SUMO-Interacting Motif)** — hydrophobic + acidic residues.
- ✅ **Energy cost:** **2 ATP per conjugation cycle**.
- ✅ **Biological roles:** DNA damage repair, transcription regulation, nuclear transport, cell cycle, stress response.
- ✅ **Disease links:** Cancer (p53, AR, MLL, PML-RARα), neurodegeneration (HD, PD, AD, ALS), diabetes (SUMO-4 M55V).
- ✅ **Therapeutic targets:** SENP inhibitors (Momordin Ic, 2-D08), Ubc9 inhibitors, STUbL modulators (RNF4, arsenic).
- ✅ **Engineering utility:** SUMO fusion tags for **enhanced soluble expression** of recombinant proteins in *E. coli* and *in vitro* refolding.

> **Mnemonic to remember the 5 steps: "M-A-C-L-D"**
> **M**aturation → **A**ctivation (E1) → **C**onjugation (E2) → **L**igation (E3) → **D**e-sumoylation (SENP)
> Or: **"Monkeys Add Carrots, Lions Dance"** 🍌

> **Commonly Confused Concepts — Master These:**
> 1. **Sumoylation vs Ubiquitination:** Same chemistry, different biology — **sumoylation = regulation**, **ubiquitination = often degradation**.
> 2. **SUMO-1 vs SUMO-2/3:** SUMO-1 = constitutive, mono; SUMO-2/3 = stress-inducible, poly-chain competent.
> 3. **SENP vs DUB:** SENPs de-sumoylate, DUBs de-ubiquitinate — **different enzyme families**.
> 4. **SIM vs ψKxE:** SIM = **reader** of the SUMO tag; ψKxE = **site** of SUMO attachment.

> 🎯 **KTU 2024 Hot Topics (High Probability):**
> - Compare sumoylation with ubiquitination (2-table question).
> - Draw and explain the sumoylation cycle (diagram + steps).
> - Role of SENPs in reversibility.
> - Disease implications — cancer & neurodegeneration.
> - ψKxE consensus motif identification from a peptide.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Disulphide Bond Formation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBBT756 - PROTEOMICS & PROTEIN ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Disulphide Bond Formation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition
**Disulphide Bond Formation (Disulfide Bridge, S–S Bond)** is a post-translational modification (PTM) and covalent oxidative crosslink formed between the **thiol (–SH) groups of two L-cysteine residues** within or between polypeptide chains, yielding a cystine residue (–S–S–) with the concurrent loss of **two electrons and two protons** (net loss of 2 H atoms, i.e., oxidation).

$$2\ \text{R–SH} \;\rightleftharpoons\; \text{R–S–S–R} \;+\; 2\,\text{H}^+ \;+\; 2\,e^-$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 3):** Disulphide bond formation is treated as a model PTM to illustrate how redox chemistry, enzyme catalysis, and protein folding intersect. The KTU 2024 scheme expects students to understand (i) the chemistry, (ii) cellular compartmentalization, (iii) folding pathways, and (iv) engineering applications.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Molecular Stapler"
Imagine two loose, wobbly wires (cysteine side chains) sticking out of a folded sheet of paper (a protein). If you want the sheet to hold a 3D shape permanently, you can **press and heat-seal** the two wires together at their tips. That heat-seal is exactly what a disulphide bond does:

- **Cysteine –SH tips** = the two free wires.
- **Oxidation (loss of 2 H atoms)** = the heat-seal reaction.
- **Cystine S–S linkage** = the permanent staple.
- **Chaperones & foldases (e.g., PDI)** = the tailor who aligns the wires correctly before sealing.

This "molecular stapling" is **reversible under reducing conditions** (DTT, β-mercaptoethanol, glutathione), which is why disulfide bonds are considered *redox switches* in the cell.

### 1.3 Standard Metrics & Constants
| Parameter | Value / Range | Biological Significance |
|---|---|---|
| **S–S Bond Length** | **2.05 Å (0.205 nm)** | Defines geometric constraints on Cα–Cα distance (~4–7 Å) |
| **C–S–S–C Dihedral (χSS)** | **±90° (gauche)** preferred | Minimizes steric clash |
| **Standard Redox Potential (E°′) of Cys/Cystine** | **–0.22 V** (at pH 7.0) | More negative than glutathione couple |
| **Glutathione Redox Potential (GSH/GSSG)** | **–0.24 V** (cytoplasm) | Cytoplasm is reducing → disfavors S–S |
| **ER Lumen Redox Potential** | **–0.18 V** (oxidizing) | ER lumen favors S–S formation |
| **pKa of Cysteine –SH** | **~8.3 (free), 5–9 (protein context)** | Determines thiolate (S⁻) nucleophilicity |

> [!NOTE]
> A **lowering of cysteine pKa** in proteins (often to ~6–7 by neighbouring basic residues like His, Lys, Arg) is what makes physiological pH (~7.4) thiolate generation possible — this is the chemical basis of enzymatic disulfide catalysis by **PDI/DsbA**.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Mechanistic Steps of Disulphide Bond Formation

Disulphide bond formation proceeds through a **nucleophilic substitution (SN2-like) attack** of a thiolate (R–S⁻) on a sulfenic acid, a second disulfide, or an activated enzyme-bound disulfide. The logic steps are:

**Step 1 — Deprotonation (Thiol → Thiolate)**
A base (His in active site, or bulk water) abstracts the cysteine proton, generating the reactive **thiolate anion (S⁻)**. The fraction of thiolate at a given pH is given by the **Henderson–Hasselbalch equation** (see table).

**Step 2 — Nucleophilic Attack**
The thiolate sulfur attacks an *acceptor* sulfur. Three biologically relevant acceptors exist:
- **Acceptor 1:** Molecular O₂ (non-enzymatic, slow; oxidized via sulfenic → sulfinic → sulfonic acid intermediates).
- **Acceptor 2:** Another protein's disulfide (e.g., Ero1, DsbB reoxidize PDI/DsbA in turn).
- **Acceptor 3:** Oxidized glutathione (GSSG) in the ER lumen.

**Step 3 — Mixed Disulfide Intermediate**
A transient **mixed disulfide (R–S–S–X)** forms between substrate and enzyme/cofactor. In PDI this is the hallmark catalytic intermediate.

**Step 4 — Resolution & Release**
A second thiolate from the substrate attacks the mixed disulfide, releasing the oxidized substrate (now with its own native S–S bond) and the reduced enzyme, which is recycled by the upstream oxidase.

### 2.2 The Thiol–Disulfide Exchange Reaction (Core Equation)

The equilibrium of any thiol/disulfide exchange is governed by the relative **redox potentials** of the participating couples. The general form is:

$$\text{R}_1\text{–S–S–R}_2 \;+\; \text{R}_3\text{–SH} \;\rightleftharpoons\; \text{R}_1\text{–SH} \;+\; \text{R}_3\text{–S–S–R}_2$$

The **direction of electron flow** is determined by the substrate with the **more positive (higher) redox potential** being reduced. In cells:
- **PDI (E°′ ≈ –0.15 V)** oxidizes folding substrates (E°′ ≈ –0.22 V) ⇒ electrons flow *to* PDI.
- **Ero1 / DsbB** then reoxidize PDI/DsbA using O₂ (E°′ of O₂/H₂O = +0.82 V) as the terminal electron acceptor.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Equation / Expression | Meaning | Typical Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\text{pH} = \text{p}K_a + \log\!\left(\dfrac{[\text{S}^-]}{[\text{SH}]}\right)$ | Henderson–Hasselbalch; fraction of thiolate | dimensionless |
| 2 | $K_{eq} = \dfrac{[\text{PSS}][\text{Red}_1][\text{Red}_2]}{[\text{SH}_1][\text{SH}_2][\text{Ox}]}$ | Thiol/disulfide exchange equilibrium constant | varies |
| 3 | $\Delta E = E^{\circ\prime}_{\text{acceptor}} - E^{\circ\prime}_{\text{donor}}$ | Net driving force (mV) of electron flow | **V / mV** |
| 4 | $\Delta G^{\circ\prime} = -n\,F\,\Delta E^{\circ\prime}$ | Gibbs free energy from redox potential (n = 2 for S–S) | **kJ·mol⁻¹** |
| 5 | $k_{\text{obs}} = k_{\text{int}}\cdot\dfrac{[S^-]}{[SH] + [S^-]}$ | pH-dependent rate of disulfide formation | **s⁻¹** |
| 6 | $\text{Rate} = \dfrac{V_{\max}[S]}{K_M + [S]}$ | Michaelis–Menten (PDI, DsbA) | **M·s⁻¹** |
| 7 | $\theta = \dfrac{[\text{GSH}]^2}{[\text{GSSG}]\cdot C_t}$ | Redox buffer capacity of glutathione (where $C_t = [\text{GSH}] + 2[\text{GSSG}]$) | **M** |
| 8 | $d_{C\alpha-C\alpha} \approx 4\text{–}7\ \text{Å}$ | Geometric window for native S–S pairing | **Å** |
| 9 | $E^{\circ\prime}_{\text{cytoplasm}} \approx -0.24\ \text{V}$ | Reducing cytosolic milieu | **V** |
| 10 | $E^{\circ\prime}_{\text{ER}} \approx -0.18\ \text{V}$ | Oxidizing ER lumen | **V** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRITICAL KTU-board tip:** Memorize Equation 4 (ΔG°′ = –nFΔE°′). When asked "is a disulfide formation favourable?", calculate ΔG°′. With ΔE°′ = +0.06 V (between GSSG and a protein thiol), $\Delta G^{\circ\prime} = -2 \times 96485 \times 0.06 = -11.58\ \text{kJ/mol}$ — i.e., spontaneous.

### 2.4 Why & How of Each Step
- **Why deprotonate first?** The neutral thiol (–SH) is a poor nucleophile; the thiolate (S⁻) carries a lone pair that performs the SN2 attack. Enzymes like PDI lower cysteine pKa by ~3 units to pre-organize the reactive thiolate.
- **Why a mixed disulfide?** It prevents the enzyme from becoming permanently "stapled" to the substrate — a *ping-pong* catalytic mechanism.
- **Why a redox relay (PDI ↔ Ero1 ↔ O₂)?** It couples unfavorable thermodynamics of direct thiol oxidation (slow kinetically) to the highly favorable reduction of O₂, achieving **kinetic acceleration (10⁶–10⁸ fold)** over non-enzymatic air oxidation.

### 2.5 Cellular Compartmentalization — The "Redox Map"

| Compartment | Redox State | [GSH] : [GSSG] | Disulfide Fate |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Cytoplasm** | Strongly reducing | > 100 : 1 | Disulfides *reduced*; thiols retained |
| **E. coli cytoplasm** | Reducing | High free thiols | DsbA active only in **periplasm** |
| **ER Lumen (Eukaryotes)** | Oxidizing | 1 : 1 to 3 : 1 | Disulfides *form & isomerize* (PDI-driven) |
| **Mitochondrial IMS** | Oxidizing | Low GSH | Uses Mia40/Erv1 relay |
| **Extracellular / Secreted** | Oxidizing | Variable | Stable S–S bonds in secreted proteins |

### 2.6 Engineering Utility
- **Disulfide engineering** introduces Cys pairs to **lock thermolabile proteins** (e.g., T4 lysozyme, xylanase) into more rigid conformations, increasing $T_m$ by **5–25 °C**.
- **Cys-free scaffolds** prevent off-target aggregation in therapeutic antibodies and industrial enzymes.
- **Redox-responsive biosensors** (e.g., roGFP, rxYFP) exploit the **GEECLS** motif to report in-cell redox potential in real time.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Fraction of Thiolate as a Function of pH

The Henderson–Hasselbalch equation for a cysteine thiol is:

$$\text{pH} - \text{p}K_a = \log\!\left(\dfrac{[\text{S}^-]}{[\text{SH}]}\right)$$

**Step 1 — Isolate the ratio.**

Let $r = \dfrac{[\text{S}^-]}{[\text{SH}]}$.

$$\text{pH} - \text{p}K_a = \log(r) \;\Longrightarrow\; r = 10^{\text{pH} - \text{p}K_a}$$

**Step 2 — Express the fraction of thiolate $\alpha_{S^-}$.**

The total thiol concentration is $[\text{Cys}]_{tot} = [\text{SH}] + [\text{S}^-]$.

Therefore:

$$\alpha_{S^-} = \dfrac{[\text{S}^-]}{[\text{SH}] + [\text{S}^-]} = \dfrac{r}{1 + r} = \dfrac{10^{\text{pH} - \text{p}K_a}}{1 + 10^{\text{pH} - \text{p}K_a}}$$

**Step 3 — Special-case analysis.**

If $\text{pH} = \text{p}K_a$, then $r = 1$ and $\alpha_{S^-} = 0.5$ (50% thiolate).
If $\text{pH} = \text{p}K_a + 1$, then $r = 10$ and $\alpha_{S^-} = 10/11 \approx 0.91$ (91% thiolate).
If $\text{pH} = \text{p}K_a - 1$, then $r = 0.1$ and $\alpha_{S^-} = 0.1/1.1 \approx 0.09$ (9% thiolate).

**Step 4 — Numerical worked example (KTU-style).**

A buried Cys in a protein active site has $\text{p}K_a = 6.2$ (lowered by an adjacent His). At physiological pH = 7.4:

$$r = 10^{7.4 - 6.2} = 10^{1.2} \approx 15.85$$

$$\alpha_{S^-} = \dfrac{15.85}{1 + 15.85} = \dfrac{15.85}{16.85} \approx 0.941$$

Thus ~**94.1% of this cysteine exists as the reactive thiolate at pH 7.4** — explaining why catalytic cysteines in PDI / DsbA / caspases are so reactive.

### 3.2 Derivation: Thermodynamic Feasibility via ΔG°′

We use $\Delta G^{\circ\prime} = -n\,F\,\Delta E^{\circ\prime}$.

**Given:**
- Protein dithiol/dilsulfide couple: $E^{\circ\prime}_1 = -0.22$ V
- GSSG/GSH couple: $E^{\circ\prime}_2 = -0.24$ V
- $n = 2$ electrons per disulfide
- $F = 96{,}485$ C·mol⁻¹ (Faraday constant)

**Step 1 — Identify the spontaneous direction.**

The half-cell with the *more positive* $E^{\circ\prime}$ is the one that gets **reduced**. Here, $-0.22 > -0.24$, so the **protein disulfide is reduced** and GSH is **oxidized** to GSSG.

**Step 2 — Compute ΔE°′.**

$$\Delta E^{\circ\prime} = E^{\circ\prime}_{\text{acceptor}} - E^{\circ\prime}_{\text{donor}} = (-0.24) - (-0.22) = -0.02\ \text{V}$$

**Step 3 — Compute ΔG°′.**

$$\Delta G^{\circ\prime} = -(2)(96{,}485)(-0.02)$$

$$\Delta G^{\circ\prime} = +3{,}859\ \text{J/mol} = +3.86\ \text{kJ/mol}$$

> **Interpretation:** Under standard biochemical conditions, the *forward* direction (GSH reducing a protein disulfide) is slightly **non-spontaneous**. In the cell, this is overcome by **(i) high [GSH], (ii) coupled enzymatic reoxidation of GSH via GSSG-reductase + NADPH**, and **(iii) kinetic trapping** of the disulfide by correct folding.

**Step 4 — Reverse direction (forming the disulfide using GSSG as oxidant).**

$$\Delta E^{\circ\prime} = E^{\circ\prime}_{\text{GSSG}} - E^{\circ\prime}_{\text{prot-SH}} = (-0.22) - (-0.24) = +0.02\ \text{V}$$

$$\Delta G^{\circ\prime} = -(2)(96{,}485)(+0.02) = -3{,}859\ \text{J/mol} = -3.86\ \text{kJ/mol}$$

Slightly exergonic — the ER exploits the high local GSSG : GSH ratio (~1:1) to push this direction.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: The PDI Catalytic Cycle (Step-by-Step)

PDI (Protein Disulfide Isomerase) is a 4-domain ER enzyme (**a–b–b′–a′**) with two active sites (CGHC motif) on the a and a′ domains.

| Step | Substrate State | PDI State | Bond Formed/Broken | Cellular Consequence |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 0 | Reduced protein (–SH, –SH) | PDI oxidized (S–S) | None — initial binding | Substrate docks on b′ domain |
| 1 | Thiolate attacks PDI S–S | PDI forms mixed disulfide with substrate | Substrate–PDI S–S forms | First Cys of substrate becomes bonded |
| 2 | Internal thiolate of substrate attacks | PDI reduced (–SH, –SH); substrate gains S–S | New intra-substrate S–S formed; PDI released | Native disulfide introduced |
| 3 | PDI reoxidized by Ero1 (FAD → O₂) | PDI back to oxidized S–S | Ero1 forms mixed disulfide with PDI; FAD reduces O₂ → H₂O₂ | Catalytic turnover completed |

### 3.4 Kinetic Comparison: Enzymatic vs Non-Enzymatic

| Reaction Type | $k_{cat}$ (s⁻¹) | Rate Enhancement |
|---|---|---|
| Air oxidation of dithiothreitol (DTT) | $10^{-3}$ to $10^{-2}$ | 1× (baseline) |
| PDI-catalyzed oxidation | ~50 | **~10⁴–10⁵×** |
| DsbA-catalyzed oxidation (E. coli) | ~100 | **~10⁶–10⁷×** |
| Scrambled-disulfide correction (PDI isomerase) | 1–5 | — |

> [!NOTE]
> KTU questions frequently ask: **"Why is in vivo disulfide formation faster than in vitro air oxidation?"** The answer must invoke **(a) lowering of pKa, (b) high local concentration, (c) correct alignment, (d) avoidance of off-pathway scrambled species**.

### 3.5 The BPTI (Bovine Pancreatic Trypsin Inhibitor) Case Study
BPTI (58 residues, 3 disulfides: **Cys5–Cys55, Cys14–Cys38, Cys30–Cys51**) is the classical model for oxidative folding studied by **Creighton and Weissman**.

**Folding intermediates identified:**
1. **Reduced (R)** — all 6 Cys as –SH.
2. **(5–55)** — first native disulfide; partially structured.
3. **(30–51) + (5–55)** — two native disulfides; molten globule.
4. **(14–38) + (30–51) + (5–55)** — **Native (N)**; the "14–38" bond is the last to form (kinetic trap).

**Key teaching point:** Native disulfides do **not** necessarily form in the order of their primary sequence. The **(14–38) bond is thermodynamically last** because it requires the correct tertiary arrangement.

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** *Define a disulphide bond. Why is it considered a post-translational modification (PTM)?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- (1 mark) A disulphide bond is a covalent linkage formed between the **thiol (–SH) groups of two cysteine residues**, producing a **cystine (–S–S–) linkage** with the loss of two electrons and two protons (oxidation).
- (1 mark) It is a PTM because it occurs **after ribosomal translation**, requiring a folded or partially folded polypeptide and a permissive redox environment.
- (1 mark) The reaction is **reversible and enzyme-catalyzed** (PDI, DsbA, Ero1, DsbB), making it a regulated chemical modification rather than a co-translational event.

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024]** *State the Henderson–Hasselbalch equation for a cysteine thiol and explain why lowering the pKa makes a cysteine more reactive.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- (1 mark) $\text{pH} = \text{p}K_a + \log\!\left(\dfrac{[\text{S}^-]}{[\text{SH}]}\right)$ — defines the equilibrium between thiol (–SH) and thiolate (–S⁻).
- (1 mark) At pH 7.4, a free cysteine with $\text{p}K_a = 8.3$ exists as only ~**9% thiolate**; if pKa is lowered to 6.2, ~**94% exists as thiolate**.
- (1 mark) The thiolate (S⁻) is the **nucleophilic species** that attacks the acceptor sulfur; lowering pKa therefore **dramatically accelerates** disulfide formation.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> **KTU 2024 Format:** Each Part-B question carries 14 marks, typically split as **(a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks**. Module-internal choice is mandatory.

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Describe the **cellular compartmentalization of disulfide bond formation** in eukaryotes, with special emphasis on the **Ero1–PDI relay** in the ER lumen. Include the role of **Ero1's FAD cofactor** and the fate of electrons.

**Model Solution:**

1. **(1 mark)** Disulfide bond formation is restricted to the **oxidizing compartments** — ER lumen, mitochondrial intermembrane space, and bacterial periplasm. The cytoplasm is too reducing (high GSH/GSSG ratio > 100:1) and contains enzymes that actively reduce any disulfide that forms (e.g., thioredoxin, glutaredoxin).

2. **(1 mark)** In the ER lumen, **PDI (Protein Disulfide Isomerase)** is the primary catalyst. PDI has four thioredoxin-like domains: a, b, b′, a′. The **a and a′ domains** contain the catalytic **CGHC** active site.

3. **(1 mark)** PDI acts by accepting electrons from the substrate's cysteine thiols via a **mixed-disulfide intermediate** (ping-pong kinetics), becoming reduced in the process.

4. **(1 mark)** The reduced PDI is reoxidized by **Ero1 (Endoplasmic Reticulum Oxidoreductin 1)**, an FAD-containing enzyme bound to the ER membrane. Ero1's active site contains a **CXXC motif** that forms a mixed disulfide with PDI.

5. **(1 mark)** Electrons flow: Substrate → PDI → Ero1 → FAD → **O₂** (terminal electron acceptor). The product is **H₂O₂**, which is detoxified by ER-resident peroxiredoxin IV (PrxIV) and GPx8.

6. **(1 mark)** The relay is **stoichiometrically driven by O₂** consumption and **kinetically accelerated** ~10⁶-fold compared with non-enzymatic air oxidation.

7. **(1 mark) — Conclusion/regulation:** The **Ero1α/β paralogs** are feedback-regulated by ER stress (the **UPR**); under reducing stress, Ero1 transcription is upregulated to restore the oxidizing folding environment.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Using the **BPTI oxidative folding pathway** as an example, explain the concepts of **scrambled disulfides** and how **PDI's isomerase activity** corrects them. Include a stepwise description of the bond-formation order.

**Model Solution:**

1. **(1 mark)** BPTI has **58 residues and 3 disulfides**: Cys5–Cys55, Cys14–Cys38, Cys30–Cys51. There are **15 possible disulfide pairings** (3!! = 3 × 1 = 3 native plus 12 non-native/"scrambled").

2. **(1 mark)** In vitro oxidative folding (air oxidation or GSSG-driven) proceeds through a defined kinetic pathway: **R → (5–55) → (5–55, 30–51) → (5–55, 14–38, 30–51) Native (N)**.

3. **(1 mark)** The (14–38) disulfide forms **last**, even though it appears in the middle of the sequence, because its formation requires the **correct tertiary pre-arrangement** of Cys14 and Cys38.

4. **(1 mark)** Off-pathway species (e.g., **(5–30), (5–51), (14–55)**, etc.) are termed **scrambled isomers**. They are kinetically trapped and thermodynamically stable; they cannot directly interconvert with the native fold without breaking a bond first.

5. **(1 mark)** PDI's **isomerase function** is essential here: it reduces the mispaired disulfide (using its own CGHC active site, which becomes reduced), allowing the polypeptide to "try again." This requires the **G(residue between C's)H** motif — the histidine acts as a built-in acid/base catalyst that lowers the kinetic barrier.

6. **(1 mark)** PDI then re-inserts the correct pairing. The catalytic cycle: **PDI(SH,SH) → mixed disulfide → substrate rearranges → correct S–S formed → PDI reoxidized by Ero1**.

7. **(1 mark) — Conclusion:** Without PDI, BPTI folding in the ER would take **hours** and yield mostly trapped scrambled species. With PDI, native BPTI accumulates in **seconds to minutes**, demonstrating that **isomerase activity is rate-limiting in oxidative folding**.

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — ALTERNATIVE CHOICE**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Derive an expression for the **fraction of cysteine in the thiolate form** at pH 7.4 for a buried active-site cysteine whose pKa is 6.2. Comment on the **biological significance** of such a shift.

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 (1 mark) — State the Henderson–Hasselbalch equation:**
$$\text{pH} - \text{p}K_a = \log\!\left(\dfrac{[\text{S}^-]}{[\text{SH}]}\right)$$

**Step 2 (1 mark) — Substitute pH = 7.4 and pKa = 6.2:**
$$7.4 - 6.2 = 1.2 = \log\!\left(\dfrac{[\text{S}^-]}{[\text{SH}]}\right)$$

**Step 3 (1 mark) — Solve for the ratio:**
$$\dfrac{[\text{S}^-]}{[\text{SH}]} = 10^{1.2} \approx 15.85$$

**Step 4 (1 mark) — Express the fraction $\alpha_{S^-}$:**
$$\alpha_{S^-} = \dfrac{[\text{S}^-]}{[\text{SH}] + [\text{S}^-]} = \dfrac{15.85}{1 + 15.85} = \dfrac{15.85}{16.85} \approx 0.941$$

**Step 5 (1 mark) — Final numerical answer:**
**~94.1% of this cysteine is in the reactive thiolate form at pH 7.4.**

**Step 6 (1 mark) — Biological significance:**
This ~3-unit pKa depression (from 8.3 → 6.2) is achieved in enzymes like **PDI, DsbA, and caspases** by:
- A nearby **His residue** that stabilizes the thiolate through electrostatic interaction.
- A **hydrophobic pocket** that desolvates the cysteine.
- A **positive macrodipole** or $\alpha$-helix N-terminus.

**Step 7 (1 mark) — Concluding remark:**
This pKa shift is the chemical foundation of **enzymatic disulfide catalysis**; without it, the rate of S–S formation at physiological pH would be physiologically negligible.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Compare and contrast the **E. coli DsbA/DsbB** system with the **eukaryotic Ero1/PDI** system. Highlight the **electron acceptor strategy** and the **isomerase requirement** in each.

**Model Solution:**

| Feature | **E. coli Periplasm (DsbA/DsbB)** | **Eukaryotic ER (Ero1/PDI)** |
|---|---|---|
| Primary oxidase | DsbA (periplasmic) | PDI (ER luminal) |
| Re-oxidizing enzyme | DsbB (inner-membrane) | Ero1α / Ero1β (ER-membrane) |
| Cofactor | None on DsbA; **quinones** on DsbB | **FAD** on Ero1 |
| Terminal e⁻ acceptor | O₂ (via ubiquinone/menaquinone → cytochrome bo₃) | O₂ → H₂O₂ (via FAD) |
| Isomerase | **DsbC** (separate protein, kept reduced by DsbD) | **PDI itself** has intrinsic isomerase activity |
| Scrambled correction | Yes (DsbC is dedicated) | Yes (PDI's b′ domain binds scrambled substrates) |
| Redox potential of catalytic Cys | DsbA **–0.089 V** (very oxidizing) | PDI **–0.15 V** (moderately oxidizing) |
| CXXC motif | CPHC (DsbA), CXXC (DsbB) | **CGHC** (PDI), CXXC (Ero1) |
| Cellular localization of DsbC reduction | Inner-membrane DsbD uses cytoplasmic thioredoxin | PDI is auto-reoxidized by Ero1; no separate reductase needed |

**Key point-by-point discussion (7 marks, 1 mark each):**

1. **(1 mark) DsbA is the most oxidizing disulfide catalyst known** (E°′ = –0.089 V vs. PDI's –0.15 V), so it drives disulfide formation in the periplasm very aggressively.
2. **(1 mark) DsbB regenerates DsbA using the electron transport chain** (quinones → O₂ via cytochrome bo₃ bd), so no H₂O₂ is released — this protects the periplasm from oxidative damage.
3. **(1 mark) Ero1 produces H₂O₂**, requiring the **peroxiredoxin IV (PrxIV) / GPx8** detoxification system in the ER — a feature absent in the bacterial periplasm.
4. **(1 mark) DsbC** is a *dedicated* isomerase, kept reduced by the **DsbD** inner-membrane relay, which itself uses cytoplasmic thioredoxin (i.e., the reducing cytoplasm as the electron donor for the correction pathway).
5. **(1 mark) PDI** combines oxidase AND isomerase activity in a single polypeptide; the **b′ domain provides the substrate-binding surface** that recognizes scrambled and partially folded species.
6. **(1 mark) In both systems**, scrambled disulfides are reduced *first* and re-formed correctly *second*; they are never directly interconverted in a single catalytic step.
7. **(1 mark) Conclusion:** Despite the analogous relay architecture (oxidase ↔ carrier ↔ O₂), the two systems differ in **(i) cofactor choice, (ii) H₂O₂ vs H₂O electron sink, (iii) separation of oxidase and isomerase functions in bacteria vs. their unification in PDI of eukaryotes** — reflecting independent evolutionary solutions to the same redox-folding problem.

---

### 5.3 Examiner's Valuation Warnings & Common Pitfalls

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Board Pitfall #1 — "Cysteine vs. Cystine confusion":** Many students interchange *Cys* (reduced, –SH) and *Cystine* (oxidized, –S–S–). Examiners deduct **½ to 1 mark** for this. *Cys* is the residue; *Cystine* is the disulfide-linked dimer of two cysteines. Use the correct term based on redox state.
>
> **KTU Board Pitfall #2 — "Conflating cytoplasm and ER redox":** A common error is stating that "disulfides form in the cytoplasm." They generally **do not**, because the cytosolic thioredoxin/glutaredoxin systems rapidly reduce them. Marking deduction: **1 mark**.
>
> **KTU Board Pitfall #3 — "PDI = only oxidase":** PDI is **both an oxidase AND an isomerase**. Students who state only the oxidase function lose **up to 2 marks** in the 7-mark question.
>
> **KTU Board Pitfall #4 — "Ero1 reduces O₂ to H₂O":** Ero1 actually reduces O₂ to **H₂O₂ (hydrogen peroxide)**, not water. This is a 1-mark trap.
>
> **KTU Board Pitfall #5 — "Skipping the mixed disulfide step":** Many students skip the **mixed-disulfide (R–S–S–X) intermediate** in mechanism questions. This is a mandatory intermediate worth **1 mark**.
>
> **KTU Board Pitfall #6 — "Forgetting the unit in redox calculations":** $E^{\circ\prime}$ must be in **Volts (V)** and $F$ in **C/mol**; $\Delta G$ comes out in **J/mol**, which must be converted to **kJ/mol** for clarity. Examiners may deduct **½ mark** for missing units.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Rapid-Revision Checklist

- [x] **Definition:** Disulfide bond = covalent **Cys–S–S–Cys (cystine)** formed by oxidation of two thiols (loss of 2H⁺ + 2e⁻).
- [x] **Chemistry:** Nucleophilic attack of **thiolate (S⁻)** on an oxidized sulfur (S–S, sulfenic, or enzyme-bound).
- [x] **pKa matters:** Lower pKa → more thiolate → faster S–S formation; PDI/DsbA have pKa ~6 vs free Cys 8.3.
- [x] **H–H equation:** $\text{pH} = \text{p}K_a + \log([\text{S}^-]/[\text{SH}])$; $\alpha_{S^-} = 10^{\text{pH}-\text{p}K_a}/(1 + 10^{\text{pH}-\text{p}K_a})$.
- [x] **Thermodynamics:** $\Delta G^{\circ\prime} = -nF\Delta E^{\circ\prime}$; favorable when ΔE°′ > 0 (i.e., acceptor is more positive).
- [x] **Compartments:** S–S forms in **ER, mitochondrial IMS, bacterial periplasm** — *not* the cytoplasm.
- [x] **Eukaryotic relay:** **Substrate → PDI → Ero1 → FAD → O₂ → H₂O₂**.
- [x] **Bacterial relay:** **Substrate → DsbA → DsbB → quinone → cytochrome → O₂ → H₂O**.
- [x] **Isomerase function:** PDI (eukaryotes) / DsbC (bacteria) correct scrambled disulfides via *reduction-then-reoxidation*.
- [x] **BPTI model:** Three disulfides; (5–55) first, (30–51) second, **(14–38) last**.
- [x] **Scrambled isomers:** 15 possible pairings for 3 disulfides; only **1 native** set.
- [x] **Engineering uses:** Introduce disulfides to **increase Tₘ** by 5–25 °C; remove them in **cytosolic therapeutics**; redox biosensors (roGFP).
- [x] **E°′ key values:** PDI = –0.15 V; DsbA = –0.089 V; GSSG = –0.24 V; O₂/H₂O = +0.82 V.
- [x] **Geometry:** S–S bond length **2.05 Å**; Cα–Cα span **4–7 Å**; $\chi_{SS} \approx \pm 90°$.

> [!TIP]
> **One-line mnemonic for KTU viva:** *"**C**atalytic **C**ysteine, **C**ompartmentalized, **C**atalyzed by **C**GHC — the disulfide is the cell's molecular staple."*

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Tagging of Proteins with Chemical and Genetic Approaches
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBBT756 - PROTEOMICS & PROTEIN ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Tagging of Proteins with Chemical and Genetic Approaches

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Protein tagging** is the deliberate covalent attachment of a reporter, affinity, or recognition moiety to a protein of interest, either *post-translationally* (chemical tagging) or *co-translationally* by genetic fusion of a peptide/protein tag encoded within the gene (genetic tagging). The tag enables **purification, detection, quantification, subcellular localization, immobilization, or functional modulation** of the target protein without altering its core biological activity.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Verbatim Essence):**
> Protein tagging encompasses two complementary paradigms:
> 1. **Chemical Tagging** – the *in vitro* or *in vivo* derivatization of pre-existing amino acid side chains (–NH₂, –SH, –COOH) with chemical reporter groups (biotin, fluorophores, radioactive isotopes, crosslinkers).
> 2. **Genetic Tagging** – the *in-frame* fusion of a short peptide epitope (e.g., **6×His**, **FLAG**, **HA**, **Myc**) or a full-length reporter protein (e.g., **GFP**, **GST**, **MBP**, **SUMO**) at the N- or C-terminus of the protein by recombinant DNA technology.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

Imagine a college fest where you need to identify every student belonging to a particular club. You have two strategies:

- **Strategy A — Stamping on the spot (Chemical Tagging):** You grab each student (the protein) who *already exists* and paste a sticker (the chemical label) on their T-shirt. The sticker may be a glow-in-the-dark badge (fluorophore), a magnetic chip (biotin), or a radioactive tracer. The student's *genes* (DNA) are untouched.

- **Strategy B — Issuing a T-shirt at registration (Genetic Tagging):** Before the fest, you change the *registration form* (the gene) so that every student is *born* wearing a uniform T-shirt with a built-in barcode (the affinity tag, e.g., 6×His). The shirt becomes part of the student — it is translated from the same mRNA as the protein itself.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why tag proteins at all?**
> - The tag acts as a *molecular handle* — a specific, high-affinity hook for purification (Ni-NTA, anti-FLAG antibody, streptavidin).
> - It is a *reporter* for visualization (GFP fluorescence, radioactive decay).
> - It enables *pull-down*, *co-immunoprecipitation*, *ChIP*, and *FRET* experiments.
> - In **proteomics** and **structural biology**, tags are non-negotiable tools.

### 1.3 Universal Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **Streptavidin–Biotin binding affinity (K_d):** ≈ **10⁻¹⁴ M** — the strongest non-covalent biological interaction known.
- **GFP excitation/emission:** Excitation ≈ **488 nm**, Emission ≈ **507 nm**.
- **6×His–Ni²⁺/Co²⁺ coordination:** imidazole ring of histidine provides lone pair for divalent metal ion chelation in **IMAC** (Immobilized Metal Affinity Chromatography).
- **Typical tag size for peptides:** 6–30 amino acids; for protein tags: 20–450 amino acids (e.g., GFP ≈ **27 kDa**, MBP ≈ **42 kDa**, GST ≈ **26 kDa**).
- **Standard linker length (Glycine-Serine):** (GGGGS)ₙ where n = 1–4, providing **flexibility** and reducing steric clash.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Chemical Tagging — The Chemistry of Modification

Chemical tagging exploits the **nucleophilic** side chains of amino acids, primarily:

- **ε-amino group of Lysine** (–NH₂, pKa ≈ 10.5) — reacts with **NHS-esters**, **isothiocyanates**, **aldehydes (after reduction)**.
- **Thiol group of Cysteine** (–SH, pKa ≈ 8.3) — reacts with **maleimides**, **iodoacetamides**, **disulfide exchange reagents**.
- **N-terminal α-amino group** (pKa ≈ 7.6–8.0) — can be selectively modified at lower pH than Lys-ε-NH₂.
- **Carboxyl groups of Asp/Glu** (–COOH) — react with carbodiimide-activated coupling (EDC/NHS).
- **Carbohydrate moieties of glycoproteins** — oxidized by periodate and then coupled to hydrazide probes.

#### 2.1.1 Mechanism of NHS-Ester Biotinylation (Most Common)

- Step 1: NHS-ester attacks the **deprotonated amine** (–NH₂) of lysine.
- Step 2: Tetrahedral intermediate collapses, releasing **N-hydroxysuccinimide** as a leaving group.
- Step 3: Stable **amide bond** is formed between the protein and the biotin tag.

The reaction is typically performed in **phosphate buffer (pH 7.2–8.5)** at **4 °C** for 2–4 hours to prevent hydrolysis of the NHS-ester.

#### 2.1.2 Maleimide Chemistry on Cysteine

- The maleimide undergoes a **Michael addition** with the thiolate anion (–S⁻) at pH 6.5–7.5.
- Forms a **stable thioether bond**, which is non-reversible and resistant to reducing agents (unlike disulfide bonds).

#### 2.1.3 Common Chemical Tags

- **Biotin** (vitamin B7) – binds streptavidin/avidin; used in Western blot, pull-down, ELISA.
- **Fluorophores** (FITC, Alexa Fluor, Cy-dyes) – for imaging and FRET.
- **Radioactive labels** (¹²⁵I, ³²P, ³⁵S) – ultra-sensitive detection in autoradiography.
- **Spin labels** (TEMPO derivatives) – for EPR spectroscopy.
- **Crosslinkers** (glutaraldehyde, BS³, EDC) – for protein-protein interaction studies.
- **Click chemistry handles** (azide-alkyne, tetrazine-TCO) – bioorthogonal labeling.

### 2.2 Genetic Tagging — Fusion Proteins by Design

#### 2.2.1 Categories of Genetic Tags

| Category | Examples | Purpose |
|----------|----------|---------|
| **Affinity tags (peptide)** | 6×His, FLAG (DYKDDDDK), HA (YPYDVPDYA), Myc (EQKLISEEDL), Strep-II (WSHPQFEK) | Purification & detection |
| **Solubility tags (protein)** | GST, MBP, SUMO, Trx, NusA | Enhance folding & solubility |
| **Fluorescent tags (protein)** | GFP, YFP, CFP, mCherry, mNeonGreen | Live-cell imaging |
| **Epitope tags** | V5, T7, AU1, AU5 | Immunodetection |
| **Self-cleaving tags** | SUMO (Ulp1), Thrombin, TEV, PreScission | Tag removal after purification |

#### 2.2.2 The 6×His Tag — The Workhorse

- Comprises **six consecutive histidine residues** (≈ 0.84 kDa).
- Binds **Ni²⁺-NTA** (nitrilotriacetic acid) or **Co²⁺-CMA** (carboxymethylaspartate) resins.
- Eluted with **250 mM imidazole** (which competes for Ni²⁺ coordination).
- Tolerates denaturants (8 M urea, 6 M guanidinium-HCl) — ideal for purifying inclusion bodies.

#### 2.2.3 GST (Glutathione S-Transferase)

- **26 kDa** tag from *Schistosoma japonicum*.
- Binds **glutathione-Sepharose** resin with high affinity.
- Eluted with **10 mM reduced glutathione** in 50 mM Tris-HCl, pH 8.0.
- Often improves solubility of eukaryotic proteins expressed in *E. coli*.

#### 2.2.4 GFP (Green Fluorescent Protein)

- **27 kDa**; originally from *Aequorea victoria*.
- Forms its chromophore **autocatalytically** via cyclization of Ser-Tyr-Gly (residues 65–67).
- Requires **molecular oxygen**, not external cofactors.
- Variants: **EGFP** (enhanced), **superfolder GFP** (folds faster), **mCherry** (red), **mClover3** (bright green).

#### 2.2.5 Linker Design

- **Flexible linkers:** (GGGGS)ₙ — glycine provides rotational freedom, serine provides solubility.
- **Rigid linkers:** (EAAAK)ₙ — α-helical, prevents interference between domains.
- **Cleavable linkers:** contain a protease site (e.g., **TEV protease**: ENLYFQ/G).

### 2.3 KTU Formula / Property Cheat Sheet

| Property / Equation | Value / Formula | Application |
|---------------------|-----------------|-------------|
| Biotin–Streptavidin K_d | ≈ 10⁻¹⁴ M | Pull-down, detection limit |
| NHS-ester half-life at pH 8.5, 25 °C | ≈ 1 hour | Biotinylation buffer design |
| Maleimide–Thiol reaction pH optimum | 6.5 – 7.5 | Site-specific Cys labeling |
| GFP excitation λ (EGFP) | **488 nm** | Confocal / FACS |
| GFP emission λ (EGFP) | **507 nm** | Confocal / FACS |
| Imidazole elution concentration | **250 mM** | 6×His elution |
| Reduced glutathione elution | **10 mM** | GST elution |
| Beer's Law (protein quantitation) | A = ε · c · ℓ | Bradford/UV absorbance |
| Molar extinction coefficient of GFP | ≈ 55,000 M⁻¹cm⁻¹ | Quantitation at 488 nm |
| TEV protease recognition site | ENLYFQ↓G/S | Cleavable linker design |
| Linker length formula (flexible) | n × 3.5 Å (for GGGGS) | Predict fusion protein geometry |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility of Genetic Tags:**
> - Enables **one-step purification** of recombinant proteins from crude lysates.
> - Permits **in vivo imaging** of protein trafficking in living cells.
> - Facilitates **structural biology** (cryo-EM tags like MBP or GFP can aid particle picking).
> - Forms the basis of **CRISPR knock-in** fluorescent tagging for endogenous loci.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Analytical Strategy: Choosing a Tag — The KTU Decision Matrix

The selection of a tagging strategy follows a structured analytical workflow:

- **Step 1 — Define the experimental goal:** Purification? Imaging? Pull-down? Quantitation?
- **Step 2 — Assess protein characteristics:** Size, solubility, pI, post-translational modifications, toxicity, subcellular location.
- **Step 3 — Match tag to expression system:** *E. coli* → 6×His, GST, MBP. Mammalian cells → FLAG, HA, GFP. Yeast → TAP, GFP.
- **Step 4 — Evaluate downstream requirements:** Need to remove the tag? Use cleavable design. Need fluorescence? Use GFP.
- **Step 5 — Validate empirically:** Western blot, fluorescence microscopy, functional assay.

### 3.2 Step-by-Step Construction of a His₆-Fusion Protein (Detailed Protocol)

The process of creating a genetically tagged fusion protein follows a strict sequence of molecular biology operations:

**Step 1 — Gene Amplification by PCR**
Design forward and reverse primers that introduce the tag sequence in-frame with the gene of interest.

$$\text{Forward primer structure: 5'-}\underbrace{\text{RE site}}_{\text{BamHI}}\text{-}\underbrace{\text{ATG}}_{\text{Start}}\text{-}\underbrace{\text{CAT CAT CAC CAC CAC CAC CAC}}_{\text{His}_6 \text{ (CAC = His)}}\text{-}\underbrace{\text{Gene-specific sequence}}_{\approx 18 \text{ nt}}\text{-3'}$$

**Step 2 — Restriction Digestion**
Both the amplified insert and the expression vector (e.g., pET-28a) are digested with the same restriction enzymes (BamHI + NdeI, for example) to generate compatible sticky ends.

**Step 3 — Ligation**
The digested insert and vector are ligated using **T4 DNA ligase** at 16 °C overnight, maintaining the reading frame:

$$\text{ATG - His}_6 \text{ - Linker - Gene of Interest - Stop codon}$$

**Step 4 — Transformation into *E. coli* BL21(DE3)**
Heat-shock at 42 °C for 45 seconds, recovery in SOC medium, plating on kanamycin (50 µg/mL) agar.

**Step 5 — Expression Induction**
Cultures grown to **OD₆₀₀ ≈ 0.6** are induced with **0.5 mM IPTG** for 4–6 hours at 25 °C (lower temperature improves solubility).

**Step 6 — Lysis and IMAC Purification**
- Resuspend pellet in lysis buffer: **50 mM NaH₂PO₄**, **300 mM NaCl**, **10 mM imidazole**, pH 8.0.
- Add **lysozyme (1 mg/mL)** and sonicate.
- Centrifuge at 12,000 × g, 4 °C, 20 min.
- Load supernatant on **Ni²⁺-NTA agarose** column.
- Wash with 20 mM imidazole to remove non-specific binders.
- Elute with **250 mM imidazole** in 5 column volumes.

**Step 7 — Analysis**
SDS-PAGE confirms molecular weight; Western blot with anti-His antibody confirms identity.

### 3.3 Step-by-Step Chemical Biotinylation of a Purified Protein

This procedure attaches biotin covalently to surface-exposed lysine residues:

- **Step 1 — Buffer Exchange:** Dialyze protein into **PBS (pH 7.4)** or use a desalting column. Remove Tris or amines (they compete with lysine –NH₂).
- **Step 2 — Calculate Molar Ratio:** For moderate biotinylation (3–5 biotins per protein), use a **10-fold molar excess of NHS-biotin** over protein.
  $$\text{Amount of NHS-biotin (mg)} = \frac{M_{\text{biotin}} \times n_{\text{protein}} \times \text{excess factor}}{1000}$$
  where $M_{\text{biotin}}$ (NHS-LC-Biotin) ≈ 557 g/mol, $n_{\text{protein}}$ is moles of protein.
- **Step 3 — Reaction:** Add NHS-biotin (dissolved in anhydrous **DMSO**) dropwise to the protein solution. Incubate **2 hours at 4 °C** with gentle agitation.
- **Step 4 — Quenching:** Add **Tris-HCl (pH 7.5) to 50 mM** to quench unreacted NHS-esters (15 min, 4 °C).
- **Step 5 — Removal of Free Biotin:** Dialyze extensively (3 changes × 4 hours) or use a **desalting column** (e.g., PD-10, Sephadex G-25).
- **Step 6 — Verification:** **HABA assay** — bind HABA (4'-hydroxyazobenzene-2-carboxylic acid) to avidin; biotin displaces HABA, decreasing A₅₀₀. The absorbance drop is proportional to biotin incorporation.

### 3.4 Site-Specific Labeling via Cysteine-Maleimide Chemistry

For **site-specific** labeling (essential for FRET, EPR, single-molecule studies):

- **Step 1 — Engineering:** Mutate a single surface residue to **Cys** (or introduce Cys at a specific position by site-directed mutagenesis).
- **Step 2 — Reduce:** Treat with **1 mM TCEP** (Tris(2-carboxyethyl)phosphine) for 30 min to ensure free thiol.
- **Step 3 — React:** Add **5–10× molar excess of maleimide-fluorophore**; incubate **2 hours at 25 °C in dark, pH 7.0**.
- **Step 4 — Quench:** Add **10 mM DTT** or **cysteine** to consume unreacted maleimide.
- **Step 5 — Purify:** HPLC or dialysis to remove free dye.

The labeling efficiency (dye/protein) is calculated from UV-Vis spectroscopy:

$$\text{Labeling ratio} = \frac{A_{\text{dye}} / \varepsilon_{\text{dye}}}{A_{\text{protein}} / \varepsilon_{\text{protein}}}$$

where $A_{\text{dye}}$ is absorbance at the dye's λmax and $A_{\text{protein}}$ is absorbance at 280 nm (corrected for dye contribution at 280 nm by the formula $A_{280,\text{corrected}} = A_{280} - CF \cdot A_{\text{dye}}$, where $CF$ is the dye's correction factor supplied by the manufacturer).

### 3.5 Construction of a GFP Fusion Protein — Vector Design Logic

The pEGFP-N1 vector system (a representative example) illustrates the principles of C-terminal GFP tagging:

$$\underbrace{\text{CMV promoter}}_{\text{constitutive expression}} \rightarrow \underbrace{\text{MCS}}_{\text{cloning site}} \rightarrow \underbrace{\text{EGFP}}_{\text{C-terminal tag}} \rightarrow \underbrace{\text{SV40 polyA}}_{\text{stability}}$$

To clone a gene in-frame with **C-terminal EGFP**:
- The gene's **stop codon must be deleted** (via PCR with a primer omitting TAA/TAG/TGA).
- The reading frame is verified by sequencing:  ... GCA | GGC | GAG | GGC | ATG | ... 
- The fusion produces a continuous protein: **Protein-of-Interest—(GGGGS)ₙ—EGFP**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Validation Step — Frame Check:**
> A frame-shift produces a premature stop codon and a truncated, non-fluorescent protein. KTU examiners often test this with a numerical or conceptual question.

### 3.6 TEV-Mediated Tag Removal — Detailed Mechanism

The Tobacco Etch Virus (TEV) protease recognizes the consensus sequence **ENLYFQ↓G/S** and cleaves between **Q and G/S**.

**Mechanism:**
- The catalytic triad of TEV is **His⁴⁶, Asp⁸¹, Cys¹⁵¹** (cysteine protease-like).
- It performs a **nucleophilic attack** on the peptide carbonyl of the glutamine residue (Q).
- Cleavage is **highly specific**, with reduced activity at non-canonical sites.
- Optimal conditions: **30 °C, 1 mM EDTA, 1 mM DTT, pH 8.0** for 2–4 hours.
- TEV is typically used at a 1:50 (w/w) ratio relative to the substrate protein.

**Post-cleavage workflow:**
- The released tag (His₆ or GST) is removed by **reverse IMAC** or **glutathione resin**.
- The cleaved protein is recovered in the flow-through.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Comparative Architecture — Chemical vs Genetic Tagging

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Purified Native Protein] --> B[Add Chemical Reagent e.g. NHS-Biotin]
    B --> C[Amide Bond Formation]
    C --> D[Biotinylated Protein]
    D --> E[Detect with Streptavidin-HRP or Streptavidin-FITC]

    F[Gene of Interest] --> G[PCR with Tag-Encoding Primers]
    G --> H[Restriction Digest and Ligation]
    H --> I[Expression Vector e.g. pET28a-His6]
    I --> J[Transform into E coli BL21 DE3]
    J --> K[IPTG Induction]
    K --> L[Translate Fusion Protein with His6 Tag]
    L --> M[Purify on Ni-NTA Column]
    M --> N[Elute with 250 mM Imidazole]
```

### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Fusion Protein Construct

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    P1[Promoter e.g. T7 lac] --> P2[Ribosome Binding Site]
    P2 --> P3[Start Codon ATG]
    P3 --> P4[Signal Peptide optional for secretion]
    P4 --> P5[Affinity Tag e.g. His6 or GST]
    P5 --> P6[Cleavable Linker e.g. TEV site ENLYFQG]
    P6 --> P7[Protein of Interest]
    P7 --> P8[Detection Tag optional e.g. FLAG or HA]
    P8 --> P9[Stop Codon TAA]
    P9 --> P10[Transcription Terminator]
```

### 4.3 Workflow of IMAC Purification of a 6xHis Fusion

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    S1[Cell Pellet] --> S2[Lysis with Lysozyme and Sonication]
    S2 --> S3[Centrifuge 12000 x g 20 min]
    S3 --> S4[Load Supernatant on Ni-NTA Column]
    S4 --> S5[Wash with 20 mM Imidazole to remove non-specific binders]
    S5 --> S6[Elute with 250 mM Imidazole in 5 column volumes]
    S6 --> S7[Dialyze into Storage Buffer]
    S7 --> S8[Analyze by SDS-PAGE and Western Blot]
    S8 --> S9{Identity Confirmed?}
    S9 -- Yes --> S10[Store at minus 80 C with 10 percent Glycerol]
    S9 -- No --> S11[Optimize Wash and Elution Conditions]
```

### 4.4 Chemical Tagging Mechanism — NHS-Ester Reaction

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[Lysine NH2 on Protein] --> A2[Nucleophilic Attack]
    A3[NHS-Ester of Biotin] --> A2
    A2 --> A4[Tetrahedral Intermediate]
    A4 --> A5[Collapse and Release of NHS leaving group]
    A5 --> A6[Stable Amide Bond]
    A6 --> A7[Biotinylated Lysine Residue]
```

### 4.5 Genetic Tag Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    Q1{What is the Goal?}
    Q1 -- Purify --> Q2{Soluble Expression Needed?}
    Q2 -- Yes --> Q3[Use GST or MBP or SUMO]
    Q2 -- No --> Q4[Use 6xHis with Denaturing IMAC]
    Q1 -- Detect --> Q5[Use FLAG or HA or Myc with specific antibody]
    Q1 -- Image in Live Cells --> Q6[Use GFP or mCherry]
    Q1 -- Quantify --> Q7[Use Biotin or Radioactive Iodine 125]
    Q1 -- Interactomics --> Q8[Use BirA proximity labeling or APEX2]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — June 2024]**
**Define protein tagging. Differentiate between chemical tagging and genetic tagging with one suitable example each.**

**Model Answer:**

Protein tagging is the covalent attachment of a reporter or affinity group to a protein of interest to enable its detection, purification, or functional study.

- **Chemical Tagging:** Modification of pre-existing amino acid side chains of an already-purified protein with a chemical reagent *in vitro*. Example: **NHS-biotin labeling of lysine residues** for streptavidin-based detection.
