\text{Confident PSMs} = 216{,}000 \times 0.30 = 64{,}800
$$

Assuming an average of **~3 unique peptides per identified protein**:

$$
\text{Proteins identified} = \dfrac{64{,}800}{3} \approx 21{,}600
$$

In practice, due to redundancy, ion suppression, and database size effects, a realistic number is **~3,000–5,000 proteins per single-shot run**.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Why Sub-Proteome Enrichment Improves Coverage

Suppose the whole proteome spans **$10^{6}$ molecules/cell** dynamic range, and the MS detects **$10^{4}$**. A phosphoprotein present at **$10^{2}$ molecules/cell** is invisible (below detection floor). After **IMAC enrichment** of phosphopeptides achieving **~100-fold enrichment**, the effective abundance becomes:

$$
C_{\text{enriched}} = 10^{2} \times 100 = 10^{4} \text{ molecules/cell}
$$

This now lies within the dynamic range, making the phosphoprotein detectable. The **fold-improvement in depth of coverage** is given by:

$$
\text{Gain} = \dfrac{D_{\text{enriched}}}{D_{\text{whole}}} = \dfrac{E \cdot A_{\text{sub}}}{A_{\text{sub}}} \cdot \dfrac{R_{\text{MS}}}{R_{\text{MS}}} = E \cdot \dfrac{A_{\text{sub}}}{A_{\text{total}}}
$$

where $E$ = enrichment factor, $A_{\text{sub}}$ = abundance of the sub-proteome, $A_{\text{total}}$ = abundance of the total proteome. For phosphopeptides ($A_{\text{sub}} / A_{\text{total}} \approx 0.01$, $E = 100$):

$$
\text{Gain} \approx 100 \times \dfrac{1}{0.01} = 10{,}000\text{-fold effective coverage}
$$

---

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 The Shotgun Proteomics Workflow — Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Sample Lysis and Solubilization] --> B[Reduction Alkylation Trypsin Digestion]
    B --> C[Peptide Pre-fractionation SCX HILIC OFFGEL]
    C --> D[Reverse Phase NanoLC Separation]
    D --> E[ESI Ionization]
    E --> F[MS1 Full Scan Precursor Ions]
    F --> G{Data Dependent Selection}
    G --> H[MS2 Fragmentation HCD CID ETD]
    H --> I[Database Search SEQUEST Mascot Andromeda]
    I --> J[Target Decoy FDR Filtering]
    J --> K[Protein Inference and Quantification]
    K --> L[Whole Proteome Profile]
    K --> M[Sub-Proteome Subset]

    style A fill:#e1f5ff
    style B fill:#e1f5ff
    style C fill:#fff4e1
    style D fill:#fff4e1
    style E fill:#ffe1f5
    style F fill:#ffe1f5
    style G fill:#ffe1f5
    style H fill:#ffe1f5
    style I fill:#e1ffe1
    style J fill:#e1ffe1
    style K fill:#e1ffe1
    style L fill:#f0e1ff
    style M fill:#f0e1ff
```

## 4.2 Whole vs. Sub-Proteome Decision Matrix

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S[Start Complex Biological Sample] --> Q1{Biological Question?}
    Q1 -->|Catalog all proteins| W[Whole Proteome Path]
    Q1 -->|Study specific subset| SP[Sub-Proteome Path]

    W --> W1[Global Lysis]
    W1 --> W2[Optional Fractionation]
    W2 --> W3[LC-MS/MS DDA or DIA]
    W3 --> W4[Label-Free or SILAC or TMT]
    W4 --> WR[Output: ~3000-5000 proteins identified]

    SP --> SP1{Sub-Proteome Type?}
    SP1 --> P[Phosphoproteome]
    SP1 --> G[Glycoproteome]
    SP1 --> M[Membrane Proteome]
    SP1 --> O[Mitochondrial Organelle]

    P --> PE[IMAC or TiO2 Enrichment]
    G --> GE[Lectin Affinity or HILIC]
    M --> ME[Carbonate Extraction or Solvent Delipidation]
    O --> OE[Differential Centrifugation]

    PE --> SE[LC-MS/MS]
    GE --> SE
    ME --> SE
    OE --> SE
    SE --> SR[Output: Deep coverage of targeted subset]

    style W fill:#cce5ff
    style SP fill:#ffcc99
    style WR fill:#99ff99
    style SR fill:#ff9999
```

## 4.3 Sub-Proteome Enrichment Strategies — Functional Architecture Map

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    SP[Sub-Proteome Class] --> PTM[Post-Translational Modifications]
    SP --> ORG[Organelle Specific]
    SP --> DOM[Protein Domain Class]

    PTM --> P1[Phospho Ser Thr Tyr]
    PTM --> P2[Glyco Asn Ser Thr]
    PTM --> P3[Acetyl Lys]

    P1 --> E1[IMAC TiO2 SIMAC]
    P2 --> E2[Lectin ConA WGA Jacalin]
    P3 --> E3[Anti Acetyl Lys Antibody]

    ORG --> O1[Mitochondria]
    ORG --> O2[Nucleus]
    ORG --> O3[ER and Golgi]

    O1 --> EE1[Gradient Centrifugation]
    O2 --> EE2[Chromatin Fractionation]
    O3 --> EE3[Membrane Sedimentation]

    DOM --> D1[Transmembrane Proteins]
    DOM --> D2[DNA Binding Proteins]
    DOM --> D3[Metalloenzymes]

    D1 --> DD1[Carbonate Stripping or Detergent Phase Partitioning]
    D2 --> DD2[DNA Affinity Chromatography]
    D3 --> DD3[Immobilized Metal Affinity]

    style SP fill:#e1d5ff
    style PTM fill:#fff4e1
    style ORG fill:#e1f5ff
    style DOM fill:#ffe1e1
```

---

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
> **Differentiate between whole proteome analysis and sub-proteome analysis. Give one example of an enrichment strategy used in sub-proteome analysis.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Whole proteome analysis aims to identify and quantify **all proteins** expressed in a cell, tissue, or organism, without prior fractionation based on biochemical properties. Sub-proteome analysis, in contrast, focuses on a **defined subset** of proteins sharing a common feature (e.g., post-translational modification, subcellular localization, or functional class), typically requiring a specific **enrichment step** before MS analysis. **Example:** Phosphoproteome analysis uses **IMAC (Immobilized Metal Affinity Chromatography)** or **TiO₂** enrichment to selectively capture phosphorylated peptides, dramatically reducing sample complexity and increasing depth of coverage for low-abundance phosphoproteins. (3 marks)

> **Q2.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
> **What is the principle of "shotgun" proteomics? Why is it called "shotgun"?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Shotgun proteomics is a **bottom-up, discovery-driven mass spectrometry strategy** in which a complex protein mixture is first enzymatically digested (typically with trypsin) into a large pool of peptides. These peptides are then separated by one or more stages of liquid chromatography and analyzed by tandem mass spectrometry (MS/MS), with peptide identifications matched back to their parent proteins via database searching. The term **"shotgun"** is borrowed from DNA sequencing analogies — just as a shotgun randomly fragments DNA into sequenceable pieces, this approach randomly cleaves the proteome into peptide "ammunition" that is then analyzed in high-throughput fashion, making it suitable for **global, untargeted profiling**. (3 marks)

---

## Part B — Full-Descriptive Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **Note (Internal Choice Pattern):** KTU ESE Module-3 questions follow an **OR** internal choice. Two alternatives are provided.

### Question A (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

> **A.** Describe the complete **workflow of shotgun proteomics** for whole proteome profiling. Include sample preparation, digestion, separation, ionization, MS modes, and database searching. Discuss **two quantitative strategies** (one label-free and one label-based) in detail.

#### Part (a) — Workflow Explanation (7 marks)

**1. Sample Lysis & Protein Extraction (1 mark):**
Cells or tissues are disrupted using mechanical (bead-beating, sonication), chemical (urea, SDS, RapiGest), or enzymatic (lysozyme) methods. The goal is to solubilize the entire protein complement while minimizing proteolysis by adding protease inhibitors (PMSF, leupeptin) and denaturing agents.

**2. Reduction, Alkylation & Tryptic Digestion (1 mark):**
Disulfide bonds are reduced using **DTT (dithiothreitol)** or **TCEP** at 56°C for 30 min. Free cysteines are alkylated with **iodoacetamide (IAA)** in the dark, adding a diagnostic **+57.02146 Da** mass tag. The sample is diluted to reduce urea concentration to <1 M, and **trypsin** is added at 1:50 (enzyme:substrate) ratio for overnight digestion at 37°C. Trypsin cleaves specifically at the C-terminal side of **Lys (K)** and **Arg (R)** residues, except when followed by **Pro (P)**.

**3. Peptide Pre-fractionation (1 mark):**
To reduce the dynamic range burden, peptides are often pre-separated using **strong cation exchange (SCX)**, **high-pH reversed-phase (HpH-RP)**, or **OFFGEL IEF**. This compresses the dynamic range and increases the number of identifications by ~30–50%.

**4. Liquid Chromatography & Electrospray Ionization (1.5 marks):**
Peptides are loaded onto a **nanoLC C18 reversed-phase column** (typically 75 µm × 25 cm) and eluted with a gradient of acetonitrile (5–35% B over 60–120 min). The eluate is ionized via **nano-electrospray ionization (nESI)**, producing multiply charged peptide ions (typically $[\text{M}+2\text{H}]^{2+}$ and $[\text{M}+3\text{H}]^{3+}$).

**5. Tandem Mass Spectrometry (1.5 marks):**
The mass spectrometer operates in **data-dependent acquisition (DDA)** mode:
- **MS1 scan:** High-resolution survey scan (resolution 60,000 at m/z 400) to identify peptide precursors.
- **MS2 scan:** Top **N** (typically 10–20) most intense precursors are isolated, fragmented by **HCD (Higher-energy Collisional Dissociation)**, and the fragments analyzed in the Orbitrap or ion trap.
- Common instruments: **Q-Exactive, Orbitrap Fusion, timsTOF Pro**.

**6. Database Searching & Protein Inference (1 mark):**
MS/MS spectra are searched against a **FASTA protein database** (e.g., UniProtKB) using algorithms like **SEQUEST-HT, Mascot, MS-GF+, or Andromeda**. Search parameters include: precursor mass tolerance (±10 ppm), fragment mass tolerance (±0.02 Da), fixed modification = **carbamidomethyl (C)**, variable modification = **oxidation (M)**, allowed missed cleavages = 2. **Target-decoy FDR** is applied at ≤1% at both PSM and protein levels.

#### Part (b) — Quantitative Strategies (7 marks)

**Strategy 1: Label-Free Quantification (LFQ) — 3.5 marks:**

Two sub-approaches:
- **(i) Spectral Counting:** The number of MS/MS spectra assigned to a protein correlates with its abundance. The **NSAF (Normalized Spectral Abundance Factor)** is computed as:

$$
\text{NSAF}_k = \dfrac{\text{SpC}_k / L_k}{\sum_{i=1}^{N} (\text{SpC}_i / L_i)}
$$

where $\text{SpC}_k$ is the spectral count of protein $k$, and $L_k$ is its length (number of amino acids). Normalization by length corrects for the bias that larger proteins generate more peptides.

- **(ii) Intensity-Based (MaxQuant LFQ):** Peptide ion intensities (or extracted ion chromatograms, XIC) are summed and normalized across samples. MaxQuant's **LFQ algorithm** uses delayed normalization and robust statistics to handle missing values. Fold changes and p-values are computed using Student's t-test or Limma-modulated statistics.

**Advantages:** Cheap, no chemical labelling, unlimited sample number.
**Disadvantages:** Lower reproducibility, susceptible to run-to-run variation.

**Strategy 2: Tandem Mass Tags (TMT) — 3.5 marks:**

TMT is a **chemical, isobaric labelling** strategy. Each sample is derivatized with one of **10 (or 18 in TMTpro)** isobaric tags differing only in the position of heavy isotopes ($^{13}\text{C}$, $^{15}\text{N}$). All tags have the **same nominal mass** (~229 Da) and identical chromatographic behaviour. After mixing, all differentially labelled peptides co-elute and are fragmented in MS². The **reporter ion region** (m/z 126–134) is released during HCD, and the relative intensities of the reporter ions give the **relative abundance** of the peptide (and thus protein) across samples.

**Workflow:**
1. Trypsin digest each sample.
2. Label each with a distinct TMT tag.
3. Pool and analyse by LC-MS/MS.
4. Quantify via reporter ion intensities.

**Advantages:** Multiplexing (up to 18 samples), high precision, low missing values.
**Disadvantages:** Expensive, ratio compression due to co-isolation interference.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

> **B.** What is **sub-proteome analysis**? Discuss its significance and explain **three major sub-proteome categories** with their corresponding **enrichment strategies**. Also explain how **phosphoproteomics** has revolutionized disease biomarker discovery.

#### Part (a) — Concept of Sub-Proteome & Three Categories (7 marks)

**Definition (1 mark):**
A **sub-proteome** is a defined subset of the entire proteome that shares a common biological, chemical, or spatial feature. Sub-proteome analysis is the targeted profiling of such subsets, typically after an enrichment step, to achieve deeper coverage of the targeted proteins than would be possible in a whole-proteome experiment.

**Category 1: Phosphoproteome (2 marks):**
- **Definition:** All proteins carrying a phosphate group on **Ser, Thr, or Tyr** residues.
- **Biological significance:** Phosphorylation regulates **signal transduction, cell cycle, apoptosis, and metabolism**. Dysregulated phosphorylation is a hallmark of **cancer, diabetes, and neurodegeneration**.
- **Enrichment strategy:** **IMAC (Immobilized Metal Affinity Chromatography)** uses Fe³⁺ or Ga³⁺ chelated to nitrilotriacetic acid (NTA) agarose to capture phosphopeptides via the negatively charged phosphate group. **TiO₂** micro-columns are an alternative that exploits the high affinity of phosphate for titania. Typical enrichment: **>90% selectivity** for phosphopeptides.

**Category 2: Glycoproteome (2 marks):**
- **Definition:** All proteins carrying N-linked (Asn) or O-linked (Ser/Thr) glycan modifications.
- **Biological significance:** Glycosylation modulates **protein folding, cell-cell recognition, immune evasion**, and is altered in **cancer and infectious diseases**. Most FDA-approved biomarkers (e.g., **CA-125, PSA, AFP**) are glycoproteins.
- **Enrichment strategy:** **Lectin affinity chromatography** uses immobilized lectins such as **Concanavalin A (ConA)** for high-mannose glycans, **Wheat Germ Agglutinin (WGA)** for sialic acid, and **Jacalin** for O-glycans. **HILIC (Hydrophilic Interaction Liquid Chromatography)** is an orthogonal method exploiting the hydrophilicity of glycopeptides.

**Category 3: Membrane / Organelle Sub-Proteome (2 marks):**
- **Definition:** Proteins localized to specific subcellular compartments such as the **plasma membrane, mitochondria, nucleus, ER, or peroxisomes**.
- **Biological significance:** Membrane proteins constitute **~30% of all cellular proteins** and **>60% of drug targets** (GPCRs, ion channels, transporters). However, they are notoriously under-represented in standard proteomics due to poor solubility.
- **Enrichment strategy:** **Sodium carbonate stripping** removes peripheral proteins from membranes, leaving integral membrane proteins. **Density gradient centrifugation** (e.g., sucrose, Optiprep) separates organelles based on size/density, followed by **marker-enzyme assays** to confirm purity. Detergents like **digitonin** selectively permeabilize plasma membrane while preserving organelle integrity.

#### Part (b) — Phosphoproteomics in Biomarker Discovery (7 marks)

**1. Workflow of Clinical Phosphoproteomics (3 marks):**
- **Sample collection:** Tumor vs. matched normal tissue, or plasma from patients vs. healthy controls.
- **Lysis & Digestion:** Same as standard shotgun, but with **phosphatase inhibitors** (NaF, β-glycerophosphate, sodium vanadate) added to all buffers to preserve phosphorylation.
- **Enrichment:** IMAC or TiO₂.
- **LC-MS/MS:** High-resolution Orbitrap with **synchronous precursor selection (SPS)** for MS³ (TMT) to mitigate ratio compression.
- **Data analysis:** Site localization probability is computed by the **Ascore** algorithm (threshold $\geq 19$, p $< 0.01$) or **ptmRS**. Differential phosphorylation is assessed by **ANOVA** or **Limma**.

**2. Why Phosphoproteomics is Revolutionary (2 marks):**
- **Dynamic signalling readout:** Phosphorylation changes within **seconds to minutes** in response to stimuli, providing a snapshot of **active cellular states** rather than just steady-state protein levels.
- **Drug target identification:** Kinase inhibitors (e.g., **imatinib for BCR-ABL**) directly modulate the phosphoproteome. Global phosphoproteomics reveals **off-target effects** and **mechanism of action**.
- **Resistance mechanism discovery:** Acquired resistance to EGFR inhibitors in lung cancer has been linked to **activation of compensatory kinases** (MET, AXL) — visible only in the phosphoproteome, not the transcriptome.

**3. Case Examples (2 marks):**
- **HER2+ breast cancer:** Tyrosine phosphoproteomics identified **>100 hyperphosphorylated sites** in tumor vs. normal tissue, including activation of **EGFR, PDGFR, and Src family kinases**.
- **Alzheimer's disease:** Brain phosphoproteomics revealed **hyperphosphorylation of tau** at specific Ser/Thr sites (S262, S356) that correlate with **neurofibrillary tangle** burden — now used in **CSF-based diagnostic assays**.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing shotgun with top-down proteomics.** Shotgun = peptides first, top-down = intact proteins. (Lose 1–2 marks)
> 2. **Forgetting to add phosphatase inhibitors** in phosphoproteomics sample prep. (Lose 1 mark)
> 3. **Mixing up iTRAQ reporter masses vs. TMT reporter masses.** iTRAQ 8-plex = 113–121; TMT 10-plex = 126–134. (Lose 1 mark)
> 4. **Skipping the FDR / target-decoy step** when describing database search. Always mention **FDR ≤ 1% at PSM level**. (Lose 1 mark)
> 5. **Writing the mass shift of carbamidomethyl incorrectly** — it is **+57.02146 Da**, not +57.0. (Lose 0.5 mark)
> 6. **Forgetting Pro rule in trypsin specificity:** "Cleaves at K and R, but not if followed by Proline." (Lose 0.5 mark)

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 3 / Shotgun Proteomics**

- **Shotgun (bottom-up) proteomics** = digest proteins to peptides → LC-MS/MS → database search.
- **Top-down** = intact proteins ionized; rarely used due to technical challenges.
- **Trypsin** cleaves C-terminal to **K, R** (not before **P**); adds **+57.02146 Da** (Cys alkylation with IAA) and detects **+15.99491 Da** (Met oxidation) as common modifications.
- **Whole proteome** = breadth-first, no enrichment; typically **3,000–5,000 proteins** per run.
- **Sub-proteome** = depth-first, requires enrichment; gives **>10,000-fold** effective coverage gain.
- **Key sub-proteomes & enrichments:**
  * **Phospho** → **IMAC, TiO₂**
  * **Glyco** → **Lectin (ConA, WGA)**, **HILIC**
  * **Membrane** → **Na₂CO₃ strip, density gradient**
  * **Mitochondrial** → **Differential centrifugation + marker assay**
  * **Acetyl** → **Anti-AcK antibody**
- **Quantification:** Label-free (LFQ, NSAF), SILAC (metabolic), iTRAQ/TMT (isobaric chemical), DIA/SWATH (acquisition method).
- **Database search engines:** SEQUEST, Mascot, Andromeda (MaxQuant), MS-GF+, PEAKS.
- **FDR control:** **Target-decoy approach**, threshold **≤ 1% at PSM and protein level.**
- **Phospho-site localization:** **Ascore ≥ 19** or **ptm-RS ≥ 0.99**.
- **Biomarker significance:** Most clinical biomarkers (PSA, CA-125, AFP) are **glycoproteins**, not whole proteome hits — sub-proteome analysis is essential.
- **Dynamic range of MS:** ~$10^{4}$–$10^{6}$, which is why **enrichment is the dominant strategy** for low-abundance post-translational modifications.
- **Mass accuracy benchmarks:** **<5 ppm** for precursor (Orbitrap), **<0.02 Da** for fragments.
- **Major instruments:** **Q-Exactive, Orbitrap Fusion Lumos, timsTOF Pro, TripleTOF 6600**.
- **MudPIT (Multidimensional Protein Identification Technology)** combines SCX + RP in a single capillary column — still the gold standard for ultra-deep whole proteome coverage.

> **Last-line takeaway for KTU boards:** *Shotgun proteomics trades molecular intactness for analytical depth — and that trade is exactly why sub-proteome enrichment is the engine of modern biomarker discovery.*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBBT756 - PROTEOMICS & PROTEIN ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Protein-DNA Interactions

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Protein–DNA Interaction** is the specific, physico-chemical association between a DNA-binding protein (transcription factor, repressor, activator, polymerase, histone, or architectural protein) and a double-helical (or sometimes single-stranded) DNA target, mediated by a defined **DNA-binding domain (DBD)** on the protein surface. The interaction is governed by a combination of **hydrogen bonds, electrostatic salt-bridges, van der Waals (shape complementarity) contacts, and water-mediated bridges**, and is characterized quantitatively by an equilibrium binding constant $K_d$ (typically in the **nM to μM** range for sequence-specific proteins) and a binding free energy $\Delta G^\circ_{bind}$.

The strength and specificity of the interaction arise from two conceptually distinct molecular recognition modes:

- **Direct Readout** – the protein “reads” the chemical identity (hydrogen-bond donors/acceptors, methyl groups) of exposed DNA bases.
- **Indirect Readout** – the protein “senses” the intrinsic shape, flexibility, bendability, or electrostatic potential of the DNA double helix, which is in turn a function of its base sequence.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Lock, Key, and Hand-in-Glove

Imagine a locksmith trying to open a particular door (the gene) on a long street of identical-looking houses (the genome). Two things help him succeed:

1. **The key’s teeth must match the lock’s pins** (a direct, chemical, hand-shake recognition — *direct readout*).
2. **The street must be wide enough for him to stand, and the door frame must be rigid** (a structural, geometric, architectural fit — *indirect readout*).

The locksmith is the **transcription factor** (protein), the key is the **DNA-binding domain**, the lock is the **response element / operator / promoter sequence**, and the street is the **chromatin / genomic DNA**. Specificity comes from BOTH a matching key (chemistry) and a matching door-frame shape (mechanics).

## 1.3 Key Standard Metrics & Constants (Bolded)

- **Hydrogen bond length in DNA recognition: ~2.7–3.1 Å.**
- **B-DNA helical pitch: ~10.5 bp per turn, 3.4 Å rise per base pair.**
- **Major groove width in B-DNA: ~12 Å (large enough to admit an α-helix).**
- **Minor groove width in B-DNA: ~6 Å (accommodates smaller motifs or drugs).**
- **Typical $K_d$ of sequence-specific TFs: $\mathbf{10^{-9}}$ to $\mathbf{10^{-12}}$ M.**
- **Dielectric constant of water at 25 °C: ~78.5; protein interior ~4.**
- **Physiological [KCl]: ~150 mM; intracellular [Na⁺]: ~10–15 mM.**

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** The KTU Module-3 syllabus for *PEBBT756* treats Protein–DNA Interactions as the conceptual bridge between *sequence* (genomics) and *function* (transcriptional regulation). Mastery of (a) DNA-binding motifs, (b) read-out mechanisms, and (c) experimental detection methods is mandatory for Part-B (14-mark) questions.

## 1.4 Classification Snapshot

Protein–DNA interactions are conventionally grouped by **structural class of the DBD**:

| Class | Recognition Element (in DNA) | Example Protein | Biological Role |
|---|---|---|---|
| Helix–turn–helix (HTH) | Major groove | λ Cro, Lac repressor | Phage / prokaryotic gene regulation |
| Zinc finger (Cys₂His₂) | Major groove | TFIIIA, Sp1 | Eukaryotic transcription |
| Helix–loop–helix / bHLH | Major groove | Myc, MyoD | Development, oncogenesis |
| Leucine zipper (bZIP) | Inverted repeat (CRE/AP-1) | GCN4, Fos–Jun | Stress response, proliferation |
| HMG-box | Minor groove / bends DNA | SRY, HMGB1 | Chromatin remodelling, sex determination |
| Homeodomain | Major groove | Hox proteins | Body-plan specification |
| Winged-helix (Fox) | Major groove | FOXP2, FOXO1 | Metabolism, language |
| Histone-fold | Sugar-phosphate backbone | Histone octamer | Nucleosome packaging |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A single transcription factor may carry *multiple* DNA-binding motifs; for example, the human glucocorticoid receptor (GR) contains a **Cys₂Cys₂ zinc finger** that targets an inverted-repeat half-site in the GRE.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Operative Logic of Specificity — A Six-Step Decision Process

A transcription factor (TF) “decides” whether to bind a given DNA site through a hierarchical, energetically-coupled cascade. For the KTU examiner, walk through these six logical steps whenever you answer a question on specificity:

1. **Long-range electrostatic steering.** The negatively-charged DNA polyanion (each bp carries ≈ 2 e⁻ from the phosphate backbone) attracts a positively cloud of Lys/Arg on the protein. The encounter complex forms within a diffusion-limited regime.
2. **1-D sliding / 1-D diffusion.** The protein “slides” along the helix, sampling 50–1000 bp by *facilitated diffusion*, decreasing the effective dimensionality of search and accelerating target location.
3. **Local dehydration & minor-groove entry.** Cation-π and water-release contributions become favourable when the protein DBD inserts its recognition helix into the major (or minor) groove.
4. **Shape complementarity (indirect readout).** DNA roll, twist, propeller twist and bendability are sequence-dependent. Proteins select sequences whose local geometry matches their DBD.
5. **Base-specific hydrogen bonds (direct readout).** Donor–acceptor patterns of A·T and G·C edges (Watson–Crick, Hoogsteen, sugar edge) are read by side chains of Asn, Gln, Asp, Arg, His.
6. **Cooperative assembly.** Adjacent sites recruit additional TF copies, looping DNA and stabilising the nucleoprotein complex (Hill coefficient $n_H > 1$).

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **All equations below are routinely tested as 3-mark short derivations or as part of 14-mark Part-B problems.** Commit them to memory.

| # | Formula | Meaning / Use | Variables & Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\Delta G^\circ = -RT \ln K_a$ | Free energy of binding | $R = 1.987\,\text{cal mol}^{-1}\text{K}^{-1}$, $T$ in K, $K_a$ in M$^{-1}$ |
| 2 | $K_d = \dfrac{[P][D]}{[PD]}$ | Dissociation constant | M (often nM) |
| 3 | $\Delta G^\circ = \Delta H^\circ - T\Delta S^\circ$ | van't Hoff decomposition | $\Delta H^\circ$ in kcal/mol |
| 4 | $\dfrac{d \ln K_a}{dT} = \dfrac{\Delta H^\circ}{RT^2}$ | van't Hoff equation | – |
| 5 | $f = \dfrac{[PD]}{[P]_T}$ | Fraction bound | dimensionless, $[P]_T = [P] + [PD]$ |
| 6 | $f = \dfrac{[D]}{K_d + [D]}$ | Hyperbolic (non-cooperative) | Hill coefficient $n = 1$ |
| 7 | $f = \dfrac{[D]^n}{K_d^{\,n} + [D]^n}$ | Hill equation (cooperative) | $n =$ Hill coefficient |
| 8 | $K_d^{(app)} = K_d \dfrac{1 + [C]}{K_{C}}$ | Competitor-displaced $K_d$ | $[C]$ = competitor conc. |
| 9 | $\Delta\Delta G^\circ = -RT \ln\dfrac{K_d^{mut}}{K_d^{wt}}$ | Mutant-vs-wild-type ΔΔG | kcal mol$^{-1}$ |
| 10 | $S = -k_B \sum_i p_i \ln p_i$ | Information content (Shannon) | bits / site |
| 11 | $\tau \approx \dfrac{N}{D_1 D_3}$ | Facilitated-diffusion search time | $N$ bp, $D_1$ 1-D, $D_3$ 3-D diffusion coeff. |
| 12 | $R_0 = \sqrt[6]{\dfrac{1}{k_F}\dfrac{9\ln(10)}{128\pi^5 N_A}\dfrac{\Phi_D \kappa^2 Q_D}{n^4 \tau_c}}$ | Förster distance (FRET-based detection) | Å |

> [!WARNING]
> The **vertical pipe symbol `|` is FORBIDDEN inside markdown tables** (per KTU template). For absolute value, always use the LaTeX command `\vert` or `\mid`. Example: $\Delta G = \vert \Delta H - T\Delta S \vert$ is invalid; use $\Delta G = \lvert \Delta H - T\Delta S \rvert$.

## 2.3 Direct Readout — The Chemical Recognition Code

Direct readout exploits the **asymmetric presentation of hydrogen-bond donors and acceptors** in the major and minor grooves of A·T and G·C base pairs.

- **Major groove of G·C** displays: O6 (acceptor), N7 (acceptor), and a cytosine exocyclic NH₂ (donor) on the other strand.
- **Major groove of A·T** displays: N6 (donor) and N7 (acceptor).
- **Minor groove** is information-poor for G·C (only one acceptor-donor edge), but rich in **shape** — a basis for *minor-groove binders* such as HMG-box proteins and the antibiotic netropsin.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Tip:** In protein engineering, the *“recognition code”* is NOT one-to-one (unlike the genetic code). A single base-pair change typically alters $K_d$ by **1.4–10× (ΔΔG = 0.2–1.4 kcal/mol)** — an important value to remember for designing or interpreting mutagenesis data.

## 2.4 Indirect Readout — The Geometric Recognition Code

Sequence → geometry → binding. The dinucleotide step parameters that govern indirect readout are:

- **Roll** (rotation about an axis into the groove) — large for A-tracts and TpA.
- **Slide** (translation along the long axis of the base pair) — favours CA/TG over CG/CG.
- **Propeller twist** — the twist of one base relative to the other within a pair; correlates with C·G-rich *CpG* islands.
- **Bendability** — anisotropic flexibility, high at *A/T-rich* sequences.

Proteins such as the **catabolite activator protein (CAP)** of *E. coli* exploit indirect readout almost exclusively: the CRP–DNA complex is stabilised by ~30° DNA bending that is intrinsically preferred at AT-rich flanks.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Synthetic biology:** Engineered zinc-finger nucleases (ZFNs), TALENs, and CRISPR–Cas9 are direct descendants of natural protein–DNA interaction chemistry. Knowing $K_d$ and $\Delta\Delta G^\circ$ for each finger enables target-programmable nucleases.
- **Drug design:** *Minor-groove binders* (e.g., Hoechst 33258, distamycin, brostallicin) exploit the AT-rich minor-groove geometry of parasitic DNA.
- **Diagnostics:** Electrophoretic mobility shift assays (EMSA) and chromatin immunoprecipitation (ChIP) rely on the *K_d* differences between specific and non-specific complexes.
- **Chromatin biology:** Understanding *histone–DNA* contacts is foundational to epigenetic therapy (HDAC inhibitors, BET bromodomain inhibitors).

## 2.6 The “Three Pillars” Model for KTU Answers

Whenever a KTU Part-B question asks you to “describe protein–DNA interaction”, structure your answer on the three pillars:

1. **Structural** – the DNA-binding motif and the DNA surface it contacts.
2. **Energetic** – the thermodynamic partition ($\Delta G$, $\Delta H$, $\Delta S$, $\Delta C_p$) and the role of water/ions.
3. **Functional** – biological consequence (activation, repression, recombination, repair, packaging).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — From $K_d$ to a Quantitative Free Energy of Binding

A standard 7-mark derivation in KTU ESE: *“Show that $\Delta G^\circ = -RT \ln K_a$ and compute $\Delta G^\circ$ for a TF–DNA complex of $K_d = 1 \text{ nM}$ at 298 K.”*

Starting from the statistical-mechanical definition of the equilibrium constant for the bimolecular binding reaction

$$P + D \rightleftharpoons PD$$

the standard-state free energy change is, by definition,

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta G^\circ &\equiv -k_B T \ln \frac{Q_{PD}/V}{Q_P/V \;\cdot\; Q_D/V} \\
&= -k_B T \ln \frac{q_{PD}}{q_P q_D / V}
\end{aligned}
$$

where $Q_i$ is the canonical partition function, $V$ the standard-state volume (1 L = $10^{-3}\text{ m}^3$ per mole at 1 M), and $k_B$ is Boltzmann’s constant. The ratio of partition functions is, by the law of mass action, exactly the equilibrium constant $K_a$ (in units of M$^{-1}$). Substituting and using the gas constant $R = N_A k_B$ (with $N_A$ = Avogadro’s number) we obtain:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta G^\circ &= -k_B T \ln K_a \\
&= -RT \ln K_a \quad \text{(per mole basis)}
\end{aligned}
$$

Substituting $K_d = 1/K_a = 10^{-9}$ M ⇒ $K_a = 10^{9}$ M$^{-1}$, $R = 1.987\,\text{cal mol}^{-1}\text{K}^{-1}$, $T = 298$ K:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta G^\circ &= -(1.987)(298) \ln(10^{9}) \\
&= -(592.1)(9 \times 2.303) \\
&= -(592.1)(20.72) \\
&\approx -12{,}274 \text{ cal mol}^{-1} \\
&\boxed{\Delta G^\circ \approx -12.3 \text{ kcal mol}^{-1}}
\end{aligned}
$$

This is a *very* tight interaction — typical of high-affinity TFs (compare to biotin–streptavidin, $-18.4$ kcal mol$^{-1}$).

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — The van’t Hoff Plot for Determining $\Delta H^\circ$

A common 7-mark KTU question: *“Derive the van’t Hoff equation and explain how $\Delta H^\circ$ is obtained experimentally for a protein–DNA complex.”*

Starting from the Gibbs–Helmholtz relation and substituting $K_a = e^{-\Delta G^\circ/RT}$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta G^\circ &= \Delta H^\circ - T \Delta S^\circ \\
\Rightarrow \ln K_a &= -\frac{\Delta H^\circ}{R T} + \frac{\Delta S^\circ}{R}
\end{aligned}
$$

Differentiating with respect to $T$ at constant pressure, and noting that $\Delta H^\circ$ itself is, in general, temperature-dependent via the heat-capacity change $\Delta C_p$ (a hallmark of protein–DNA binding):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{d \ln K_a}{dT} &= \frac{\Delta H^\circ}{R T^2} \\
\frac{d \ln K_a}{d(1/T)} &= -\frac{\Delta H^\circ}{R}
\end{aligned}
$$

A plot of $\ln K_a$ versus $1/T$ (the **van’t Hoff plot**) yields a straight line of slope $-\Delta H^\circ/R$ if $\Delta C_p \approx 0$; curvature reveals non-zero $\Delta C_p$, a signature of *coupled folding* of the DBD upon binding (“induced fit”).

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — The Hill Equation for Cooperative TF Binding

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Cooperativity is a *very* high-yield KTU topic. A 7-mark question can ask: *“A transcription factor binds a palindromic operator with $K_d = 5$ nM and Hill coefficient $n = 2$. Compute the fractional occupancy at $[D] = K_d$.”*

The Hill equation is derived by assuming $n$ identical, simultaneously-occupied binding sites that act as a single cooperative unit:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P + nD \rightleftharpoons P D_n, \quad K_H = \frac{[PD_n]}{[P][D]^n}
\end{aligned}
$$

The fractional saturation is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
f &= \frac{[PD_n]}{[P] + [PD_n]} \\
  &= \frac{K_H [D]^n}{1 + K_H [D]^n}
\end{aligned}
$$

Re-parameterising using $K_d = K_H^{-1/n}$:

$$
\boxed{\;f = \frac{[D]^n}{K_d^{\,n} + [D]^n}\;}
$$

Substituting $[D] = K_d$ and $n = 2$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
f &= \frac{K_d^{\,2}}{K_d^{\,2} + K_d^{\,2}} = \frac{1}{2}
\end{aligned}
$$

At $[D] = K_d$, occupancy is *always* 0.5 — independent of $n$ — because the Hill equation is normalised at the dissociation constant. The role of $n$ appears in the *steepness* of the curve: $n = 1$ gives a hyperbola; $n = 2$ gives a sigmoidal switch with switch-point at $f = 0.5$. This switch-like behaviour is precisely why **cooperative TFs are favoured in genetic decision-making**.

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Mutant-vs-Wild-Type ΔΔG° from $K_d$ Ratios

If a base-substitution mutant changes $K_d$ from 1 nM (WT) to 20 nM (mutant), the loss of binding free energy is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta\Delta G^\circ &= \Delta G^\circ_{mut} - \Delta G^\circ_{wt} \\
&= -RT \ln K_a^{mut} - (-RT \ln K_a^{wt}) \\
&= -RT \ln \frac{K_a^{mut}}{K_a^{wt}} \\
&= +RT \ln \frac{K_d^{mut}}{K_d^{wt}} \\
&= (1.987 \times 10^{-3})(298) \ln(20) \\
&= (0.5921)(2.996) \\
&\boxed{\Delta\Delta G^\circ \approx +1.77 \text{ kcal mol}^{-1}}
\end{aligned}
$$

A useful rule-of-thumb from the **ds-DNA TF literature**: each lost hydrogen bond or lost van der Waals methyl contact costs **0.5–2.5 kcal mol$^{-1}$** of binding free energy.

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Comparing the Major DNA-Binding Motifs

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **This matrix is essential for the 14-mark comparative essay question** that the KTU examiner loves: *“Compare and contrast the helix–turn–helix, zinc-finger, leucine-zipper, and HMG-box DNA-binding motifs.”*

| Motif | Recognition Helix / Probe | Groove Read | Dimerisation | Sequence Specificity | Typical $\Delta\Delta G^\circ$ per contact |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Helix-turn-helix | $\alpha$-helix 3 inserts into major groove | Major | Optional (e.g., Cro) | High | 0.5–1.5 kcal mol$^{-1}$ |
| Cys₂His₂ zinc finger | $\alpha$-helix –N-terminal of $\beta$-hairpin– | Major | Tandem arrays for higher affinity | Programmable | 0.3–1.0 kcal mol$^{-1}$ |
| Basic leucine zipper (bZIP) | $\alpha$-helix N-terminus (basic region) | Major (inverted repeat) | Coiled-coil obligate | High (palindromic) | 1.0–2.0 kcal mol$^{-1}$ |
| bHLH | $\alpha$-helix 1 + loop | Major (E-box CANNTG) | Obligate HLH–HLH | High | 1.0–1.8 kcal mol$^{-1}$ |
| HMG-box | Three $\alpha$-helices, concave | **Minor** (bends DNA 90°+) | Often monomer | Low / shape-based | 0.7–1.5 kcal mol$^{-1}$ |
| Homeodomain | Helix 3 (recognition) | Major | Optional | High | 0.5–1.5 kcal mol$^{-1}$ |
| Winged-helix (Fox) | Helix 3 + $\beta$-sheet “wing” | Major | Variable | High | 0.4–1.2 kcal mol$^{-1}$ |
| $\beta$-sheet (TBP) | Saddle-shaped $\beta$-sheet | **Minor** (bends DNA ~90°, kinks at TATA) | Monomer in TFIID | TATA box | 1.5–3.0 kcal mol$^{-1}$ |

## 3.6 Derivation 5 — Information Content of a TF Binding Site

KTU loves short-derivation questions (3 marks). The Shannon information content of a TF binding site of length $L$ is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
S = L - \sum_{i=1}^{L}\sum_{b \in \{A,C,G,T\}} p_{b,i} \log_2 p_{b,i} \quad \text{bits}
\end{aligned}
$$

where $p_{b,i}$ is the observed frequency of base $b$ at position $i$ in a ChIP-seq-derived position weight matrix (PWM). For a 6-bp lac repressor site with a perfectly conserved 6 positions, $S = 6 - 0 = 6$ bits = the 1/4096 expected genome frequency of a *random* 6-mer, consistent with the known ~$10^6$ operator sites per *E. coli* genome and the need for tight repression.

## 3.7 Worked Example — A Complete KTU-Style 7-Mark Calculation

**Q (paraphrased from past paper):** A homodimeric TF binds its palindromic site with $\Delta H^\circ = -22$ kcal mol$^{-1}$ and $\Delta S^\circ = -32$ cal mol$^{-1}$ K$^{-1}$ at 298 K. Calculate $K_d$ and the fraction bound at $[D] = 10$ nM.

**Step 1 — Compute $\Delta G^\circ$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta G^\circ &= \Delta H^\circ - T \Delta S^\circ \\
&= -22\,000 - (298)(-32) \\
&= -22\,000 + 9\,536 \\
&= -12\,464 \text{ cal mol}^{-1} = -12.46 \text{ kcal mol}^{-1}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute $K_a$ and $K_d$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\ln K_a &= -\frac{\Delta G^\circ}{RT} = -\frac{-12\,464}{(1.987)(298)} = \frac{12\,464}{592.1} = 21.05 \\
K_a &= e^{21.05} \approx 1.36 \times 10^{9} \text{ M}^{-1} \\
K_d &= \frac{1}{K_a} \approx 7.35 \times 10^{-10} \text{ M} = 0.735 \text{ nM}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute fraction bound at $[D] = 10$ nM (using the non-cooperative hyperbola since $n=1$ not stated):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
f &= \frac{[D]}{K_d + [D]} = \frac{10}{0.735 + 10} = \frac{10}{10.735} \approx 0.932 = 93.2\%
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> Common KTU student error: forgetting the **sign convention** for $\Delta S^\circ$ in protein–DNA binding. The process is *almost always entropically unfavourable* (negative $\Delta S^\circ$) because two molecules become one; however, it can be *apparently* favourable if water/ion release contributes a large positive cratic entropy. Always sign-check against $-\Delta G^\circ > 0$ for binding.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 The Two-Mode Readout Architecture (Concept Map)

The following Mermaid **flowchart** maps the conceptual pipeline from *DNA sequence* to *biological output*, integrating both direct and indirect readout. The diagram is rendered as a **block-level functional architecture** (per protocol) because the underlying physical reality involves 3-D protein–DNA complexes that are beyond the limits of pure ASCII.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[DNA Sequence 5' to 3'] --> B[Base Pair Edges Exposed in Major and Minor Grooves]
    B --> C{DNA-Binding Domain Probe Type}
    C -- Alpha-helix recognition --> D[Direct Readout: H-bond Donors and Acceptors]
    C -- Beta-sheet or concave surface --> E[Indirect Readout: Shape and Bendability]
    D --> F[Specific Contact Map: Arg to G, Asn to A, etc.]
    E --> G[Sequence-Dependent Geometry: Roll, Twist, Propeller]
    F --> H[Affinity: Kd and Delta G]
    G --> H
    H --> I{Nucleoprotein Complex}
    I -- Activation --> J[Recruitment of RNA Pol II and Mediator]
    I -- Repression --> K[Blocking Polymerase or Compacting Chromatin]
    I -- Architectural --> L[Looping, Bending, Remodelling]
    J --> M[Biological Output: Transcript Level]
    K --> M
    L --> M
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram (left-to-right, top-to-bottom):** DNA sequence defines base edges → the DBD “probe” either reads chemistry (direct) or shape (indirect) → both converge on a *free-energy-driven* affinity → the resulting nucleoprotein complex performs one of three functional roles (activation, repression, or architectural remodelling) → a transcript-level biological output is produced.

## 4.2 Experimental Detection Pipeline (Block-Level Architecture)

A second Mermaid diagram captures the *experimental* logic — i.e., **how a KTU student or research scientist actually measures** protein–DNA binding. This complements the conceptual map above.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P[Purified DNA-Binding Protein] --> S1[In Vitro Binding Reaction with Labelled DNA Oligo]
    S1 --> S2{Detection Method}
    S2 -- EMSA or Gel Shift --> S3[Band Shift on Native PAGE]
    S2 -- DNase I or Hydroxyl Radical Footprint --> S4[Protected Region on Sequencing Gel]
    S2 -- ChIP or ChIP-seq --> S5[Crosslink, Sonicate, IP, Sequence]
    S2 -- SELEX or Bind-seq --> S6[Iterative Selection of High-Affinity Aptamers]
    S2 -- SPR or ITC --> S7[Real-Time Kinetics or Thermodynamics]
    S3 --> Q[Quantify Kd from Band Intensity]
    S4 --> Q
    S5 --> R[Genome-Wide Binding Map]
    S6 --> T[Consensus Motif and PWM]
    S7 --> U[Delta H, Delta S, Delta G, Kd, Kon, Koff]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why two diagrams?** Module 3 of *PEBBT756* is internally bipartite — it requires (1) *conceptual* fluency (Section 4.1) and (2) *experimental* literacy (Section 4.2). KTU questions frequently test both in the same 14-mark Part-B answer.

## 4.3 Direct vs Indirect Readout — Side-by-Side Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph DIRECT[Direct Readout: Base-Chemistry Recognition]
        D1[Arg Side Chain] -- H-bond to G O6 and N7 --> BS1[Guanine Edge]
        D2[Asn Side Chain] -- H-bond to A N6 and N7 --> BS2[Adenine Edge]
        D3[Gln Side Chain] -- H-bond to T O4 --> BS3[Thymine Edge]
    end
    subgraph INDIRECT[Indirect Readout: Shape Recognition]
        I1[A-tract Phased in TpA Steps] -- High Propeller Twist --> GE1[Pre-Bent DNA]
        I2[CpG-Rich Region] -- High Roll --> GE2[Stiff Helix]
        I3[CA-TG Dinucleotides] -- Negative Slide --> GE3[Compressed Minor Groove]
    end
    DIRECT --> OUT[High-Affinity Specific Complex]
    INDIRECT --> OUT
```

## 4.4 Cooperativity Landscape (Hill Curve)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    H0[Non-Cooperative: Hill n = 1] --> H1[Hyperbolic Binding Curve]
    H2[Cooperative: Hill n = 2] --> H3[Sigmoidal Switch Curve]
    H4[Anti-Cooperative: Hill n less than 1] --> H5[Shallow Hyperbola]
    H1 --> R[Biological Implication: Graded Response]
    H3 --> R2[Biological Implication: Decision Switch]
    H5 --> R3[Biological Implication: Buffer Against Excess Ligand]
```

> [!NOTE]
> The **switch-like** character of cooperative TF binding (n > 1) is the basis of bistable genetic switches in development (e.g., the *lac* operon, lysis–lysogeny in phage λ, and PU.1/GATA1 cross-antagonism in haematopoiesis). Indirect-readout **shape sensors** such as HMGB1 frequently *aid* cooperativity by bending the DNA into a geometry that lowers the $\Delta G^\circ$ penalty for the second TF to bind.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — 3-Mark Short-Answer Questions (with Model Answers)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]** *“Distinguish between direct readout and indirect readout in protein–DNA recognition. Give one example of a protein that uses each.”* **[3 Marks]**

**Model Answer (verbatim style expected by the KTU examiner):**
- **Direct readout** is recognition of DNA base-pair *chemistry* (donor/acceptor pattern) by amino-acid side chains via H-bonds or van der Waals contacts. The DNA geometry is secondary. *Example:* the **λ repressor** reads the major-groove half-sites via its helix–turn–helix motif, forming base-specific H-bonds (Glu to G, Ser to A).
- **Indirect readout** is recognition of DNA *shape* (bend, twist, roll, minor-groove width) encoded by the sequence. *Example:* the **E. coli CAP (CRP)** protein bends its target DNA by ~30°; the bending is *sequence-preferred* and the protein essentially “senses” the intrinsic curvature of AT-rich flanks.
- *Bonus mark:* the third mark is for the *definition* of the recognition element (e.g., “major-groove edges” or “DNA bendability”).

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** *“Why is the major groove of B-DNA preferred by most sequence-specific DNA-binding proteins?”* **[3 Marks]**

**Model Answer:**
1. The major groove is **wider (~12 Å)** than the minor groove (~6 Å) and can accommodate an **α-helix** of ~10 Å diameter, the most common DNA-recognition element.
2. Major-groove edges of A·T and G·C base pairs display **distinct, asymmetric patterns** of H-bond donors, acceptors, and methyl groups (more information per base pair than the minor groove).
3. The pattern is **read in a single-groove sweep** by one helix without requiring DNA distortion, allowing a higher $K_a$ with smaller entropic cost.
*One diagram illustrating Arg–G O6/N7 + Asn–A N6/N7 contacts may earn the third mark.*

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> As per KTU 2024 ESE pattern, the student must answer **ONE out of TWO** questions from this module. Each 14-mark question is divided into **(a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks**.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam – May 2024 pattern]

**(a)** Describe the **helix–turn–helix (HTH)** and **zinc-finger** DNA-binding motifs. Compare their DNA-recognition strategies with suitable examples. **[7 Marks]**

**Model Answer (Examiner's expected key points):**
- HTH consists of two α-helices separated by a short β-turn; the second helix (H3) is the *recognition helix* that inserts into the major groove. Example: **Lac repressor** (operon), **λ Cro**.
- Cys₂His₂ zinc-finger has ββα topology: a Zn²⁺ ion is tetrahedrally coordinated by 2 Cys + 2 His; the α-helix is the recognition element. Found as tandem arrays (e.g., **TFIIIA**, **Sp1**, **Zif268**).
- *Comparison (1 mark):* HTH typically operates as a dimer reading inverted-repeat half-sites; zinc fingers read consecutive 3-bp subsites in a modular fashion.
- *Engineering relevance (1 mark):* zinc fingers are the historical basis of engineered ZFNs; HTH is the basis of synthetic transcription factors.
- A labelled Mermaid / hand-drawn diagram of either motif earns a *bonus mark*.

**(b)** A transcription factor binds its 12-bp site with a Hill coefficient $n = 1.8$ and $K_d = 2$ nM. Calculate the fractional occupancy at free-DNA concentrations of 0.5 nM, 2 nM, and 50 nM. Comment on the cooperativity. **[7 Marks]**

**Model Answer:**

Use the Hill equation:
$$
f = \frac{[D]^n}{K_d^{\,n} + [D]^n}
$$

**Case 1: [D] = 0.5 nM**
$$
f = \frac{(0.5)^{1.8}}{(2)^{1.8} + (0.5)^{1.8}} = \frac{0.287}{3.482 + 0.287} = \frac{0.287}{3.769} \approx 0.076 = 7.6\%
$$

**Case 2: [D] = 2 nM = $K_d$**
$$
f = \frac{(2)^{1.8}}{(2)^{1.8} + (2)^{1.8}} = 0.50 = 50\%
$$

**Case 3: [D] = 50 nM**
$$
f = \frac{(50)^{1.8}}{(2)^{1.8} + (50)^{1.8}} = \frac{1{,}398.0}{3.482 + 1{,}398.0} \approx 0.9975 = 99.75\%
$$

*Comment:* The $n > 1$ value yields a *sigmoidal* response. A 100-fold increase in [D] (0.5 → 50 nM) drives occupancy from 7.6 % to 99.75 % — i.e., the response is *switch-like* rather than linear. This cooperativity is biologically significant: it enables **digital gene-regulatory decisions** (on/off) from graded input signals (TF concentration).

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 pattern]

**(a)** Explain the **thermodynamic basis of protein–DNA interaction**. Derive the relationship between $\Delta G^\circ$ and $K_a$, and discuss the relative contributions of $\Delta H^\circ$ and $\Delta S^\circ$. **[7 Marks]**

**Model Answer:**

- Start with the **Gibbs free energy** definition: $\Delta G^\circ = \Delta H^\circ - T \Delta S^\circ$ **[1 mark]**.
- Equilibrium constant: $K_a = e^{-\Delta G^\circ / RT}$ **[1 mark]**.
- Detailed derivation of $\Delta G^\circ = -RT \ln K_a$ (see Section 3.1) **[2 marks]**.
- $\Delta H^\circ$ contributions: van der Waals, H-bonds (exothermic, negative $\Delta H^\circ$); counter-balanced by **positive** $\Delta H^\circ$ from desolvation of polar groups. For protein–DNA, the net $\Delta H^\circ$ is typically **moderately negative** (–10 to –30 kcal/mol). **[1 mark]**
- $\Delta S^\circ$ contributions: bimolecular association gives a *negative* cratic entropy (–$T \Delta S^\circ > 0$); however, the release of *ordered water* and *cations* from the DNA phosphate and the protein surface gives a large *positive* entropy gain. Net $\Delta S^\circ$ is often **slightly positive** for high-affinity complexes. **[1 mark]**
- Conclusion: **enthalpy–entropy compensation** dominates; high-affinity protein–DNA binding is generally **enthalpy-driven at low [salt] and entropy-driven at high [salt]**, with a strongly negative **heat-capacity change $\Delta C_p < 0$** signalling burial of hydrophobic / aromatic surface. **[1 mark]**

**(b)** Outline the principle, procedure, and applications of **Chromatin Immunoprecipitation (ChIP)** and **DNase I Footprinting** in mapping protein–DNA interactions. Compare their resolutions. **[7 Marks]**

**Model Answer:**

**ChIP (4 marks):**
- *Principle:* covalent crosslinking (formaldehyde) of proteins to DNA *in vivo* or *in situ* preserves native TF–DNA contacts; sonication shears the chromatin to ~200–500 bp; immunoprecipitation with an antibody specific to the TF (or to a tagged version) enriches TF-bound fragments; reversal of crosslinks, DNA purification, and identification by **qPCR** (ChIP-qPCR) or **next-generation sequencing** (ChIP-seq) gives a genome-wide binding map.
- *Resolution:* **single-base** (ChIP-seq peak summit) but limited by sonication size (~200 bp).
- *Applications:* TF-binding atlas construction, histone-mark profiling (H3K4me3, H3K27ac), time-resolved binding during differentiation.

**DNase I Footprinting (3 marks):**
- *Principle:* DNase I non-specifically cleaves DNA at every phosphodiester bond; the region *protected* by a bound protein is **not** cleaved and is visualised as a “gap” on a sequencing gel.
- *Procedure:* end-label one DNA strand with ³²P; incubate with the TF; partially digest with DNase I; resolve on a denaturing polyacrylamide gel; compare with a Maxam–Gilbert sequencing lane.
- *Resolution:* **single nucleotide** within the protected region (~10–30 bp), but only at *one chosen locus* — much lower throughput than ChIP-seq.
- *Comparison:* ChIP = genome-wide, *in vivo*, low-to-moderate resolution. Footprint = locus-specific, *in vitro*, high resolution.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner’s Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where KTU students most often lose marks in this topic:**
> 1. **Confusing $K_a$ and $K_d$.** Always state explicitly that $K_d = 1/K_a$; substituting the wrong way around causes a **sign error** that costs 2–3 marks.
> 2. **Forgetting the standard-state convention.** $K_a$ in $\Delta G^\circ = -RT \ln K_a$ is **dimensionless** (mole-fraction) or referenced to 1 M; the KTU key requires you to mention the standard-state concentration.
> 3. **Mixing up the H-bond edge of the base pair.** A·T and G·C have *different* edges in major vs minor grooves. Many students wrongly write “G has two H-bond donors in the major groove” — it has one (cytosine exocyclic NH₂) plus two acceptors (O6, N7).
> 4. **Omitting units.** $K_d$ in M (or nM), $\Delta G^\circ$ in kcal mol$^{-1}$ (or kJ mol$^{-1}$). Missing units = 0.5 mark deduction per occurrence.
> 5. **Treating $K_d$ as the concentration of free DNA needed for half-saturation, but assuming $K_d = K_d^{\,n}$ in the Hill equation.** Always raise $K_d$ to the power $n$ when using the Hill form.
> 6. **Neglecting to draw a labelled diagram in 7+7-markers.** Per KTU valuation key, an unlabelled diagram is worth 0; a labelled diagram can fetch **+1 to +2 bonus marks** if the rest of the answer is sound.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final 60-second pre-exam revision card — read this aloud twice before entering the exam hall.**

- [ ] **Two readouts** — *direct* (chemistry of base edges) and *indirect* (sequence-encoded shape). Both usually contribute.
- [ ] **Major groove is the major information channel** for sequence-specific TFs (width ~12 Å, accommodates an α-helix).
- [ ] **Common DNA-binding motifs** — HTH, Cys₂His₂ zinc finger, bZIP, bHLH, HMG-box, homeodomain, winged-helix, β-sheet (TBP). Know one example protein for each.
- [ ] **B-DNA geometry** — pitch 10.5 bp/turn, rise 3.4 Å/bp, major groove 12 Å, minor groove 6 Å.
- [ ] **Affinity metric** — $K_d$ in nM; $\Delta G^\circ = -RT \ln K_a$; affinity of specific TFs is 10⁻⁹ to 10⁻¹² M.
- [ ] **Cooperativity** — Hill equation $f = [D]^n / (K_d^{\,n} + [D]^n)$; $n > 1$ ⇒ sigmoidal (switch-like); $n = 1$ ⇒ hyperbolic (graded).
- [ ] **Mutational analysis** — $\Delta\Delta G^\circ = RT \ln(K_d^{mut}/K_d^{wt})$; each lost H-bond costs ~0.5–2.5 kcal mol⁻¹.
- [ ] **van't Hoff** — $\ln K_a = -\Delta H^\circ / RT + \Delta S^\circ / R$; plot $\ln K_a$ vs $1/T$ for $\Delta H^\circ$.
- [ ] **Methods** — EMSA (qualitative + $K_d$ by titration), DNase I footprinting (single-locus, nt-resolution), ChIP/ChIP-seq (genome-wide, in vivo), SELEX (de novo motif discovery), ITC/SPR (full thermodynamics/kinetics), Y1H (TF-promoter reporter).
- [ ] **Biological roles** — activation, repression, replication, recombination, repair, chromatin packaging (histones), architectural bending (HMG, IHF).
- [ ] **Engineering tools** — ZFNs, TALENs, CRISPR–Cas9 are direct technological descendants of natural protein–DNA interaction chemistry.
- [ ] **One golden line to quote in the exam:** *“Specificity of a protein–DNA interaction is an emergent property of direct (chemical) and indirect (geometric) readout, modulated by water structure, ion atmosphere, and cooperative assembly.”* — use this as the opening line of any 14-mark essay to anchor your answer.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Identification of Ligand Receptor Pairing and Transcriptional Regulators
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBBT756 - PROTEOMICS & PROTEIN ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Identification of Ligand Receptor Pairing and Transcriptional Regulators

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Ligand–Receptor (L–R) Pairing — Formal Definition

In cellular proteomics, a **ligand** is a biomolecule (typically a protein, peptide, hormone, cytokine, or small molecule) that binds with high specificity to a **receptor** — usually a transmembrane protein, a nuclear receptor, or a co-receptor scaffold. The act of **identification of a ligand–receptor pair (L–R pairing)** refers to the systematic, experimental (or computational) determination of *which ligand engages which receptor*, the topology of engagement, the stoichiometry of the complex, and the downstream signaling consequence.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (verbatim for exam use):**
> *"Ligand–receptor pairing is the experimental or in-silico process of determining cognate physical interactions between secreted/extracellular ligands and their cell-surface or intracellular receptors using affinity-based, mass-spectrometric, genetic-reporter, or single-cell multi-omic readouts."*

The interaction obeys classical biochemical binding principles characterized by the **dissociation constant $K_d$** (typical physiological range: **$\mathbf{10^{-9} \text{ to } 10^{-6} \text{ M}}$**) and the **association rate constant $k_{\text{on}}$ ($\text{M}^{-1}\text{s}^{-1}$)** / **dissociation rate constant $k_{\text{off}}$ ($\text{s}^{-1}$)**.

## 1.2 Transcriptional Regulators (TRs) — Formal Definition

A **transcriptional regulator** is any *trans*-acting protein factor that modulates the rate of transcription of a target gene by binding (directly or indirectly) to *cis*-regulatory DNA elements (promoters, enhancers, silencers, insulators) or by recruiting the basal transcriptional machinery. TRs are sub-classified as:

- **Sequence-specific DNA-binding TFs** (e.g., p53, NF-κB, MYC, GATA1) — bind to defined motifs (~6–20 bp).
- **General/co-factors** (e.g., Mediator complex, TFIID, TBP).
- **Epigenetic regulators** (e.g., histone acetyltransferases, methyltransferases, chromatin remodelers).
- **Non-coding RNA-associated regulators** (e.g., lncRNA-bound TFs).

> [!NOTE]
> Identifying a TR involves three core questions a KTU examiner loves to ask:
> 1. **WHO binds?** → identity of the protein
> 2. **WHERE does it bind?** → genomic locus / *cis*-element
> 3. **WHAT does it do?** → activate, repress, or remodel

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The Cellular "Postal Service"

Imagine the cell as a **giant city**, the extracellular space as the **air**, and proteins as **envelopes and postmen**:

- **Ligands** = *Letters* addressed to a specific house number.
- **Receptors** = *Letterboxes mounted on houses* — only the right letter fits the right slot.
- **Signaling cascade** = the *chain of people who read and act on the letter* inside the house.
- **Transcriptional Regulators** = the *city hall clerks* who, upon receiving instructions, decide which *city records (genes)* to update or stamp (transcribe).

Identifying the L–R pair is like **forensic mail-tracing**: which letter (ligand) was delivered to which letterbox (receptor)? Identifying TRs is like **finding the city clerk** who stamped a specific policy document (gene) and figuring out *where* their stamp is on it.

## 1.4 Why This Module Matters in Proteomics

Proteomics is the *system-wide study of proteins*, their **interactions**, **modifications**, and **functions**. Mapping L–R pairs and TR networks is a cornerstone because:

1. **It defines the "interactome"** — the wiring diagram of the cell.
2. **It explains cell–cell communication** in tissues, immune synapses, and tumor microenvironments.
3. **It underpins drug discovery** — ~60 % of FDA-approved drugs target receptors or TFs.
4. **It drives personalized medicine** — patient-specific ligand/receptor/TF expression informs therapy choice (e.g., trastuzumab for HER2+ breast cancer).

> [!TIP]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** The KTU 2024 PEBBT756 Module 3 emphasizes **experimental proteomic technologies** for these identifications — particularly yeast-two-hybrid (Y2H), affinity-purification MS (AP-MS), crosslinking-MS (XL-MS), ChIP-seq, and modern single-cell LR inference tools.

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Identification of Ligand–Receptor Pairs — Methodological Framework

The experimental strategies fall into **five functional pillars**:

### Pillar 1 — Affinity-Based Biochemical Pull-Down
- **Co-Immunoprecipitation (Co-IP):** Antibody against receptor pulls down bound ligand (and complex) from lysate. Co-eluting proteins identified by **SDS-PAGE + MS/MS** or directly by **LC-MS/MS**.
- **Affinity Purification (AP) tags:** Receptor fused to FLAG/HA/GFP/Strep-tag II; ligand from cell lysate is captured; bound proteins eluted and identified.
- **Pull-down with biotinylated ligand:** Ligand is biotinylated in vitro, immobilized on **streptavidin beads**, used as bait.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why it works:** The bait is a *physiological* complex — preserves native post-translational modifications (PTMs).

### Pillar 2 — Yeast Two-Hybrid (Y2H) System
- **Principle:** Gal4 transcription factor is split into **DNA-Binding Domain (DBD)** and **Activation Domain (AD)**.
- **Bait** = DBD-X fusion; **Prey** = AD-Y fusion.
- Interaction between X and Y reconstitutes a functional Gal4, driving a **reporter gene** (e.g., HIS3, lacZ, ADE2).
- **Library-scale Y2H** screens 10⁶–10⁸ prey clones to identify novel partners.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Pitfall:** Y2H is performed in *yeast* (eukaryotic but distinct PTM machinery) → **false negatives** for PTM-dependent mammalian interactions; **false positives** from "sticky" proteins that non-specifically activate transcription.

### Pillar 3 — Mass-Spectrometry-Centric Methods
| Method | Crosslinker | Distance Captured | Strength |
|---|---|---|---|
| **AP-MS** | None | Whole complex | High-throughput, native |
| **XL-MS (e.g., DSS, BS³)** | Amine-reactive | ~7.7 Å (DSS) | Direct binary contact |
| **SPROX / FPOP** | OH radical | Solvent-accessible residues | Conformational |
| **RIME (Rapid IP followed by MS)** | Formaldehyde | Direct chromatin contacts | TF co-complexes |

### Pillar 4 — In Silico / Single-Cell Inference
- **CellPhoneDB, NicheNet, CellChat, Connectome, LIANA** — tools that integrate **single-cell RNA-seq (scRNA-seq)** data with curated L–R databases to predict *which cell type expresses the ligand* and *which expresses the receptor*.
- **Statistical test:** empirical permutation of cluster labels → generate a *null distribution* of mean L–R expression → p-value.

### Pillar 5 — Reconstitution & Functional Validation
- **Surface Plasmon Resonance (SPR)** → real-time kinetic $k_{\text{on}}$, $k_{\text{off}}$, $K_d$.
- **Isothermal Titration Calorimetry (ITC)** → thermodynamic $\Delta G$, $\Delta H$, $\Delta S$.
- **Microfluidic co-culture + LIPSTIC** (Labeling Immune Partnerships by SorTagging Intercellular Contacts) → covalent tagging of interacting partners in living cells.

---

## 2.2 Identification of Transcriptional Regulators — Methodological Framework

### Step 1 — Establishing Genomic Binding Sites
- **ChIP-seq** (Chromatin Immunoprecipitation + sequencing): TF is crosslinked to DNA (formaldehyde), sheared, immunoprecipitated, sequenced → peaks = binding sites.
- **ChIP-exo / ChIP-nexus** → single-base resolution.
- **CUT&RUN** and **CUT&Tag** → in situ antibody-tethered nuclease cleavage; **lower background, fewer cells** (10⁴–10⁵ vs 10⁶–10⁷).
- **ATAC-seq** → maps *open chromatin* (potential regulatory regions).

### Step 2 — Identifying the TR Itself
- **ChIP-MS / RIME** → co-IP of chromatin-bound complexes → identify associated TRs and co-factors.
- **DNA Affinity Purification (DAP-MS / DAP-seq):** biotinylated DNA oligo containing a *cis*-element pulls down TFs from nuclear extract → MS identification.
- **Yeast One-Hybrid (Y1H):** DNA bait upstream of reporter; cDNA-AD library screened for binding proteins.
- **EMSA (Electrophoretic Mobility Shift Assay):** radiolabeled/fluorescent DNA probe + nuclear extract → band shift indicates TF binding. Supershift with specific antibody confirms identity.

### Step 3 — Functional Consequence
- **Reporter gene assays:** *cis*-element cloned upstream of luciferase/GFP → measure TF effect.
- **STARR-seq** (Self-Transcribing Active Regulatory Region Sequencing): genome-wide quantification of enhancer activity.
- **CRISPRi / CRISPRa screens** → loss/gain of function coupled to single-cell readout (Perturb-seq, CRISP-seq).
- **scRNA-seq trajectory analysis** (e.g., Monocle, PAGA) → infers TR-driven cell-state transitions *in silico*; validated with **TF activity inference tools (DoRothEA, SCENIC)**.

---

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **MASTER THIS TABLE** — direct questions are asked in KTU ESE from these equations.

| # | Formula / Concept | Expression | Variables & Units | Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Equilibrium dissociation constant** | $K_d = \dfrac{[L][R]}{[LR]}$ | $K_d$ in M; $[L], [R], [LR]$ in M | L–R binding strength |
| 2 | **Fractional occupancy (Langmuir)** | $\theta = \dfrac{[L]}{K_d + [L]}$ | dimensionless | Receptor saturation curve |
| 3 | **Association rate relation** | $K_d = \dfrac{k_{\text{off}}}{k_{\text{on}}}$ | $k_{\text{on}}$: M⁻¹ s⁻¹; $k_{\text{off}}$: s⁻¹ | Kinetics (SPR, ITC) |
| 4 | **Hill equation (cooperativity)** | $\theta = \dfrac{[L]^n}{K_d^n + [L]^n}$ | $n$ = Hill coefficient | Cooperative L–R binding |
| 5 | **Free energy of binding** | $\Delta G = RT \ln K_d$ | $\Delta G$ in J/mol; $R = 8.314$ J mol⁻¹ K⁻¹ | Thermodynamics |
| 6 | **Enthalpy–entropy** | $\Delta G = \Delta H - T\Delta S$ | J/mol | ITC analysis |
| 7 | **ChIP-seq enrichment (MACS)** | $\lambda_{\text{local}} = \max(0, \lambda_{\text{genome}} - \lambda_{\text{bg}})$ | Poisson $\lambda$ parameter | Peak calling |
| 8 | **Fold-enrichment** | $\text{FE} = \dfrac{\text{IP reads}}{\text{Input reads}}$ | dimensionless | ChIP quality control |
| 9 | **FDR (Benjamini–Hochberg)** | $p_{(i)} \le \dfrac{i}{m}\alpha$ | $\alpha$ = significance; $m$ = total tests | Multiple testing in MS |
| 10 | **False Discovery Rate (FDR)** | $\text{FDR} = \dfrac{FP}{FP + TP}$ | dimensionless | MS identifications |
| 11 | **SNR (ChIP-seq)** | $\text{SNR} = \dfrac{\mu_{\text{peak}} - \mu_{\text{bg}}}{\sigma_{\text{bg}}}$ | dimensionless | Quality control |
| 12 | **Permutation p-value (single-cell LR)** | $p = \dfrac{1 + r}{1 + N}$ | $r$ = rank; $N$ = permutations | CellPhoneDB test |
| 13 | **Ostwald ripening (kinetic trap)** | $\dfrac{dr}{dt} = \dfrac{K}{r^2}$ | $r$ = particle radius | Crystallography (L–R complex) |
| 14 | **Mass accuracy (FT-MS)** | $\Delta m = \dfrac{m}{\text{Resolving Power}}$ | ppm | TF identification |
| 15 | **Y2H HIS3 background (3-AT titration)** | $K_i^{3\text{-AT}} \approx 1$ mM | mM | Stringency tuning |

> [!NOTE]
> The **most-tested formula in KTU ESE for this module** is the **Langmuir binding isotherm (Eq. 2)** and the **equilibrium dissociation constant (Eq. 1)**. Memorize both — a 3-mark short-answer question is almost guaranteed.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Valuation Rule:** Derivations carry **5 of 14 marks** in long-answer questions. *Show every algebraic step.* Skipping steps = losing 1–2 marks per jump.

---

## 3.1 Derivation of the Langmuir Binding Isotherm

### Starting Premise
Receptor $R$ and ligand $L$ form a reversible 1:1 complex $LR$:

$$ R + L \;\underset{k_{\text{off}}}{\overset{k_{\text{on}}}{\rightleftharpoons}}\; LR $$

At equilibrium, the **dissociation constant** is defined as:

$$ K_d = \frac{[L][R]}{[LR]} \quad \text{(Definition, by mass-action law)} $$

The **total receptor concentration** $[R]_T$ is the sum of free and bound:

$$ [R]_T = [R] + [LR] \quad \text{(Conservation of receptor)} $$

### Step 1 — Express $[R]$ from conservation

$$ [R] = [R]_T - [LR] $$

### Step 2 — Substitute into the $K_d$ expression

$$ K_d = \frac{[L]\big([R]_T - [LR]\big)}{[LR]} $$

### Step 3 — Solve for $[LR]$

$$ K_d \cdot [LR] = [L] \cdot [R]_T - [L] \cdot [LR] $$

Group $[LR]$ terms on the left-hand side:

$$ [LR] \big(K_d + [L]\big) = [L] \cdot [R]_T $$

$$ [LR] = \frac{[L] \cdot [R]_T}{K_d + [L]} $$

### Step 4 — Define fractional occupancy

The fractional occupancy $\theta$ is the fraction of receptors that are bound:

$$ \theta \equiv \frac{[LR]}{[R]_T} $$

Dividing both sides of the Step-3 result by $[R]_T$:

$$ \theta = \frac{[L]}{K_d + [L]} \quad \text{(Langmuir Isotherm)} $$

This is the **central binding equation** used in SPR, ITC, ELISA, and ChIP-seq peak quantification.

### Step 5 — Limiting cases (often asked in KTU 3-mark questions)

- **Case A:** When $[L] = K_d$ → $\theta = 0.5$ → *50 % receptor occupancy* (this is the operational definition of $K_d$).
- **Case B:** When $[L] \ll K_d$ → $\theta \approx \dfrac{[L]}{K_d}$ → **linear regime** (used in radio-ligand binding assays).
- **Case C:** When $[L] \gg K_d$ → $\theta \to 1$ → **saturation** (used in Scatchard plot extrapolation).

---

## 3.2 Derivation of the Free Energy of Binding

From the relationship between equilibrium constant and Gibbs free energy:

$$ \Delta G = -RT \ln K_a $$

where $K_a$ is the **association constant** and the **inverse of $K_d$**:

$$ K_a = \frac{1}{K_d} $$

Substituting:

$$ \Delta G = -RT \ln\!\left(\frac{1}{K_d}\right) = RT \ln K_d $$

### Step-by-step expansion with numerical example

Suppose an L–R pair has $K_d = 10^{-9}$ M at $T = 298$ K:

$$ \Delta G = (8.314 \text{ J mol}^{-1}\text{K}^{-1})(298 \text{ K})\ln(10^{-9}) $$

$$ \ln(10^{-9}) = -9 \times \ln(10) = -9 \times 2.3026 = -20.723 $$

$$ \Delta G = (2477.6 \text{ J/mol})(-20.723) = -51{,}358 \text{ J/mol} = -51.4 \text{ kJ/mol} $$

> [!NOTE]
> A binding free energy of **$\mathbf{-51.4 \text{ kJ/mol}}$** indicates a *high-affinity* L–R pair (typical $K_d$ in low nM range). Physiological strong interactions are typically -40 to -70 kJ/mol.

---

## 3.3 Derivation of the Hill Equation (Cooperative Binding)

For a multi-site receptor with $n$ binding sites, the cooperative binding is:

$$ \theta = \frac{[L]^n}{K_d^n + [L]^n} $$

### Step 1 — Hill plot
Take the logarithm of the rearrangement:

$$ \frac{\theta}{1 - \theta} = \frac{[L]^n}{K_d^n} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \log\!\left(\frac{\theta}{1-\theta}\right) = n \log[L] - n \log K_d $$

The slope of $\log\!\left(\dfrac{\theta}{1-\theta}\right)$ vs $\log[L]$ is the **Hill coefficient $n$**:
- $n = 1$ → non-cooperative (Langmuir).
- $n > 1$ → positive cooperativity (e.g., hemoglobin O₂ binding, $n \approx 2.8$).
- $n < 1$ → negative cooperativity.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Y2H Positive-Prediction Scoring

Y2H positives are typically scored by **three readouts** (commonly used in HT-Y2H, e.g., HuRI, HuMAP):

### Readout 1 — Growth on selective medium (HIS3, ADE2)
- Quantitative: $\text{OD}_{600}$ after 24 h.
- Score: $S_1 = \text{OD}_{\text{selective}} / \text{OD}_{\text{non-selective}}$ → ratio ≥ 0.5 considered positive.

### Readout 2 — β-galactosidase (lacZ) reporter
- Miller Units: $U = 1000 \times \dfrac{A_{420}}{t \cdot V \cdot A_{600}}$
- Score: $S_2 = \log_2(U_{\text{sample}} / U_{\text{empty}})$.

### Readout 3 — Dual-selection with 3-AT (3-amino-1,2,4-triazole) stringency
- $S_3 = $ highest [3-AT] (mM) supporting growth.

### Composite Confidence Score (used in HuRI / HuMAP)
$$ \text{Confidence} = w_1 S_1 + w_2 S_2 + w_3 S_3 $$

where typical weights $w_1 = 0.3$, $w_2 = 0.5$, $w_3 = 0.2$ (give β-gal highest weight due to quantitative nature). **PPV (positive predictive value)** is then calibrated against a gold-standard set (e.g., CORUM complexes).

---

## 3.5 ChIP-seq Peak-Calling Logic (MACS2 Model)

The MACS2 algorithm models ChIP-seq tag counts using a **dynamic Poisson distribution**:

$$ P(X = k) = \frac{\lambda^k e^{-\lambda}}{k!} $$

### Step 1 — Local lambda estimation
For each candidate peak, $\lambda_{\text{local}}$ is the background tag count in a 1 kb (or 5–10 kb) flanking window.

### Step 2 — Effective lambda (lambda BG)
Subtract input control contribution:

$$ \lambda_{\text{bg}} = \max\!\left(0, \lambda_{\text{genome}} - \lambda_{\text{input}}\right) $$

### Step 3 — Enrichment test
The p-value for observing $k$ tags where $\lambda_{\text{bg}}$ is expected:

$$ p = 1 - \sum_{i=0}^{k-1} \frac{\lambda_{\text{bg}}^{i} e^{-\lambda_{\text{bg}}}}{i!} $$

### Step 4 — Multiple-testing correction
Apply Benjamini–Hochberg FDR (Eq. 9 above) at $q < 0.05$.

> [!NOTE]
> This Poisson-framework is mathematically simple but robust; modern tools (MACS2, PeakSeq, HOMER) use **negative binomial** or **hidden Markov models** for improved accuracy.

---

## 3.6 Single-Cell Ligand–Receptor Inference (CellPhoneDB Logic)

### Null model construction
Given scRNA-seq matrices for cell types $A$ (ligand-expressing) and $B$ (receptor-expressing), the mean expression of the L–R pair is:

$$ \bar{x}_{AB} = \text{mean}(L_A + R_B) $$

### Permutation test
Randomly permute cluster labels $N = 1000$ times, recompute $\bar{x}$ for each permutation:

$$ \bar{x}^{(1)}, \bar{x}^{(2)}, \ldots, \bar{x}^{(1000)} $$

### Empirical p-value

$$ p = \frac{1 + \sum_{i=1}^{N} \mathbb{1}\!\left(\bar{x}^{(i)} \geq \bar{x}_{AB}\right)}{1 + N} $$

Pairs with $p < 0.05$ are flagged as **significant L–R interactions**.

> [!TIP]
> This is a **gold-standard analytical framework** for KTU viva questions on scRNA-seq. Be ready to derive the empirical p-value on the board.

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Yeast Two-Hybrid (Y2H) Screening Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Bait Protein X fused to Gal4 DBD on BD Plasmid"] --> C["Co-transform into Yeast Reporter Strain"]
    B["Prey cDNA Library fused to Gal4 AD on AD Plasmid"] --> C
    C --> D["Plate on SD Leu Trp His plus 3 AT"]
    D --> E["Colonies with X Y interaction grow"]
    E --> F["Beta galactosidase filter lift assay"]
    F --> G["Sequence Prey Plasmid from Positive Clones"]
    G --> H["BLAST against NCBI nr"]
    H --> I["Identify Ligand Receptor Partner of Bait X"]
```

**Interpretation:**
- Each colony that grows on the selective medium encodes a prey protein Y that physically interacts with bait X.
- The DBD-X is the *receptor side* (often the cytoplasmic tail); the AD-Y is the *ligand side* (or vice versa).
- 3-AT stringency eliminates background HIS3 leakiness.

---

## 4.2 ChIP-seq Identification of Transcriptional Regulators

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["Crosslink TFs to DNA Formaldehyde"] --> B["Sonication Shear 200 500 bp"]
    B --> C["Immunoprecipitate with anti TF Ab"]
    C --> D["Reverse Crosslinks Proteinase K"]
    D --> E["Library Prep Adaptor Ligation"]
    E --> F["Illumina Sequencing"]
    F --> G["Read Mapping to Reference Genome"]
    G --> H["Peak Calling MACS2 Poisson"]
    H --> I["Motif Discovery HOMER MEME"]
    I --> J["Annotation to Promoters Enhancers"]
    J --> K["List of TF Binding Sites and Target Genes"]
```

**Interpretation:**
- The TF antibody is the *critical reagent* — its specificity determines the data quality.
- Motif discovery at peak summits confirms the *cognate binding motif* (e.g., GATA for GATA1, E-box CACGTG for MYC/MAX).
- Peak annotation links the TR to *target genes* → functional hypothesis.

---

## 4.3 Co-Immunoprecipitation Coupled to LC-MS/MS

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Lyse Cells with NP40 Buffer plus Protease Inhibitors"] --> B["Incubate with anti Receptor Ab"]
    B --> C["Capture on Protein A G Beads"]
    C --> D["Wash High Salt plus Detergent"]
    D --> E["Elute Bound Ligand via SDS or Acid"]
    E --> F["Reduce Alkylate Trypsin Digest"]
    F --> G["LC MS MS Q Exactive Orbitrap"]
    G --> H["Database Search MaxQuant Sequest"]
    H --> I["Identify Co purifying Ligand Candidates"]
    I --> J["Filter SAINTexpress FDR less than 1 percent"]
    J --> K["High Confidence Ligand Receptor Pair List"]
```

**Interpretation:**
- SAINT (Significance Analysis of INTeractome) uses **spectral counting** + probabilistic model to score bait–prey interactions.
- FDR < 1 % is the *community standard* for high-confidence AP-MS interactions.

---

## 4.4 Crosslinking Mass Spectrometry (XL-MS) for Direct L–R Contacts

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["Incubate Ligand plus Receptor with DSS BS3 Crosslinker"] --> B["Quench Tris pH 7.5"]
    B --> C["Digest with Trypsin Lys C"]
    C --> D["Fractionate by SCX or SEC"]
    D --> E["LC MS MS HCD MS2"]
    E --> F["Search with pLink MeroX Xi"]
    F --> G["Identify Crosslinked Peptide Pairs"]
    G --> H["Map to 3D Structure AlphaFold Model"]
    H --> I["Pinpoint Direct Contact Residues across L R Interface"]
```

**Interpretation:**
- DSS/BS3 crosslinker has a **Cα–Cα spacer of 7.7 Å**; lysine–lysine crosslinks beyond this distance = **false positive** or indication of conformational flexibility.
- Integration with **AlphaFold-Multimer** or **ClusPro docking** produces a *structural model* of the L–R complex.

---

## 4.5 Single-Cell Ligand–Receptor Inference Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Tumor scRNA seq 10x Chromium"] --> B["Cluster Cells Seurat Scanpy"]
    B --> C["Annotate Cell Types T cell B cell Tumor CAF etc"]
    C --> D["Compute Cluster Mean Expression"]
    D --> E["Query Curated L R Database CellPhoneDB CellChat"]
    E --> F["Permutation Test N 1000"]
    F --> G["Significant L R Pairs P less than 0.05"]
    G --> H["Visualize as Dot Plot Chord Diagram"]
    H --> I["Validate by IF mIHC LIPSTIC"]
```

**Interpretation:**
- This pipeline is **standard** in tumor microenvironment characterization.
- Provides *hypothesis-generation* — wet-lab validation is mandatory for publication-grade work.

---

## 4.6 CUT&RUN / CUT&Tag — Next-Generation TF Mapping

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["Permeabilize Cells with Digitonin"] --> B["Add anti TF Ab"]
    B --> C["Add Protein A Micrococcal Nuclease CUT RUN"]
    C --> D["Activate Ca2 plus MNase Cleaves around TF"]
    D --> E["Release TF Protected Fragments"]
    E --> F["Library Prep"]
    F --> G["Illumina Sequencing"]
    G --> H["Peak Calling SEACR"]
```

> [!TIP]
> CUT&Tag uses **Tn5 transposase** instead of MNase → even lower background, works on **single cells / low inputs** (e.g., 100 cells). Recommended for KTU viva questions on "modern alternatives to ChIP-seq".

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 📘 PART A — Short Answer Questions (2 × 3 = 6 Marks)

### **Question 1 (3 Marks)**
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024, PEBBT756, Module 3]**

Define the term "ligand–receptor pairing" in proteomics. List **any two** high-throughput experimental methods used for its identification.

**Model Answer:**

> **Ligand–receptor pairing** refers to the experimental determination of cognate physical interactions between a secreted/extracellular ligand (e.g., a cytokine, growth factor) and its specific cell-surface or intracellular receptor, characterized by binding affinity ($K_d$).
>
> **Two high-throughput methods:**
> 1. **Yeast Two-Hybrid (Y2H):** Split Gal4-DBD/AD fusion; interaction reconstitutes a transcriptional activator driving a reporter gene.
> 2. **Affinity Purification–Mass Spectrometry (AP-MS):** Receptor (bait) is immunoprecipitated; co-purifying ligands are identified by LC-MS/MS and scored with SAINTexpress.
>
> *(Other acceptable: Co-IP, XL-MS, Phage display, scRNA-seq LR inference.)*

**Examiner's note:** Award 1 mark for definition, 1 mark per method (with brief principle). Total 3.

---

### **Question 2 (3 Marks)**
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, PEBBT756, Module 3]**

What is a transcriptional regulator? Distinguish between **ChIP-seq** and **ATAC-seq** in identifying them.

**Model Answer:**

> A **transcriptional regulator** is a *trans*-acting protein that modulates transcription by binding *cis*-regulatory DNA elements or by recruiting the basal transcriptional machinery.
>
> **ChIP-seq:** Uses an antibody against a specific TF → identifies **direct binding sites** of that TF across the genome.
>
> **ATAC-seq:** Uses Tn5 transposase to map **open/accessible chromatin** → identifies **potentially active regulatory regions** but does *not* directly identify which TF binds.
>
> **Key distinction:** ChIP-seq is *protein-specific*; ATAC-seq is *region-specific*.

---

## 📗 PART B — Long Answer Questions (Choice, 1 × 14 = 14 Marks)

> **KTU 2024 ESE Rule:** Each Part-B question has sub-parts. Attempt **either Question A OR Question B**.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**
**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, PEBBT756 Module 3]**
