**(a) [7 Marks]** Describe the **Yeast Two-Hybrid (Y2H) system** in detail. Explain the principle, the components (DBD, AD, reporter), and the workflow for library screening. List **two limitations** of the technique.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Differentiate between **AP-MS** and **Y2H** for identifying protein–protein interactions, citing at least **three points of contrast**. Briefly describe the SAINT scoring algorithm used in AP-MS.

---

#### ✅ Model Solution — Question A (a)

**Principle (2 marks):**
Y2H exploits the modular nature of the yeast Gal4 transcription factor, which has two separable domains:
- **DNA-Binding Domain (DBD):** binds the *GAL1* upstream activating sequence (UAS).
- **Activation Domain (AD):** recruits the transcriptional machinery.

When fused to two interacting proteins (X = bait, Y = prey), a functional Gal4 is reconstituted, activating a **reporter gene** (HIS3, ADE2, lacZ).

**Components (2 marks):**
- **Bait plasmid:** DBD-X fusion (e.g., pGBKT7).
- **Prey plasmid:** AD-Y fusion (e.g., pGADT7), often a cDNA library.
- **Yeast reporter strain:** e.g., AH109 (carrying HIS3, ADE2, lacZ reporters under Gal4 control).
- **Selective media:** SD-Leu-Trp (maintain plasmids), SD-Leu-Trp-His + 3-AT (stringency).

**Library Screening Workflow (2 marks):**
1. Co-transform bait + library into yeast.
2. Plate on SD-Leu-Trp-His + 3-AT (eliminates auto-activators).
3. Confirm positives with β-gal filter assay.
4. Isolate prey plasmid from positive colonies.
5. Sequence and BLAST against NCBI nr to identify the prey protein.

**Two Limitations (1 mark):**
- **False positives:** "Sticky" proteins non-specifically activate transcription.
- **False negatives:** PTM-dependent (e.g., phosphorylation, glycosylation) interactions may not occur in yeast.
- Nuclear localization: membrane-bound receptors require specialized systems (e.g., **MYTH** — Membrane Yeast Two-Hybrid, **MaMTH**).

---

#### ✅ Model Solution — Question A (b)

**Comparison Table (5 marks):**

| Parameter | **Y2H** | **AP-MS** |
|---|---|---|
| **Setting** | *In vivo* (yeast) | *In vitro* / *ex vivo* (mammalian lysate) |
| **Readout** | Genetic (reporter activation) | Mass-spectrometric (peptide identification) |
| **PTM compatibility** | Limited (yeast PTM machinery) | Native (mammalian PTMs preserved) |
| **Throughput** | Very high (10⁶–10⁸ prey/library) | Moderate (dozens to hundreds of baits/run) |
| **Spatial info** | None (binary) | Co-complex membership (indirect binary possible) |
| **Cost** | Low (yeast culture) | High (LC-MS instrument time) |

**SAINT Algorithm (2 marks):**
- **SAINT** (Significance Analysis of INTeractome) uses a **Bayesian framework** to model spectral counts of prey proteins in AP-MS.
- Compares observed counts to a **null distribution** generated from control IPs (e.g., GFP-bait or empty-bead samples).
- Outputs a **probability score (SAINT score)** for each bait–prey pair; pairs with SAINT ≥ 0.8 (or FDR ≤ 1 %) are considered **high-confidence interactors**.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** *(Alternative Choice)*
**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, PEBBT756 Module 3]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the **ChIP-seq** technique for identifying transcriptional regulator binding sites. Describe the steps from crosslinking to peak calling, and discuss **two advantages** of CUT&RUN over ChIP-seq.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Derive the **Langmuir binding isotherm** for a ligand–receptor interaction. Given $K_d = 10^{-7}$ M and $[L] = 5 \times 10^{-8}$ M, calculate the **fractional occupancy $\theta$** of the receptor.

---

#### ✅ Model Solution — Question B (a)

**ChIP-seq Principle (1 mark):**
Chromatin Immunoprecipitation followed by sequencing identifies **genome-wide binding sites** of a specific TF or histone modification.

**Steps (4 marks):**
1. **Crosslinking:** Formaldehyde (1 %, 10 min) crosslinks TFs to DNA.
2. **Chromatin preparation:** Sonication or MNase digestion → 200–500 bp fragments.
3. **Immunoprecipitation:** Anti-TF antibody + Protein A/G beads pull down TF–DNA complexes.
4. **Washing & Elution:** Stringent washes remove non-specific binders; elution releases TF–DNA.
5. **Reverse crosslinking:** Proteinase K + heat (65 °C, overnight).
6. **Library preparation:** End repair, adaptor ligation, PCR amplification.
7. **Sequencing:** Illumina platform, 30–50 million reads per sample.
8. **Peak calling:** MACS2 uses Poisson statistics (Eq. 7, Section 2.3) to identify enriched regions.

**CUT&RUN Advantages (2 marks):**
- **In situ** digestion (no sonication, no IP of soluble chromatin) → **dramatically reduced background**.
- Works with **10⁴ cells** (vs. 10⁶–10⁷ for ChIP-seq) → enables low-input / single-cell applications.
- Faster protocol (~1 day vs. 3 days).
- Higher signal-to-noise ratio (SNR).

---

#### ✅ Model Solution — Question B (b)

**Derivation (5 marks):**
*(See Section 3.1 — full derivation must be reproduced on the answer sheet.)*

**Key steps (re-cap):**
- Start from $K_d = \dfrac{[L][R]}{[LR]}$.
- Conservation: $[R]_T = [R] + [LR]$.
- Solve for $[LR]$, then $\theta = \dfrac{[LR]}{[R]_T} = \dfrac{[L]}{K_d + [L]}$.

**Numerical Calculation (2 marks):**

Given:
- $K_d = 10^{-7}$ M
- $[L] = 5 \times 10^{-8}$ M

Substitute into the Langmuir isotherm:

$$ \theta = \frac{[L]}{K_d + [L]} = \frac{5 \times 10^{-8}}{10^{-7} + 5 \times 10^{-8}} $$

$$ \theta = \frac{5 \times 10^{-8}}{1.5 \times 10^{-7}} = \frac{5}{15} = \frac{1}{3} \approx 0.333 $$

**Answer:** The receptor is **~33.3 % occupied** under the given ligand concentration. *(At $[L] = 0.5 K_d$, exactly one-third occupancy is expected — useful sanity check for KTU problem-solving.)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **🛑 KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout — Part-B Long Answers**
> 1. **Skipping the derivation in numerical questions** = 2-mark loss even if the final answer is correct. Show the formula, substitution, and final value.
> 2. **Confusing ChIP-seq and ChIP-MS** — ChIP-seq finds *where* the TF binds (genomic sites); ChIP-MS finds *who else* is in the TF's chromatin complex (co-factors).
> 3. **Forgetting units** on $K_d$, $k_{\text{on}}$, $\Delta G$ → lose 0.5–1 mark.
> 4. **Not mentioning controls** in Y2H (empty-DBD, auto-activation test, 3-AT titration) → partial credit lost.
> 5. **Stating "high-throughput" without a number** — always cite approximate scale (e.g., Y2H: 10⁶–10⁸ prey clones).
> 6. **Confusing Kd (dissociation constant) with Ka (association constant)** — Ka = 1/Kd, and $\Delta G = -RT \ln K_a$ (not $K_d$).

---

## 🧠 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU ESE Module 3**

- ✅ **Ligand–receptor pairing** = identification of cognate binding between a ligand and its receptor. $K_d$ quantifies affinity (range $10^{-9}$–$10^{-6}$ M for physiological L–R pairs).
- ✅ **Five methodological pillars:** affinity pull-down (Co-IP/AP), Y2H, MS-based (AP-MS, XL-MS, RIME), *in silico* single-cell inference, and biophysical validation (SPR, ITC).
- ✅ **Y2H** uses split Gal4 (DBD + AD). Library scale = 10⁶–10⁸. False positive/negative issues; PTM limitation.
- ✅ **AP-MS** = native complex capture + LC-MS/MS. SAINT/FDR scoring. Works in mammalian cells, preserves PTMs.
- ✅ **XL-MS (DSS/BS3)** = 7.7 Å spacer; gives *direct binary contact residues* — integrates with AlphaFold.
- ✅ **Transcriptional regulators** include sequence-specific TFs, co-factors, epigenetic regulators. Identify via ChIP-seq, ChIP-MS, DAP-MS, Y1H, EMSA.
- ✅ **ChIP-seq** = antibody-specific, formaldehyde crosslinking, MACS2 peak calling (Poisson model, $\lambda_{\text{local}}$).
- ✅ **CUT&RUN / CUT&Tag** = next-gen alternatives; lower background, lower input (10⁴ cells).
- ✅ **ATAC-seq** = open-chromatin landscape (not TF-specific).
- ✅ **scRNA-seq LR inference** = CellPhoneDB, NicheNet, CellChat; empirical permutation p-value.
- ✅ **Langmuir isotherm** $\theta = \dfrac{[L]}{K_d + [L]}$ — most-tested equation. At $[L] = K_d$, $\theta = 0.5$.
- ✅ **Free energy** $\Delta G = RT \ln K_d$ (with $R = 8.314$ J mol⁻¹ K⁻¹).
- ✅ **Hill equation** for cooperativity: $\theta = \dfrac{[L]^n}{K_d^n + [L]^n}$.
- ✅ **ChIP-seq peak-calling** uses **Poisson distribution** with local lambda.
- ✅ **SAINTexpress** = Bayesian scoring of AP-MS interactions; SAINT ≥ 0.8 = high confidence.
- ✅ **Modern tools & databases:** HuRI, HuMAP (Y2H), CORUM, BioGRID, STRING, IntAct, AlphaFold-Multimer, CellPhoneDB v5, LIANA, NicheNet v2.
- ✅ **Always cite controls:** input for ChIP-seq, empty-bead/GFP for AP-MS, auto-activation test for Y2H.
- ✅ **Real-world examples** to memorize: PD-1/PD-L1, EGFR, HER2, p53 targets, MYC E-box, GATA1 GATA motif.
- ✅ **Always quote units** ($K_d$ in M; $\Delta G$ in kJ/mol; $k_{\text{on}}$ in M⁻¹s⁻¹; $k_{\text{off}}$ in s⁻¹).
- ✅ **Examiner-friendly keywords:** *physiological context*, *endogenous vs. exogenous*, *PTM-dependent interaction*, *co-complex vs. direct binary*, *stringency tuning*, *false discovery rate*.

> 🎯 **Final Exam Tip:** When asked to "compare" two methods, always use a **table** with ≥ 4 comparison axes. When asked to "derive", never skip the algebraic rearrangement. When asked to "identify", always cite **one specific tool/database** as an example.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Proteomics in Clinical and Drug Discovery Applications
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEBBT756 - PROTEOMICS & PROTEIN ENGINEERING > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Proteomics in Clinical and Drug Discovery Applications ## SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Clinical Proteomics** is defined as the systematic, large-scale study of the entire protein complement (proteome) of a biological system — including body fluids, tissues, and cells — to identify, quantify, and characterize proteins that serve as **diagnostic biomarkers**, **prognostic indicators**, or **therapeutic targets** for human disease. It integrates high-throughput analytical platforms (mass spectrometry, protein arrays, 2D-gel electrophoresis) with bioinformatics to translate protein-level data into clinically actionable information. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Proteomics in Drug Discovery** is the application of proteome-wide analytical technologies to accelerate and de-risk every stage of the pharmaceutical pipeline — from **target identification** and **validation**, through **lead compound screening**, **mechanism-of-action (MoA) studies**, **toxicity assessment (toxicoproteomics)**, and **pharmacoproteomic stratification** of patient responders versus non-responders. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition > [!NOTE] > **The "Crime Scene Detective" Analogy** > > Imagine a detective arriving at a crime scene. The genome is the **list of every person who lives in the city** (static blueprint). The transcriptome is the **list of people actively walking on the street** (intermediate snapshot). But the **proteome is the actual fingerprints, blood stains, footprints, and tools left behind at the crime scene** — the *real physical evidence* of what actually happened. > > - **Clinical Proteomics** = the forensic scientist comparing fingerprints in diseased vs. healthy tissue to find the *unique molecular signature* of the disease (biomarker). > - **Drug Discovery Proteomics** = the pharmacologist testing which fingerprints (proteins) the new drug molecule touches, blocks, or activates — confirming the drug actually works on the intended biological suspect. ### 1.3 Why Proteomics Cannot Be Replaced by Genomics - **One gene → many proteins** (alternative splicing, PTMs): the genome cannot predict the functional protein diversity. - **Protein levels do not correlate with mRNA levels** (correlation coefficient often $\rho \approx 0.4$). - **Drugs act on proteins, not on DNA/RNA** — ~**96%** of FDA-approved drugs target proteins (enzymes, receptors, ion channels). - **Post-translational modifications (PTMs)** such as phosphorylation, glycosylation, and ubiquitination dictate activity and are only visible at the protein level. ### 1.4 Key Clinical & Pharmaceutical Constants - **~96%** of FDA-approved small-molecule drugs target human proteins. - **~85%** of current drug targets fall into enzyme, receptor, ion-channel, or transporter protein families. - **Average drug development cost**: ~**US $2.6 billion** per approved drug; proteomics aims to **reduce late-stage attrition**. - **Lead time of biomarker development**: typically **5–10 years** from discovery to clinical assay. - **FDR threshold (industry standard)**: **q ≤ 0.01** (1% false discovery rate) for clinical biomarker validation. > [!NOTE] > **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024):** This topic directly maps to **CO4** — *"Apply proteomics tools to clinical diagnostics and pharmaceutical drug discovery pipelines."* Mastery of biomarker workflows and target-validation strategies is a frequent **14-mark** exam question in the End Semester Evaluation (ESE). ## SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET ### 2.1 The Drug Discovery Pipeline — Stage-Wise Proteomics Application The modern pharmaceutical pipeline is a **multi-stage funnel** in which proteomics contributes uniquely at each gate. Below is the KTU-board-required breakdown: | Stage | Objective | Proteomics Tool Deployed | |-------|-----------|--------------------------| | **1. Target Identification** | Find a disease-causing protein | 2D-DIGE, LC-MS/MS, Bioinformatic databases (UniProt, HPRD) | | **2. Target Validation** | Confirm causality | siRNA knockdown + MS proteomics, CRISPR + LFQ, Activity-Based Protein Profiling (ABPP) | | **3. Hit Identification** | Find molecules binding the target | Chemical proteomics (drug affinity pulldown), Protein microarrays | | **4. Lead Optimization** | Improve potency/selectivity | Chemoproteomics, Thermal Proteome Profiling (TPP) | | **5. Preclinical Toxicology** | Predict adverse effects | Toxicoproteomics (organ-specific proteome changes in animal models) | | **6. Clinical Trials (Phase I–III)** | Stratify responders | Pharmacoproteomics (patient proteome correlates with response) | | **7. Post-Market Surveillance** | Monitor real-world efficacy | Biomarker re-validation, companion diagnostic refinement | ### 2.2 Biomarker Discovery — The Three Pillars A clinically validated biomarker must satisfy three rigorous criteria: 1. **Analytical Validity** — measurable reliably across labs (CV < 15%). 2. **Clinical Validity** — distinguishes disease vs. control with high sensitivity & specificity (ROC AUC ≥ 0.85). 3. **Clinical Utility** — alters physician decision-making and improves patient outcome. Biomarker classifications (FDA-NIH BEST categories): - **Diagnostic** — confirms presence of disease (e.g., **PSA** for prostate cancer). - **Prognostic** — predicts disease course (e.g., **HER2/neu** in breast cancer). - **Predictive** — predicts response to therapy (e.g., **EGFR mutation status** for erlotinib). - **Pharmacodynamic (PD)** — confirms drug engages target. - **Surrogate** — substitutes for a clinical endpoint (e.g., **HbA1c** in diabetes). ### 2.3 Core Proteomic Technologies in Clinical & Drug-Discovery Workflows > [!NOTE] > **Activity-Based Protein Profiling (ABPP)** is a chemoproteomic technique that uses **covalent activity-based probes (ABPs)** carrying a reactive warhead, a linker, and a reporter tag. The probe covalently modifies only the **active form** of an enzyme, allowing direct read-out of enzyme activity (not just abundance) across the proteome. This is invaluable for **on-target engagement validation** in lead-optimization. > [!NOTE] > **Thermal Proteome Profiling (TPP / CETSA-MS)** measures the **thermal stability** shift of a target protein upon drug binding. A drug-bound target resists denaturation, so the protein's melting curve (Tm) shifts to a higher temperature. Coupled with quantitative MS, TPP provides a **label-free, proteome-wide target-engagement map**. ### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > The following table consolidates every equation, metric, and statistical bound a KTU student must memorize for this topic. All formulas are mathematically defensible and conform to standard proteomics & clinical-chemistry conventions. | Concept | Equation / Definition | Variables & Units | |---------|----------------------|-------------------| | Mass accuracy (ppm) | $\Delta m_{ppm} = \dfrac{\vert m_{measured} - m_{theoretical} \vert}{m_{theoretical}} \times 10^{6}$ | $m$ in Da; high-res MS expects $\leq$ **5 ppm** | | False Discovery Rate (FDR) — target-decoy approach | $FDR = \dfrac{FP}{FP + TP} \approx \dfrac{2 \cdot D_{pass}}{T_{pass}}$ | $D_{pass}$: decoy hits; $T_{pass}$: target hits | | Peptide-Spectrum Match (PSM) score — Mascot | $-10 \cdot \log_{10}(P)$ | $P$: probability of random match; score ≥ **20** is significant | | Receiver Operating Characteristic AUC | $AUC = \int_{0}^{1} TPR(FPR^{-1}(u)) \, du$ | $TPR$: sensitivity; $FPR$: 1 − specificity | | Sensitivity | $Se = \dfrac{TP}{TP + FN}$ | Dimensionless; report as % | | Specificity | $Sp = \dfrac{TN}{TN + FP}$ | Dimensionless; report as % | | Positive Predictive Value | $PPV = \dfrac{TP}{TP + FP}$ | Depends on disease prevalence | | Drug–target binding affinity (Kd) | $K_d = \dfrac{[D][T]}{[DT]}$ | Molar (M); lower Kd = stronger binding | | IC50 (half-maximal inhibitory concentration) | $\dfrac{v_i}{v_0} = 0.5$ at $[I] = IC_{50}$ | Molar; typically nM–µM range for drugs | | Hill equation for dose–response | $E = E_{max} \cdot \dfrac{[L]^n}{K_d + [L]^n}$ | $n$: Hill coefficient; $K_d$: dissociation constant | | Label-Free Quantification (LFQ) intensity ratio | $R_{i,j} = \dfrac{I_i}{I_j}$ | $I$: peptide ion intensity; used in MaxQuant | | Spectral counting (NSAF) | $NSAF_i = \dfrac{(SpC_i \big/ L_i)}{\sum_{k=1}^{N}(SpC_k \big/ L_k)}$ | $SpC$: spectral count; $L$: protein length | | Tandem Mass Tag (TMT) reporter ratio | $R_{127/126} = \dfrac{I_{127}}{I_{126}}$ | Used for multiplexed relative quantitation | | Companion Diagnostic Index (CDx) | $CDx_{score} = w_1 \cdot \Delta P + w_2 \cdot \Delta G$ | Weighted sum of protein & genomic scores | > [!NOTE] > **Critical Note on Table Syntax:** The KTU engine uses `\vert` or `\mid` instead of `|` for absolute-value and conditional-probability bars to prevent markdown-table rendering breakage. Always copy these equations exactly as shown. ### 2.5 Real-World Engineering & Pharmaceutical Utility - **Companion Diagnostics (CDx)**: HER2 testing (HercepTest) is the gold-standard CDx enabling **trastuzumab** therapy in breast cancer. Annual market for CDx is projected at **> US $8 billion** by 2027. - **Companion-Drug Co-Development**: The FDA now mandates parallel drug–diagnostic co-development (e.g., **vemurafenib + cobas® BRAF V600 test** for melanoma). - **Reducing Attrition**: ~**90%** of drug candidates fail in clinical trials; proteomic PD biomarkers catch mechanism failures earlier, saving **~ US $100–400 million** per failed program. - **Repurposing**: Comparative proteomics between drug-treated and disease-treated cells can identify new indications (e.g., **thalidomide** repurposed for multiple myeloma via cereblon E3 ligase identification). ## SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX ### 3.1 Derivation: Estimating the Minimum Number of Patients for a Validated Biomarker Study A classic ESE 14-mark sub-question. The relationship between sample size, effect size, significance level, and statistical power is derived as follows. **Given:** - Effect size (Cohen's d) = $\delta$ - Significance level $\alpha$ = 0.05 (two-tailed) - Desired power $1 - \beta$ = 0.80 - Standard normal quantiles: $z_{1-\alpha/2} = 1.96$ and $z_{1-\beta} = 0.84$ **Step 1 — Express the z-statistic for the difference of two independent means** $$ z = \dfrac{\bar{X}_1 - \bar{X}_2}{\sqrt{\dfrac{\sigma^2}{n_1} + \dfrac{\sigma^2}{n_2}}} $$ **Step 2 — Substitute equal variances and equal allocation ($n_1 = n_2 = n$)** $$ z = \dfrac{\bar{X}_1 - \bar{X}_2}{\sigma \sqrt{\dfrac{2}{n}}} = \dfrac{\Delta}{\sigma}\sqrt{\dfrac{n}{2}} $$ **Step 3 — Set $z = z_{1-\alpha/2} + z_{1-\beta}$ (the standard power-formula trick)** $$ z_{1-\alpha/2} + z_{1-\beta} = \dfrac{\Delta}{\sigma}\sqrt{\dfrac{n}{2}} $$ **Step 4 — Recognize Cohen's d = $\Delta / \sigma$ and solve for n** $$ n = 2\left(\dfrac{z_{1-\alpha/2} + z_{1-\beta}}{\delta}\right)^2 $$ $$ n = 2\left(\dfrac{1.96 + 0.84}{\delta}\right)^2 = \dfrac{15.68}{\delta^2} $$ **Step 5 — Numerical illustration** Suppose a candidate biomarker shows Cohen's d = 0.5 between disease and control groups: $$ n = \dfrac{15.68}{(0.5)^2} = \dfrac{15.68}{0.25} = 62.72 \approx 63 \text{ per group} $$ Total minimum cohort = **2 × 63 = 126 subjects**. This is why KTU examiner answers emphasize cohort-size justification. ### 3.2 Derivation: False Discovery Rate (FDR) — Target–Decoy Strategy The target-decoy approach (TDA) is the **gold standard** for MS-based proteomics FDR estimation. **Step 1 — Define the hit counts** - $T$ = target database hits above score threshold - $D$ = decoy database hits above the same threshold - $TP$ = true positives, $FP$ = false positives **Step 2 — Assume the decoy hits approximate the false positives** The decoy database is constructed by reversing or randomizing real protein sequences. Any match to a decoy is, by definition, a false positive because decoys do not exist in nature. $$ FP \approx D $$ **Step 3 — Estimate FDR** $$ FDR = \dfrac{FP}{FP + TP} \approx \dfrac{D}{D + T} $$ **Step 4 — Optional refinement with PSM aggregation** At the peptide level (common in MaxQuant/Andromeda), we typically use: $$ FDR_{peptide} = \dfrac{2 \cdot D_{peptide}}{T_{peptide} + D_{peptide}} $$ The factor of 2 arises because each real peptide has a decoy counterpart of equal detectability, and the search space is doubled. **Step 5 — Practical example** If a search returns T = 9,500 target PSMs and D = 250 decoy PSMs at threshold: $$ FDR \approx \dfrac{2 \times 250}{9500 + 250} = \dfrac{500}{9750} = 0.0513 \approx 5.13\% $$ This exceeds the 1% threshold, so the score cutoff must be **raised** until FDR ≤ 0.01. KTU examiners reward students who *show the calculation* rather than quote the threshold. ### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Comparative Performance of Clinical Proteomics Platforms | Platform | Sensitivity (fmol) | Throughput | Quantitation | Cost | KTU-Relevant Use | |----------|--------------------|------------|--------------|------|------------------| | 2D-PAGE + MS | 1–10 | Low | Semi-quantitative | Low | Historical biomarker screening | | LC-MS/MS (DDA) | 0.1–1 | Medium | LFQ / TMT | Medium | Plasma proteomics | | LC-MS/MS (DIA / SWATH-MS) | 0.05–0.5 | High | Robust label-free | Medium | Clinical cohort screening | | MALDI-TOF MS | 1–10 | Very high | Spectral patterns | Low | Microbial ID, clinical MS | | SELDI-TOF MS | 5–50 | High | Peak profiling | Medium | Early cancer biomarker discovery | | Protein microarray | 0.01–1 | Very high | Multiplex | High | Autoantibody / cytokine profiling | | Immuno-MS (SISCAPA) | attomole | Medium | Absolute (with SIAM) | High | Low-abundance plasma proteins (e.g., CRP, troponin) | | Olink / SomaScan | sub-femtomole | Very high | Relative NPX / RFU | Very high | Large-scale clinical biomarker panels | > [!NOTE] > **SISCAPA (Stable Isotope Standards and Capture by Anti-Peptide Antibodies)** is an immunoaffinity-MS technique that uses anti-peptide antibodies coupled to magnetic beads and isotope-labeled internal standards. It achieves **attomolar-level** sensitivity, enabling precise quantitation of low-abundance plasma proteins that elude conventional shotgun proteomics. ### 3.4 Decision-Flow Algorithm: Choosing a Proteomics Strategy for a Clinical Problem 1. **Is the question exploratory or hypothesis-driven?** - Exploratory → SWATH-MS / DIA or 2D-DIGE. - Hypothesis-driven → targeted SRM (MRM) or SISCAPA. 2. **Is the sample blood plasma (high dynamic range > 10 orders of magnitude)?** - Yes → Deplete top-14 proteins OR use SomaScan/Olink (aptamer-based) OR SISCAPA. - No (tissue/cell) → Standard shotgun LC-MS/MS. 3. **Are you measuring activity or abundance?** - Activity → ABPP. - Abundance → Standard MS quantitation. 4. **Do you need target engagement validation?** - Yes → TPP / CETSA-MS. 5. **Is the cohort > 500 patients?** - Yes → Olink PEA or SomaScan for throughput. ## SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS ### 4.1 End-to-End Clinical Proteomics → Drug Discovery Workflow ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Patient Cohort Recruitment] --> B[Biofluid / Tissue Sampling] B --> C[Sample Prep: Lysis, Digestion, Depletion] C --> D[MS-Based Profiling: LC-MS or 2D-DIGE] D --> E[Bioinformatics: ID, Quant, Statistics] E --> F{Is candidate biomarker validated?} F -- No --> G[Re-screen Larger Cohort] G --> D F -- Yes --> H[ELISA / SRM / SISCAPA Verification] H --> I[Clinical Assay Development] I --> J[Companion Diagnostic Co-Development] J --> K[Drug Target Validation: ABPP, TPP] K --> L[High-Throughput Screening] L --> M[Lead Optimization: Chemoproteomics] M --> N[Preclinical: Toxicoproteomics] N --> O[Clinical Trials: Pharmacoproteomics] O --> P[FDA Approval and Post-Market Surveillance] ``` ### 4.2 Biomarker Discovery Funnel — Discovery to Clinical Assay ```mermaid flowchart LR S1[Discovery Phase
1000s of proteins] --> S2[Verification Phase
100s of candidates] S2 --> S3[Validation Phase
10s of candidates] S3 --> S4[Clinical Assay
1 to 5 biomarkers] S4 --> S5[FDA Cleared Test
e.g. OVA1, VeriStrat] S1 -.2D-DIGE, LC-MS/MS.-> S2 S2 -.SRM/MRM, ELISA.-> S3 S3 -.Multi-site clinical trial.-> S4 S4 -.CLIA Lab Validation.-> S5 ``` ### 4.3 Activity-Based Protein Profiling (ABPP) Mechanism ```mermaid flowchart TD P1[Activity-Based Probe ABP] --> P2[Reactive Warhead
covalent binding to active enzyme] P2 --> P3[Tag Linker
PEG or alkyl chain] P3 --> P4[Reporter Group
biotin, fluorophore, or alkyne] P4 --> P5[Proteome Incubated with ABP] P5 --> P6[Active Enzymes Covalently Labeled] P6 --> P7[Pull-down or In-gel Fluorescence] P7 --> P8[LC-MS or Gel Imaging] P8 --> P9[Active Enzyme Inventory] ``` ### 4.4 Pharmacoproteomics Stratification Loop ```mermaid flowchart TD X1[Patient Enters Trial] --> X2[Baseline Plasma Proteome Profiled] X2 --> X3{Cluster Into Responder or Non Responder?} X3 --> X4[Responder Subproteome] X3 --> X5[Non Responder Subproteome] X4 --> X6[Drug Continues to Be Effective] X5 --> X7[Dose Escalation or Therapy Switch] X6 --> X8[Outcome Improved by Stratification] X7 --> X8 ``` ### 4.5 Thermal Proteome Profiling — Target Engagement Readout ```mermaid flowchart LR Y1[Cell Lysate or Live Cells] --> Y2[Drug Added at Range of Temperatures] Y2 --> Y3[Heat Shock Across 37 to 67 Degrees Celsius] Y3 --> Y4[Soluble Fraction Isolated by Centrifugation] Y4 --> Y5[LC-MS or TMT Quantitation] Y5 --> Y6[Melting Curve Tm Fitted for Each Protein] Y6 --> Y7{Drug Shifts Tm?} Y7 -- Yes --> Y8[Confirmed On Target Hit] Y7 -- No --> Y9[Off Target or No Engagement] ``` ## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP ### PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) > **[KTU University Exam — Model Question, Module 4]** #### Question 1 (3 Marks) **Define a biomarker. List the FDA-NIH BEST categories of biomarkers with one example each.** **Model Answer:** > A biomarker is an objectively measurable biological indicator of a normal or pathological process, or a pharmacological response to therapy. **BEST categories with examples:** | Category | Function | Example | |----------|----------|---------| | Diagnostic | Confirms disease | PSA for prostate cancer | | Prognostic | Predicts disease course | HER2/neu in breast cancer | | Predictive | Predicts therapy response | EGFR mutation for erlotinib | | Pharmacodynamic | Confirms target engagement | Phospho-ERK reduction | | Surrogate | Replaces clinical endpoint | HbA1c for diabetes | | Monitoring | Tracks disease progression | CD4+ T-cell count in HIV | (Any **four** categories with examples earn full 3 marks.) --- #### Question 2 (3 Marks) **What is pharmacoproteomics? How does it improve clinical trial outcomes?** **Model Answer:** Pharmacoproteomics is the large-scale study of a patient's proteome to predict or monitor response to a drug. It improves clinical trial outcomes by: - **Stratifying patients** into responders vs. non-responders based on baseline proteomic signatures, reducing trial size and cost. - **Confirming pharmacodynamic engagement** of the intended protein target in early-phase trials. - **Identifying off-target effects** before they manifest as adverse events. - **Enabling personalized dosing** based on individual proteomic profiles, increasing therapeutic index. --- ### PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice Provided) > **[KTU University Exam — ESE, Module 4, 14-Mark Pattern]** --- #### **Question A (14 Marks)** **(a) [7 Marks]** Describe the **stages of the drug discovery pipeline** and explain in detail how proteomics contributes to **target identification, target validation, and lead optimization**. Use specific technique names. **(b) [7 Marks]** With a neat diagram, explain **Activity-Based Protein Profiling (ABPP)** and its role in target validation. List two FDA-approved drugs discovered using proteomics-guided approaches. --- #### **Model Answer to Question A** **(a) Drug Discovery Pipeline & Proteomics Contribution (7 marks)** The drug discovery pipeline is a sequential funnel: 1. **Target Identification** — Proteomics tools such as **2D-DIGE** and **shotgun LC-MS/MS** compare the proteome of disease vs. healthy tissue to find differentially expressed proteins. Bioinformatics databases (**UniProt, HPRD, STRING**) are mined for tissue-specific and druggable candidates. *(1.5 marks)* 2. **Target Validation** — Once a candidate protein is identified, its causal role is confirmed using: - **RNAi knockdown** combined with **LFQ proteomics** to verify downstream pathways. - **CRISPR knockout** followed by **TMT-labeled LC-MS/MS** to measure compensatory proteome changes. - **Activity-Based Protein Profiling (ABPP)** to confirm enzyme activity in disease samples. *(1.5 marks)* 3. **Lead Identification (Screening)** — Chemical proteomics uses **drug-affinity matrices** (drug immobilized on beads) to pull down binding proteins from cell lysates. Hits are identified by MS. Protein microarrays enable parallel screening of thousands of proteins against a single compound. *(2 marks)* 4. **Lead Optimization** — **Chemoproteomics** techniques such as **Thermal Proteome Profiling (TPP / CETSA-MS)** measure drug-induced thermal stability shifts of cellular proteins to confirm on-target engagement and identify off-target liabilities. Affinity-based MS methods such as **Kinobeads** profile inhibitor selectivity across the kinome. *(2 marks)* **(b) Activity-Based Protein Profiling (ABPP) Diagram & Role (7 marks)** **Diagram (3 marks):** A clear, labeled schematic of an ABP — **Reactive Warhead → Linker → Reporter Tag** — with the workflow: ``` ABP + Cell Proteome → Covalent Labeling of Active Enzymes → Streptavidin Pull-down (if biotin) or CuAAC Click Chemistry (if alkyne) → LC-MS/MS Identification ``` **Role in Target Validation (2.5 marks):** - ABPP directly measures **functional enzyme activity** in native proteomes, distinguishing active from inactive or inhibitor-bound enzyme pools. - Enables **in situ target engagement** of covalent and non-covalent drugs. - Used to **profile selectivity** across an entire enzyme family (e.g., serine hydrolases, cysteine proteases, kinases). **Two FDA-approved drugs discovered using proteomics-guided approaches (1.5 marks):** - **Bortezomib** (Velcade®) — proteasome inhibitor for multiple myeloma; target identified via proteasome profiling. - **Vemurafenib** (Zelboraf®) — BRAF V600E kinase inhibitor; kinome profiling (KiNativ) guided selectivity optimization. --- #### **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)** **(a) [7 Marks]** What is **toxicoproteomics**? Explain its role in preclinical drug safety assessment with two examples of organ-specific toxicity biomarkers. **(b) [7 Marks]** Derive the **sample-size formula** for a two-group biomarker validation study and apply it to compute the minimum cohort size when Cohen's d = 0.4 and power = 0.90. --- #### **Model Answer to Question B** **(a) Toxicoproteomics (7 marks)** Toxicoproteomics is the sub-discipline of proteomics that uses global protein-profiling technologies to **identify, quantify, and interpret protein alterations caused by toxicants or drug candidates**. It complements traditional histopathology and clinical chemistry by providing **molecular-level, mechanism-based readouts** of toxicity. *(1.5 marks for definition)* **Role in Preclinical Drug Safety (3.5 marks):** - **Mechanistic Elucidation**: Identifies the molecular pathways of drug-induced organ damage before histological lesions appear. - **Biomarker Discovery**: Discovers early-warning biomarkers in plasma/urine that predict organ-specific toxicity. - **Cross-Species Translation**: Validates that toxicity pathways observed in rat/dog models translate to humans. - **Reducing Late-Stage Attrition**: ~30% of drug failures are due to clinical hepatotoxicity; proteomic biomarkers (e.g., **keratin-18, GSTA1, ARG1**) predict DILI earlier than ALT elevation. **Two Organ-Specific Toxicity Biomarker Examples (2 marks):** | Organ | Proteomic Biomarker | Disease Context | |-------|--------------------|-----------------| | Liver (Hepatotoxicity) | Keratin-18 (KRT18), miR-122 | Drug-induced liver injury (DILI) | | Kidney (Nephrotoxicity) | Clusterin, Kim-1 (HAVCR1), Osteopontin | Aminoglycoside nephrotoxicity | | Heart (Cardiotoxicity) | Cardiac troponin I (cTnI), FABP3 | Anthracycline cardiotoxicity | --- **(b) Sample-Size Derivation (7 marks)** **Step 1 — Two-sample z-test statistic** (1 mark) $$ z = \dfrac{\bar{X}_1 - \bar{X}_2}{\sigma \sqrt{\dfrac{2}{n}}} $$ **Step 2 — Set z equal to the sum of critical values** (2 marks) $$ z_{1-\alpha/2} + z_{1-\beta} = \dfrac{\Delta}{\sigma}\sqrt{\dfrac{n}{2}} $$ **Step 3 — Substitute Cohen's d = $\Delta/\sigma$ and solve for n** (2 marks) $$ n = 2\left(\dfrac{z_{1-\alpha/2} + z_{1-\beta}}{\delta}\right)^2 $$ **Step 4 — Numerical application** (2 marks) For $\alpha$ = 0.05 (two-tailed), $z_{1-\alpha/2} = 1.96$; for power = 0.90, $z_{1-\beta} = 1.28$; $\delta = 0.4$. $$ n = 2\left(\dfrac{1.96 + 1.28}{0.4}\right)^2 = 2\left(\dfrac{3.24}{0.4}\right)^2 = 2 \times (8.1)^2 = 2 \times 65.61 = 131.22 $$ $$ n \approx 132 \text{ per group} $$ Total minimum cohort = **2 × 132 = 264 subjects**. *(1 mark)* --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks** > > 1. **Do not confuse Sensitivity with PPV.** Sensitivity depends only on the test, whereas PPV depends on disease prevalence. KTU examiners specifically deduct 1–2 marks if these are interchanged. > 2. **Always state the assumption of decoy symmetry** when describing the FDR target-decoy approach — otherwise the formula is unjustified. > 3. **In drug-discovery diagrams**, you MUST show all three components of an ABP (warhead, linker, tag). A probe drawn as just a labeled rectangle loses 2 marks. > 4. **Sample-size derivations must include the algebraic substitution of $z_{1-\alpha/2} + z_{1-\beta}$** explicitly. Skipping this step loses 2 marks. > 5. **Avoid claiming "proteomics replaces genomics"** — it complements it. KTU examiners penalize overstatements. --- ### TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER (Rapid-Revision Checklist) > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Density Summary — Re-read 30 Minutes Before the Exam** - **Clinical Proteomics** = proteome-wide analysis for biomarker & target discovery in human disease. - **Drug Discovery Proteomics** = application of proteomics to every pipeline stage (target ID → post-market). - **~96% of FDA-approved drugs target proteins** (enzymes, receptors, channels, transporters). - **FDA-NIH BEST biomarker categories** = Diagnostic, Prognostic, Predictive, PD, Surrogate, Monitoring — memorize one example each. - **ABPP (Activity-Based Protein Profiling)** uses a **warhead–linker–tag probe** to label only **active enzymes**. - **TPP / CETSA-MS** measures **drug-induced thermal stability shifts** to confirm target engagement. - **2D-DIGE** = classical differential proteomics; **SWATH-MS / DIA** = modern high-throughput. - **SISCAPA, SomaScan, Olink** are the three principal clinical-platform technologies for low-abundance plasma proteomics. - **Toxicoproteomics** biomarkers include **KRT18 (liver), Kim-1/clusterin (kidney), cTnI (heart)**. - **Companion diagnostics** are co-developed with drugs (e.g., **HER2 testing + trastuzumab**, **BRAF V600 + vemurafenib**). - **Sample-size formula**: $n = 2\left(\dfrac{z_{1-\alpha/2} + z_{1-\beta}}{\delta}\right)^2$ — memorize the four key z-values (1.96, 1.645, 0.84, 1.28). - **FDR target-decoy**: $FDR = \dfrac{2 D_{pass}}{T_{pass} + D_{pass}}$; clinical threshold = **1%**. - **NSAF** normalization corrects raw spectral counts for protein length. - **Mascot score** = $-10 \log_{10}(P)$; threshold ≥ 20 is conventional. - **Mass accuracy** in high-res MS = **≤ 5 ppm**; report via the ppm formula (not %). - **Hill equation** $E = E_{max} \cdot \dfrac{[L]^n}{K_d + [L]^n}$ governs dose–response. - **Pharmacoproteomics** stratifies trial patients into responders vs. non-responders, reducing late-stage attrition and saving **~$100–400 M per failed program**. - **Drug repurposing** via comparative proteomics (e.g., thalidomide → multiple myeloma via cereblon). - Always state the **cohort-size assumption** (equal allocation, equal variance) before deriving n.

topics.title: Proteomics in Study of Diseases
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEBBT756 - PROTEOMICS & PROTEIN ENGINEERING > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Proteomics in Study of Diseases # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition **Disease Proteomics** (also termed Clinical or Biomedical Proteomics) is the systematic, large-scale study of the entire protein complement — the **proteome** — expressed by a cell, tissue, organ, or biofluid under pathological conditions, with the explicit aim of identifying, quantifying, and functionally characterizing proteins and their post-translational modifications (PTMs) that drive, mark, or modulate human disease. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Verbatim Paraphrase):** > Disease proteomics is the application of high-throughput proteomic technologies (2D-PAGE, mass spectrometry, protein microarrays, MS imaging) to discover disease-associated protein biomarkers, elucidate pathogenic mechanisms, identify therapeutic targets, and stratify patients for personalized medicine. The field is built upon three foundational pillars: | Pillar | Formal Meaning | |---|---| | **Discovery Proteomics** | Hypothesis-free, global profiling to find novel proteins altered in disease | | **Targeted Proteomics** | Hypothesis-driven, precise quantification (e.g., SRM/MRM, PRM) of predefined candidates | | **Functional Proteomics** | Mapping of protein–protein interactions (interactomics), PTMs, and activity states in disease | ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition Imagine a city during normal days vs. during a major blackout (the disease state). The infrastructure is the same, but some streetlights fail, emergency vehicles reroute, certain shops close, and traffic patterns shift. If you **photograph every building, every worker, every vehicle, and every signal light** during both states, you can pinpoint exactly which components malfunctioned. > [!NOTE] > **Analogy Map:** > - City = Cell / Tissue / Organism > - Buildings = Proteins (the functional units) > - Workers = Protein complexes & PTMs > - Blackout = Disease state (cancer, neurodegeneration, infection) > - Photographs = Mass spectrometry spectra & 2D gels > - Comparative Report = **Differentially expressed proteins = Biomarkers** The key insight: **mRNA levels do NOT always correlate with protein abundance** (correlation often only 0.4–0.6), and proteins are the actual drug targets (>95% of drugs act on proteins, not nucleic acids). Therefore, studying proteins directly is indispensable. ## 1.3 Standard Metrics in Disease Proteomics - **Limit of Detection (LOD):** ~**amol to fmol** range (10⁻¹⁸ to 10⁻¹⁵ mol) for modern MS - **Dynamic Range:** **>10⁶** for serum proteome (albumin ~10⁹× more abundant than IL-6) - **Coefficient of Variation (CV):** Targeted assays target **<15%** - **Number of proteins identified per study:** 500–10,000+ (depending on platform) - **Biomarker FDA criteria:** **AUROC ≥ 0.80** for clinical adoption ## 1.4 Why Disease Proteomics Matters — The Clinical Imperative > [!IMPORTANT] > **The Central Dogma Gap:** Genome → Transcriptome → **PROTEOME** → Phenotype > Proteins undergo ~**>200 known PTMs** (phosphorylation, glycosylation, ubiquitination, acetylation, methylation, SUMOylation, etc.) that NO transcriptomics study can capture. Disease often manifests at the **protein activity** level, not the gene level. Diseases studied by proteomics include (mapped to KTU syllabus priorities): 1. **Cancer** (breast, lung, ovarian, colorectal, pancreatic, prostate) 2. **Neurodegenerative disorders** (Alzheimer's, Parkinson's, ALS, Huntington's) 3. **Cardiovascular diseases** (myocardial infarction, atherosclerosis, heart failure) 4. **Infectious diseases** (bacterial, viral — including SARS-CoV-2, tuberculosis, malaria) 5. **Autoimmune diseases** (RA, SLE, multiple sclerosis, type 1 diabetes) 6. **Metabolic disorders** (type 2 diabetes, obesity, NAFLD) 7. **Inherited disorders** (cystic fibrosis, sickle cell, lysosomal storage diseases) # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Disease Proteomics Workflow — Structured Logic Steps The complete pipeline from clinical sample to validated biomarker follows **seven canonical stages**: ### Stage 1: Clinical Cohort Design - Define **cases** (diseased) vs. **controls** (healthy) - Match for age, sex, BMI, ethnicity, comorbidities - Minimum sample size for 80% power: typically **n ≥ 30 per group** for discovery, **n ≥ 100** for validation ### Stage 2: Sample Collection & Pre-analytics - Biofluids: **serum, plasma, urine, CSF, saliva, tears, bronchoalveolar lavage** - Tissues: biopsies, FFPE (formalin-fixed paraffin-embedded), fresh-frozen - Critical: standardized SOPs to avoid pre-analytical variation (proteins degrade within **30 min at RT**) ### Stage 3: Protein Extraction & Depletion - Lysis buffers (urea, thiourea, CHAPS, DTT) - **High-abundance protein depletion** (removes albumin, IgG — top 22 proteins = **~99%** of plasma mass) - Subcellular fractionation (nuclear, mitochondrial, membrane, cytosolic) ### Stage 4: Separation & MS Analysis - 2D-PAGE, 2D-DIGE, or LC (1D/2D nanoLC) - Tandem MS: **MS1 (survey scan)** → fragment → **MS2 (tandem scan)** - Ionization: ESI or MALDI ### Stage 5: Bioinformatics & Statistical Analysis - Database searching: **MASCOT, Sequest HT, MS-GF+, Andromeda (MaxQuant)** - FDR control: **<1%** at peptide level - Quantification: label-free (LFQ intensity, spectral counting) or labeled (SILAC, iTRAQ, TMT) ### Stage 6: Biomarker Validation - **Orthogonal validation:** ELISA, Western blot, IHC, MRM/SRM - **Independent cohort confirmation** ### Stage 7: Clinical Translation - **AUROC ≥ 0.80**, sensitivity ≥ 75%, specificity ≥ 75% - Regulatory pathway: **FDA / EMA Biomarker Qualification** ## 2.2 Why Each Step Matters — The Engineering Logic > [!NOTE] > **Engineering Perspective:** Disease proteomics is essentially a **signal-processing problem** buried in a **biological matrix**. The "signal" is the differentially expressed protein; the "noise" includes biological variability (inter-individual), pre-analytical degradation, and technical variance (instrument drift). Each step is engineered to maximize **Signal-to-Noise Ratio (SNR)**. $$ \text{SNR}_{\text{proteomics}} = \frac{\mu_{\text{disease}} - \mu_{\text{control}}}{\sqrt{\sigma_{\text{bio}}^2 + \sigma_{\text{tech}}^2}} $$ Where: - $\mu_{\text{disease}}$ = mean protein abundance in cases - $\mu_{\text{control}}$ = mean protein abundance in controls - $\sigma_{\text{bio}}$ = biological standard deviation - $\sigma_{\text{tech}}$ = technical standard deviation ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet & Cheat Sheet ### Table 2.1 — Key Equations in Disease Proteomics | Equation / Metric | Mathematical Form | Application | |---|---|---| | **Fold Change (FC)** | $\text{FC} = \dfrac{Ab_{\text{disease}}}{Ab_{\text{control}}}$ | Differential expression threshold (typically FC $\geq 1.5$ or $\leq 0.67$) | | **Log₂ Fold Change** | $\log_2 \text{FC} = \log_2 \left( \dfrac{Ab_{\text{disease}}}{Ab_{\text{control}}} \right)$ | Symmetric scale; $\vert\log_2 \text{FC}\vert \geq 1$ commonly used | | **Student's t-statistic** | $t = \dfrac{\bar{x}_1 - \bar{x}_2}{s_p \sqrt{\dfrac{1}{n_1} + \dfrac{1}{n_2}}}$ | Compare two-group means | | **Volcano Plot Coordinates** | $x = \log_2 \text{FC}, \quad y = -\log_{10}(p\text{-value})$ | Visualizes differential expression | | **AUROC (ROC curve)** | $\text{AUROC} = \int_0^1 \text{TPR}(\text{FPR}^{-1}(u))\, du$ | Diagnostic accuracy | | **Sensitivity** | $\text{Se} = \dfrac{\text{TP}}{\text{TP} + \text{FN}}$ | True positive rate | | **Specificity** | $\text{Sp} = \dfrac{\text{TN}}{\text{TN} + \text{FP}}$ | True negative rate | | **Positive Predictive Value** | $\text{PPV} = \dfrac{\text{Se} \cdot P}{\text{Se} \cdot P + (1-\text{Sp})(1-P)}$ | Depends on disease prevalence $P$ | | **Bonferroni Correction** | $\alpha_{\text{adj}} = \dfrac{\alpha}{m}$ | For $m$ hypotheses (controls family-wise error) | | **FDR (Benjamini-Hochberg)** | $p_{(i)} \leq \dfrac{i}{m} \cdot q$ | $q$ = desired FDR (typically 0.05) | | **SILAC Ratio** | $R = \dfrac{\text{Heavy}}{\text{Light}} = \dfrac{\text{Heavy spike}}{\text{Endogenous}}$ | Quantitative comparison | | **TMT Reporter Ion Intensity** | $I_k \propto [\text{Protein}_k]$ | 10-plex or 16-plex multiplexing | | **Dynamic Range** | $\text{DR} = \log_{10}\left(\dfrac{C_{\max}}{C_{\min}}\right)$ | Serum: ~10⁶ orders of magnitude | | **Peptide-Spectrum Match (PSM)** | $\text{FDR} = \dfrac{\text{Decoy PSMs}}{\text{Total PSMs}}$ | Quality control in MS identification | ### Table 2.2 — Major Disease Proteomic Biomarkers (FDA-Approved or Clinical Use) | Disease | Biomarker | Sample | Discovery Method | |---|---|---|---| | Ovarian cancer | **CA-125 (MUC16)** | Serum | Immunoassay (historical) | | Prostate cancer | **PSA (KLK3)** | Serum | 2D-PAGE / MS | | Heart failure | **NT-proBNP, BNP** | Plasma | MS + immunoassay | | Breast cancer | **HER2/neu** | Tissue | IHC + proteomics validation | | Liver fibrosis | **FibroMeter, ELF score** | Serum | MS-based panel | | Alzheimer's | **Aβ42, total tau, p-tau181** | CSF / plasma | IP-MS, Simoa | | COVID-19 severity | **IL-6, CRP, D-dimer** | Plasma | Targeted MS panels | | Pancreatic cancer | **CA 19-9** | Serum | Glycoproteomics | ## 2.4 Real-World Utility of Disease Proteomics in Engineering & Medicine - **Companion diagnostics:** Her2 testing for trastuzumab eligibility - **Drug target identification:** BCR-ABL kinase → imatinib (Gleevec) - **Resistance mechanisms:** Proteomic profiling of drug-resistant tumors - **Liquid biopsies:** Cell-free tumor DNA + **tumor-derived proteins** (e.g., circulating tumor cells, exosomes) - **Multi-omics integration:** Proteo-genomics (e.g., CPTAC — Clinical Proteomic Tumor Analysis Consortium) - **Pharmacoproteomics:** Predicting individual drug response from baseline proteome # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1 — From MS Intensity to Fold Change This is the **core quantitative workflow** used in every differential proteomics study (label-free or labeled). ### Step 1: Obtain Peptide Intensity For peptide $p$ from protein $P$, the mass spectrometer records a peak intensity $I_{p}^{(s)}$ in sample $s$. The protein abundance $Ab_P^{(s)}$ is estimated as the sum (or median) of its peptide intensities: $$ Ab_P^{(s)} = \sum_{p \in P} I_p^{(s)} $$ **Explanation:** A protein is digested into multiple peptides; summing their MS intensities approximates the protein's molar abundance. Median is more robust to outliers than sum. ### Step 2: Normalize Across Samples To remove technical variation (loading differences, ionization efficiency), apply **median normalization**: $$ \tilde{I}_p^{(s)} = \dfrac{I_p^{(s)}}{f^{(s)}}, \quad \text{where} \quad f^{(s)} = \dfrac{\text{Median}(I_p^{(s)})}{\text{Median}_{s'}(I_p^{(s')})} $$ **Explanation:** Each sample's intensity is divided by a scaling factor $f^{(s)}$ that equalizes the global median, preserving biological ratios while correcting for technical bias. ### Step 3: Compute Average per Group For a study with $n_D$ disease samples and $n_C$ control samples: $$ \bar{Ab}_P^{(D)} = \dfrac{1}{n_D} \sum_{s=1}^{n_D} \tilde{Ab}_P^{(s)}, \quad \bar{Ab}_P^{(C)} = \dfrac{1}{n_C} \sum_{s=1}^{n_C} \tilde{Ab}_P^{(s)} $$ ### Step 4: Fold Change Calculation $$ \text{FC}_P = \dfrac{\bar{Ab}_P^{(D)}}{\bar{Ab}_P^{(C)}} $$ ### Step 5: Log Transformation (Symmetry) $$ \Delta_P = \log_2 \text{FC}_P = \log_2 \bar{Ab}_P^{(D)} - \log_2 \bar{Ab}_P^{(C)} $$ **Explanation:** A 2-fold upregulation ($\text{FC} = 2$) and a 2-fold downregulation ($\text{FC} = 0.5$) are equally distant from no change on a $\log_2$ scale ($\Delta = +1$ vs. $\Delta = -1$), which is statistically and visually more appropriate. ### Step 6: Welch's t-test (Unequal Variance) $$ t_P = \dfrac{\bar{Ab}_P^{(D)} - \bar{Ab}_P^{(C)}}{\sqrt{\dfrac{s_D^2}{n_D} + \dfrac{s_C^2}{n_C}}} $$ Where $s_D^2$ and $s_C^2$ are sample variances. ### Step 7: Multiple Testing Correction (Benjamini-Hochberg) Rank $p$-values: $p_{(1)} \leq p_{(2)} \leq \dots \leq p_{(m)}$. Find the largest $i$ such that: $$ p_{(i)} \leq \dfrac{i}{m} \cdot q $$ All $p_{(j)}$ for $j \leq i$ are declared significant at FDR level $q$ (typically $q = 0.05$). ## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Diagnostic Test Performance Metrics Given a candidate biomarker with measured value $x$ in patient $i$: ### Step 1: Choose Threshold $c$ Receiver Operating Characteristic (ROC) analysis sweeps across all candidate thresholds $c$. ### Step 2: Build Confusion Matrix at Each $c$ $$ \text{TP}(c) = \vert\{i : x_i > c \text{ and disease}_i = \text{yes}\}\vert $$ $$ \text{FN}(c) = \vert\{i : x_i \leq c \text{ and disease}_i = \text{yes}\}\vert $$ $$ \text{FP}(c) = \vert\{i : x_i > c \text{ and disease}_i = \text{no}\}\vert $$ $$ \text{TN}(c) = \vert\{i : x_i \leq c \text{ and disease}_i = \text{no}\}\vert $$ ### Step 3: Compute TPR and FPR $$ \text{TPR}(c) = \dfrac{\text{TP}(c)}{\text{TP}(c) + \text{FN}(c)} = \dfrac{\text{TP}(c)}{P} $$ $$ \text{FPR}(c) = \dfrac{\text{FP}(c)}{\text{FP}(c) + \text{TN}(c)} = \dfrac{\text{FP}(c)}{N} $$ ### Step 4: AUROC via Trapezoidal Rule $$ \text{AUROC} = \sum_{k} \dfrac{\text{FPR}_{k+1} - \text{FPR}_k}{2} \cdot (\text{TPR}_{k+1} + \text{TPR}_k) $$ **Interpretation:** AUROC = 1.0 = perfect; AUROC = 0.5 = random. ## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Label-Free Quantification Variance Decomposition The observed intensity $I_p^{(s)}$ decomposes as: $$ I_p^{(s)} = \mu_P + \beta_s + \gamma_p + \epsilon_{p,s} $$ Where: - $\mu_P$ = true mean abundance of protein $P$ - $\beta_s \sim \mathcal{N}(0, \sigma_{\text{bio}}^2)$ = biological sample effect - $\gamma_p \sim \mathcal{N}(0, \sigma_{\text{pep}}^2)$ = peptide-specific ionization - $\epsilon_{p,s} \sim \mathcal{N}(0, \sigma_{\text{tech}}^2)$ = technical noise **Total variance:** $$ \sigma_{\text{total}}^2 = \sigma_{\text{bio}}^2 + \sigma_{\text{pep}}^2 + \sigma_{\text{tech}}^2 $$ **Required sample size for detecting fold change $\Delta$ at power $1-\beta$ and significance $\alpha$:** $$ n_{\text{per group}} = \left( \dfrac{(z_{1-\alpha/2} + z_{1-\beta}) \cdot \sigma_{\text{total}}}{\Delta} \right)^2 $$ For $\alpha = 0.05$, $z_{1-\alpha/2} = 1.96$; for $1-\beta = 0.80$, $z_{1-\beta} = 0.84$: $$ n_{\text{per group}} = \left( \dfrac{2.80 \cdot \sigma_{\text{total}}}{\Delta} \right)^2 $$ **Example:** To detect $\Delta = 1.5$ (in $\log_2$ units) with $\sigma = 0.4$: $$ n = \left( \dfrac{2.80 \times 0.4}{0.585} \right)^2 = (1.916)^2 \approx 3.67 \Rightarrow n \geq 4 $$ But considering biological variability realistically ($\sigma \approx 0.8$), $n \geq 16$ per group — explaining why **proteomics studies need n ≥ 30** for confidence. # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Disease Proteomics Workflow — Block-Level Architecture ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Patient Cohort
Cases and Controls] --> B[Sample Collection
Serum Plasma Tissue CSF] B --> C[Protein Extraction
Lysis Depletion Fractionation] C --> D[Separation
2D-PAGE or nanoLC] D --> E[Mass Spectrometry
ESI or MALDI Tandem MS] E --> F[Bioinformatics
Database Search Quantification] F --> G[Statistical Analysis
FDR Control Volcano Plot] G --> H[Biomarker Candidates
Differentially Expressed Proteins] H --> I[Orthogonal Validation
ELISA WB IHC MRM] I --> J[Independent Cohort
Confirmation] J --> K[Clinical Translation
FDA Approval AUROC] style A fill:#e1f5ff,stroke:#01579b style E fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#f57f17 style K fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20 style H fill:#ffccbc,stroke:#bf360c ``` ## 4.2 Multi-Disease Proteomics Mapping — Functional Architecture ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph Oncology OC1[Breast Cancer
HER2 EGFR] OC2[Lung Cancer
EGFR ALK PDL1] OC3[Ovarian Cancer
CA125 HE4] end subgraph Neurodegeneration ND1[Alzheimers
Amyloid Tau] ND2[Parkinsons
Alpha Synuclein] ND3[ALS
SOD1 TDP43] end subgraph CardioMetabolic CM1[Heart Failure
BNP Troponin] CM2[Type 2 Diabetes
Insulin Adiponectin] end subgraph Infectious ID1[Tuberculosis
Antigen85 ESAT6] ID2[COVID19
IL6 CRP] ID3[Malaria
HRP2 LDH] end TECH[Proteomics Technology
MS LC 2D Arrays] --> Oncology TECH --> Neurodegeneration TECH --> CardioMetabolic TECH --> Infectious style TECH fill:#e8eaf6,stroke:#1a237e style Oncology fill:#fce4ec,stroke:#880e4f style Neurodegeneration fill:#e1bee7,stroke:#4a148c style CardioMetabolic fill:#fff3e0,stroke:#e65100 style Infectious fill:#ffebee,stroke:#b71c1c ``` ## 4.3 Biomarker Discovery Decision Tree ```mermaid flowchart TD S[Start
Disease Question] --> Q1{Sample Type?} Q1 -->|Biofluid| P1[Serum Plasma Urine CSF] Q1 -->|Tissue| P2[Fresh Frozen FFPE Biopsy] P1 --> M1[Deplete High Abundance
Top 22 Proteins] P2 --> M2[Subcellular Fractionation] M1 --> TECH{Quantitation?} M2 --> TECH TECH -->|Discovery| D1[Label Free LFQ
or TMT 16 plex] TECH -->|Targeted| D2[SRM MRM PRM
Simoa] D1 --> BIO[Biological Replicates
n greater than 30] D2 --> BIO BIO --> STAT[Statistical Filtering
FDR less than 0.05
FC greater than 1.5] STAT --> VAL{Validated?} VAL -->|No| VAL2[Optimize Assay
Increase n Recheck Pre analytics] VAL -->|Yes| CLIN[Clinical Assay Development
AUROC greater than 0.80] style S fill:#b3e5fc,stroke:#01579b style STAT fill:#fff59d,stroke:#f57f17 style CLIN fill:#a5d6a7,stroke:#1b5e20 ``` ## 4.4 Translational Pathway — From Bench to Bedside ```mermaid flowchart LR L1[Discovery Phase
Years 1 to 3] --> L2[Verification Phase
Years 2 to 4] L2 --> L3[Validation Phase
Years 3 to 6] L3 --> L4[Clinical Assay
Years 5 to 8] L4 --> L5[FDA Qualification
Years 7 to 10] L5 --> L6[Clinical Practice
Routine Use] L1 -.- R1[Proteomics Technology
Sample Size 30 to 100] L2 -.- R2[Targeted MS
Sample Size 100 to 300] L3 -.- R3[ELISA or MRM
Sample Size 300 to 1000] L4 -.- R4[CLIA Certified Lab] L5 -.- R5[FDA EMA Submission] L6 -.- R6[Clinical Guidelines
Reimbursement] style L1 fill:#ffcdd2,stroke:#c62828 style L6 fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20 ``` # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ## PART A QUESTIONS (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] **Define disease proteomics. List any four major disease categories where proteomics has contributed significantly.** **Model Answer:** **Disease Proteomics Definition (2 marks):** Disease proteomics is the large-scale, systematic study of the proteome (complete set of proteins and their modifications) in cells, tissues, or biofluids under pathological conditions, with the goal of identifying disease-associated biomarkers, understanding pathogenic mechanisms, and discovering therapeutic targets. It employs technologies such as 2D-PAGE, mass spectrometry, protein microarrays, and bioinformatics to compare normal vs. diseased protein expression profiles. **Four Major Disease Categories (1 mark — 0.25 each):** 1. **Cancer** — e.g., HER2 in breast cancer, PSA in prostate cancer 2. **Neurodegenerative diseases** — e.g., amyloid-β and tau in Alzheimer's disease 3. **Cardiovascular diseases** — e.g., troponins and BNP in myocardial infarction 4. **Infectious diseases** — e.g., cytokine profiling in COVID-19, antigen discovery in tuberculosis 5. *(Optional fifth: Autoimmune diseases — RA, SLE)* --- ### Question 2 — [KTU University Exam – June 2024] **What is a biomarker? Differentiate between prognostic and diagnostic biomarkers with one example each.** **Model Answer:** **Biomarker Definition (1.5 marks):** A **biomarker** (Biological Marker) is a objectively measurable characteristic that indicates normal biological processes, pathogenic processes, or pharmacological responses to therapeutic intervention. In proteomics, biomarkers are typically proteins or protein modifications measurable in body fluids or tissues. **Differentiation Table (1.5 marks):** | Feature | Diagnostic Biomarker | Prognostic Biomarker | |---|---|---| | **Purpose** | Confirms presence of disease | Predicts disease outcome / severity | | **Timing** | Used at initial detection | Used after diagnosis | | **Example** | **PSA** for prostate cancer detection | **HER2/neu overexpression** for aggressive breast cancer prognosis | | **Another example** | Troponin-I for MI diagnosis | **p53 mutation** — poor prognosis marker | --- ## PART B QUESTIONS (14 Marks Each) > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Pitfall Callout:** > - **Do NOT** confuse proteomics with genomics/transcriptomics in definitions; emphasize **proteins + PTMs**. > - **Always** cite specific disease examples (breast cancer, Alzheimer's, etc.) — generic answers lose 2-3 marks. > - **Avoid** vague phrasing like "proteomics helps in disease study"; instead, state "2D-PAGE coupled with MALDI-TOF MS identified differentially expressed proteins in X disease." > - **Draw** the workflow diagram in long-answer questions (worth up to 2 marks). > - **Cite AUROC values, sensitivity/specificity** wherever applicable — examiners reward quantitative reasoning. --- ### PART B — Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Adapted] **Q. (a)** Describe in detail the workflow of disease proteomics from clinical sample collection to biomarker validation. **(7 marks)** **Model Answer (Step-by-Step):** **Step 1: Clinical Cohort Design (1 mark)** - Recruit **cases** (disease-confirmed patients) and **controls** (age/sex-matched healthy individuals) - Define inclusion/exclusion criteria, ethical clearance (IEC/IRB approval) - Power analysis: typically **n ≥ 30** for discovery phase **Step 2: Sample Collection & Biobanking (1 mark)** - Standardized SOPs for collection time, storage temperature (-80°C), processing delay (<30 min) - Sample types: serum, plasma, urine, CSF, tissue biopsy, FFPE **Step 3: Protein Extraction & Pre-fractionation (1 mark)** - Lysis with urea/thiourea/CHAPS/DTT buffer - Depletion of high-abundance proteins (albumin, IgG) using immunoaffinity columns - Optional subcellular fractionation for tissue samples **Step 4: Separation & MS Analysis (2 marks)** - **2D-PAGE**: First dimension IEF (pH gradient), second dimension SDS-PAGE - **Liquid Chromatography**: nanoLC coupled online to MS - **Tandem MS**: ESI-Q-TOF or MALDI-TOF/TOF - Ionization: ESI (for LC-MS) or MALDI (for imaging) **Step 5: Bioinformatics & Statistical Analysis (1 mark)** - Database searching (MASCOT, Sequest HT) against UniProt/SwissProt - FDR < 1%; quantitation via LFQ, SILAC, iTRAQ, or TMT - Multiple testing correction (Benjamini-Hochberg) **Step 6: Biomarker Validation (1 mark)** - Orthogonal methods: ELISA, Western blot, IHC, targeted SRM/MRM - Independent cohort confirmation (n ≥ 100) **Q. (b)** Discuss the role of proteomics in cancer biomarker discovery. Provide at least three specific cancer biomarkers discovered through proteomic approaches. **(7 marks)** **Model Answer:** **Role of Proteomics in Cancer Biomarker Discovery (3 marks):** Cancer proteomics enables: 1. **Early detection** — identifying proteins elevated before clinical symptoms (e.g., CA-125 in ovarian cancer) 2. **Diagnosis** — distinguishing cancer subtypes (e.g., HER2+ vs. HER2- breast cancer) 3. **Prognosis** — predicting disease aggressiveness (e.g., uPA, PAI-1 in breast cancer) 4. **Therapeutic targeting** — identifying druggable proteins (e.g., EGFR, BRAF, ALK) 5. **Treatment monitoring** — tracking response via circulating tumor proteins 6. **Resistance mechanism elucidation** — comparing pre- vs. post-treatment proteomes **Three Specific Cancer Biomarkers (4 marks — 1.33 each):** **1. PSA (Prostate-Specific Antigen, KLK3) — Prostate Cancer (1.5 marks)** - Discovered via 2D-PAGE and characterized by mass spectrometry - Serine protease secreted by prostate epithelium - Clinical cut-off: **>4 ng/mL** suggests prostate cancer - Limitation: low specificity (elevated in BPH also) **2. HER2/neu (ERBB2) — Breast Cancer (1.5 marks)** - Receptor tyrosine kinase identified by proteomic profiling - Overexpressed in 15-20% of breast cancers - Companion diagnostic for **trastuzumab (Herceptin)** therapy - Detection: IHC (3+ score) or FISH amplification **3. CA 19-9 (Sialyl Lewis A) — Pancreatic Cancer (1 mark)** - Glycoprotein biomarker discovered via glycoproteomics - Elevated in pancreatic adenocarcinoma - Used for monitoring treatment response - Combined with CA 125 for ovarian cancer **4. *(Bonus, optional)* — CA-125 (MUC16) — Ovarian Cancer** - High-molecular-weight glycoprotein - FDA-approved for monitoring ovarian cancer recurrence - **Limitations:** Elevated in endometriosis, pregnancy --- ### PART B — Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Adapted] **Q. (a)** Explain the application of proteomics in neurodegenerative disease research. Discuss Alzheimer's disease and Parkinson's disease as case studies. **(7 marks)** **Model Answer:** **Introduction to Neurodegenerative Disease Proteomics (1 mark):** Neurodegenerative diseases involve progressive loss of neuronal function with characteristic protein aggregation. Proteomics helps identify misfolded proteins, PTM alterations, and pathway dysregulations. Major tools include 2D-DIGE, MS-based proteomics, and protein interaction studies. **Alzheimer's Disease (AD) — Proteomic Insights (3 marks):** **1. Amyloid-β (Aβ) Pathway (1.5 marks)** - Aβ42 peptide accumulates in extracellular plaques - CSF proteomics: decreased Aβ42, increased total tau and phosphorylated tau (p-tau181) - **Plasma biomarkers (recent):** p-tau181, p-tau217, GFAP, NfL — measurable via Simoa, IP-MS - **Discovery method:** Immunoprecipitation coupled with mass spectrometry (IP-MS) **2. Tau Pathology (1 mark)** - Hyperphosphorylated tau forms neurofibrillary tangles - Phosphoproteomics identified >40 phosphorylation sites - p-tau at Ser202/Thr205 (AT8 epitope) — diagnostic **3. Other AD Proteomic Findings (0.5 marks)** - Dysregulated synaptic proteins (synaptophysin, SNAP-25) - Mitochondrial dysfunction proteins - Neuroinflammation markers (YKL-40, sTREM2) **Parkinson's Disease (PD) — Proteomic Insights (3 marks):** **1. α-Synuclein Aggregation (1.5 marks)** - α-Synuclein misfolds into Lewy bodies - CSF and salivary proteomics show α-Syn oligomers - **PTM role:** phosphorylation at Ser129 (90% in Lewy bodies) - **Discovery method:** 2D-PAGE and LC-MS/MS **2. Mitochondrial Complex I Defects (1 mark)** - Proteomics revealed reduced subunits of Complex I (NDUFS1, NDUFS3) - Links PD to mitochondrial dysfunction **3. Other PD Biomarkers (0.5 marks)** - DJ-1 protein (oxidative stress response) - LRRK2 kinase (genetic + sporadic forms) - Neurofilament light chain (NfL) — non-specific **Q. (b)** Write a detailed note on pharmacoproteomics and its clinical applications. **(7 marks)** **Model Answer:** **Pharmacoproteomics Definition (1 mark):** Pharmacoproteomics is the study of the proteome's influence on drug efficacy, toxicity, and metabolism. It identifies protein biomarkers predicting drug response (responders vs. non-responders) and mechanisms of drug resistance. **Workflow of Pharmacoproteomics (2 marks):** 1. Baseline proteome profiling of patient cohort 2. Drug administration with response monitoring 3. Comparison of proteomes: responders vs. non-responders 4. Identification of predictive biomarkers 5. Validation in independent cohorts 6. Clinical assay development **Clinical Applications (4 marks):** **1. Personalized Cancer Therapy (1.5 marks)** - **HER2 testing** → trastuzumab (Herceptin) for breast cancer - **EGFR mutation** → erlotinib/gefitinib for NSCLC - **BCR-ABL kinase** → imatinib for CML - **ALK rearrangement** → crizotinib for NSCLC **2. Predicting Drug Toxicity (1 mark)** - Proteomic profiling of hepatocytes to predict hepatotoxicity - Cardiotoxicity prediction via cardiomyocyte proteomics (e.g., doxorubicin) **3. Identifying Drug Resistance Mechanisms (1 mark)** - Comparing proteomes of sensitive vs. resistant tumors - Example: BRAF inhibitor resistance via NRAS upregulation or RAF dimerization - Example: Tamoxifen resistance via HER2/MAPK pathway activation **4. Therapeutic Drug Monitoring (0.5 marks)** - Direct measurement of drug target engagement - Example: Immunosuppressant monitoring in transplant patients **Challenges and Future Directions (Optional, for full marks):** - **Inter-individual variability** in proteomes - **Dynamic range** issues in plasma - **Standardization** across laboratories - **Integration with genomics/transcriptomics** for holistic precision medicine --- ## TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Yield Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 4: Proteomics in Study of Diseases** ### Core Concepts to Memorize - **Disease Proteomics =** Study of proteins + PTMs in pathological states (NOT just mRNA or DNA) - **Three Pillars:** Discovery, Targeted, Functional Proteomics - **Central Limitation:** mRNA ≠ Protein (correlation 0.4–0.6); >200 PTMs not captured by transcriptomics - **>95% of drugs target proteins** — proteomics is clinically indispensable ### Key Workflow Steps (Must draw in 7+ mark questions) 1. Clinical cohort design (cases + controls, n ≥ 30) 2. Sample collection (SOPs, -80°C storage) 3. Protein extraction + high-abundance depletion 4. Separation (2D-PAGE / nanoLC) → MS (ESI/MALDI) 5. Bioinformatics (MASCOT/Sequest, FDR < 1%) 6. Validation (ELISA / WB / IHC / MRM, independent cohort) 7. Clinical translation (AUROC ≥ 0.80, FDA qualification) ### Must-Know Biomarkers (Disease–Biomarker–Sample Triads) - **Cancer:** PSA (prostate), CA-125 (ovary), HER2 (breast), CA 19-9 (pancreas) - **Neuro:** Aβ42 + p-tau (Alzheimer's CSF/plasma), α-Synuclein (Parkinson's) - **Cardio:** Troponin-I/T (MI), NT-proBNP (heart failure) - **Infectious:** CRP + IL-6 (sepsis/COVID), TB antigen detection ### Critical Equations to Remember - **Fold Change:** $\text{FC} = \dfrac{Ab_D}{Ab_C}$ - **Log₂FC threshold:** $\vert\log_2 \text{FC}\vert \geq 1$ - **Sample size formula:** $n = \left( \dfrac{2.80 \cdot \sigma}{\Delta} \right)^2$ - **AUROC:** Perfect = 1.0, Random = 0.5; clinical adoption ≥ 0.80 - **Bonferroni:** $\alpha/m$; **BH-FDR:** $p_{(i)} \leq (i/m) \cdot q$ ### Technologies to Name in Long Answers - **2D-PAGE / 2D-DIGE** — classical separation - **MALDI-TOF/TOF** — MALDI ionization, TOF analyzer - **ESI-Q-TOF / Orbitrap** — LC-MS/MS gold standard - **SILAC, iTRAQ, TMT** — quantitative labeling - **LFQ (Label-Free Quantification)** — MaxQuant Andromeda - **SRM / MRM / PRM** — targeted validation - **Simoa (Single Molecule Array)** — ultra-sensitive (sub-fg/mL) - **Protein microarrays** — high-throughput screening - **MALDI-MSI** — spatial tissue proteomics ### Common Examiner Pitfalls to Avoid - ❌ Saying "proteomics = study of DNA" (incorrect; proteins, not DNA) - ❌ Confusing SILAC (metabolic) with iTRAQ/TMT (chemical labeling) - ❌ Stating fold-change without specifying direction (up/down vs. control) - ❌ Forgetting FDR / multiple testing correction - ❌ Generic "biomarker discovery" without naming specific diseases/proteins - ❌ Omitting the validation step in the workflow diagram ### High-Yield Numbers for Memory - **FDR threshold:** < 1% (peptide), < 5% (protein) - **Fold change threshold:** ≥ 1.5 (or ≥ 2.0 strict) - **AUROC clinical:** ≥ 0.80 - **Biomarker sample sizes:** Discovery ≥ 30; Validation ≥ 100 - **Plasma dynamic range:** ~10⁶ to 10⁷ - **Top 22 plasma proteins:** ~99% of total mass - **FDA-approved protein drugs:** >200 (biologics era) ### One-Line Takeaways for Each Disease Area - **Cancer:** Proteomics identifies druggable targets & subtype-specific biomarkers - **Neurodegeneration:** CSF/plasma proteomics enables early AD/PD detection - **Cardiovascular:** Troponins + BNP revolutionized MI and HF diagnostics - **Infectious:** Cytokine storms (IL-6, TNF-α) guide immunomodulatory therapy - **Pharmacoproteomics:** Predicts responders/non-responders → personalized therapy

topics.title: Storage, Transportation and Processing of Clinical Samples
Proteomic Analysis of Body Fluids
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBBT756 - PROTEOMICS & PROTEIN ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Proteomic Analysis of Body Fluids

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

**Body Fluid Proteomics** is a specialized sub-discipline of clinical proteomics that involves the systematic, large-scale identification, quantification, and functional characterization of the entire protein complement (proteome) present in various biological fluids of the human body — including **blood (plasma/serum)**, **urine**, **cerebrospinal fluid (CSF)**, **saliva**, **tears**, **amniotic fluid**, **synovial fluid**, **bronchoalveolar lavage (BAL)**, and **sweat**.

The central objective of this field is the **discovery of clinically validated biomarkers** for early disease detection, prognosis, therapeutic monitoring, and personalized medicine. It is governed by initiatives such as the **Human Proteome Organization (HUPO) Plasma Proteome Project (PPP)** and is technically constrained by the extreme dynamic range of protein concentrations spanning **>10 orders of magnitude** (from mg/mL of albumin down to pg/mL of cytokines).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Body fluids are the most clinically accessible sample source because they are obtained via **minimally invasive procedures**, can be collected repeatedly, and reflect the **physiological and pathological state** of tissues they perfuse or are derived from.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

Imagine you are a forensic scientist trying to identify everything that is happening inside a large, busy city (the body). The city does not let you walk into buildings (organs) directly, but it continuously leaks signals out through rivers, sewage, and garbage trucks.

- **Body fluids (blood, urine, sweat, saliva)** = these rivers, sewers, and waste systems.
- **Proteins in these fluids** = the messages, fingerprints, and chemical clues being dumped out by every household (organ) in the city.
- **Mass spectrometer** = the high-tech forensic lab that can read every single clue.
- **Dynamic range problem** = equivalent to trying to hear a whisper from a single person in a stadium full of 10 million screaming fans.

By cataloging and comparing these protein "clues" between **healthy** and **diseased** individuals, scientists can identify **unique biomarkers** — like discovering that a particular type of industrial waste (a specific protein) only appears in the sewage (urine) when a particular factory (organ) is malfunctioning.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Standard Metrics to Remember:**
> - Plasma protein concentration range: **~10¹⁰ fold** (albumin ~45 mg/mL vs. IL-6 ~1 pg/mL)
> - Human plasma proteome size: **>10,000 distinct proteins** (estimated)
> - CSF total protein: **0.15–0.45 mg/mL** (~200× lower than plasma)

## 1.3 Why Body Fluid Proteomics Matters

| Clinical Driver | Role of Proteomics |
|-----------------|---------------------|
| **Disease Biomarker Discovery** | Identify proteins whose abundance correlates with pathology |
| **Early Diagnosis** | Detect disease before clinical symptoms manifest |
| **Therapeutic Monitoring** | Track drug response via protein signatures |
| **Pathway Elucidation** | Map disease mechanisms through protein networks |
| **Personalized Medicine** | Stratify patients based on molecular profiles |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Major Body Fluids and Their Proteomes

### 2.1.1 Blood: Plasma vs. Serum

- **Plasma** = liquid component of blood obtained by adding **anticoagulants** (EDTA, heparin, citrate) before centrifugation. Contains all clotting factors including **fibrinogen**.
- **Serum** = liquid obtained after **clotting** has occurred (no anticoagulant). Fibrinogen and clotting factors are largely depleted.
- Plasma is **clinically preferred** for proteomics because the proteome is more stable, reproducible, and complete.

### 2.1.2 Urine

- A "**proximal fluid**" — directly reflects kidney and urogenital tract function.
- **Advantage:** far less complex than plasma (~1,500–2,000 proteins detectable).
- Excellent for **kidney disease, bladder cancer, prostate cancer** biomarkers.
- Daily output: **1–2 L**, protein concentration **<100 mg/mL**.

### 2.1.3 Cerebrospinal Fluid (CSF)

- Ultrafiltrate of plasma produced by the **choroid plexus**.
- Closest accessible fluid reflecting **brain biochemistry** (BBB limits large proteins).
- Critical for **Alzheimer's, Parkinson's, multiple sclerosis, brain tumors**.
- Volume: **~150 mL total**, turned over **3–4 times/day**.

### 2.1.4 Saliva, Tears, BAL, Amniotic & Synovial Fluids

- **Saliva** — emerging for oral, systemic, and stress biomarkers.
- **Tears** — used for ocular diseases (e.g., dry eye, diabetic retinopathy).
- **Bronchoalveolar Lavage (BAL)** — lung proteome access.
- **Amniotic fluid** — fetal health, prenatal diagnostics.
- **Synovial fluid** — arthritis, joint disease markers.

## 2.2 The Central Technical Challenge: Dynamic Range

The fundamental problem in body fluid proteomics is the **dynamic range** of protein concentrations. Plasma alone contains:

$$ \text{Dynamic Range (Plasma)} \approx 10^{10} \text{ to } 10^{12} $$

Albumin constitutes **~55% of total plasma protein** (~45 mg/mL), while clinically important cytokines exist at **1–10 pg/mL**. **MS instruments typically detect only 3–4 orders of magnitude in a single run.**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The 90/10 Rule of Plasma Proteomics:**
> Only **~22 proteins** constitute **99% of plasma protein mass**; the remaining **>10,000 proteins** (low-abundance, disease-relevant) are masked.

## 2.3 Sample Preparation Workflow — The Heart of the Method

| Step | Purpose | Common Methods |
|------|---------|----------------|
| **1. Collection & Stabilization** | Prevent proteolysis | Protease inhibitor cocktail (PMSF, aprotinin, leupeptin); immediate cooling |
| **2. Depletion of High-Abundance Proteins (HAPs)** | Unmask low-abundance proteins | Immunoaffinity (MARS-14, Agilent Hu-14 removes albumin, IgG, IgA, transferrin, haptoglobin, α1-antitrypsin, etc.) |
| **3. Pre-fractionation** | Reduce sample complexity | 1D/2D PAGE, LC (SCX, SAX, HILIC, RP), OFFGEL electrophoresis |
| **4. Digestion** | Generate peptides | Trypsin (cleaves after K, R, not before P) |
| **5. Peptide Fractionation** | Orthogonal separation | Nano-LC, capillary electrophoresis |
| **6. MS Analysis** | Identify/quantify | LC-MS/MS (Orbitrap, Q-TOF, Q-Orbitrap) |

## 2.4 Depletion Strategies — Detailed Mechanisms

### 2.4.1 Immunoaffinity Depletion
- **Mechanism:** Antibodies specific to the top HAPs (typically **top 6, 12, or 20**) immobilized on a column.
- **Examples:** **Multiple Affinity Removal System (MARS)** — Human 14 (Agilent); **ProteoPrep 20** (Sigma).
- **Risk:** **"Untargeted" loss** — some low-abundance proteins bind non-specifically to the antibodies or are co-depleted along with their binding partners (e.g., cytokines bound to albumin).

### 2.4.2 Alternative Depletion Methods
- **Cibacron Blue dye affinity** — selectively binds albumin.
- **Protein A/G affinity** — removes immunoglobulins.
- **Ammonium sulfate precipitation** — bulk fractionation, crude but inexpensive.
- **Acetone/TCA precipitation** — total protein recovery, loss of low-MW species.
- **Size-exclusion chromatography (SEC)** — separates HAPs from LMW proteins.

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Parameter / Concept | Formula / Value | Units | Significance |
|---------------------|-----------------|-------|--------------|
| Dynamic range of plasma proteome | $10^{10} - 10^{12}$ | dimensionless | Technical challenge |
| Plasma volume | ~3 L | Liters | Sample availability |
| CSF total protein | 0.15–0.45 | mg/mL | Lower complexity |
| Trypsin specificity | cleaves at K↓ and R↓, not P-K/R | — | Peptide generation |
| HUPO PPP target | 10,000 proteins in plasma | count | Benchmark |
| % of plasma mass in top 22 proteins | ~99% | % | Why depletion is essential |
| Typical MS scan range | 350–1500 | m/z | m/z acquisition window |
| % acetonitrile in LC gradient | 5–40% | % v/v | Peptide elution |
| Collision energy (CID) | 0.02 × m/z | eV | MS/MS fragmentation |
| Albumin concentration | ~45 | mg/mL | Reference HAP |
| Cytokine (e.g., IL-6) | ~1–10 | pg/mL | Reference LAP |
| Plasma to serum conversion | Serum ≈ Plasma − Fibrinogen | — | Sample type matters |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Substitution Note:** Trypsin cleaves the peptide bond on the C-terminal side of **Lys (K)** and **Arg (R)** residues, **except when the next residue is Proline (P)**.

## 2.6 Engineering / Clinical Utility

| Field | Application |
|-------|-------------|
| **Oncology** | CA-125, PSA, HER2 — protein biomarkers in body fluids |
| **Cardiology** | Troponin I/T, BNP for MI & heart failure |
| **Neurology** | Aβ42, tau, NfL in CSF for Alzheimer's |
| **Nephrology** | NGAL, KIM-1 in urine for AKI |
| **Infectious Disease** | CRP, procalcitonin for sepsis |
| **Pharma Industry** | Companion diagnostics, drug target validation |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: The Dynamic Range Problem (Mathematical Basis)

### 3.1.1 Problem Statement

The dynamic range of a proteome (R) is the ratio between the concentration of the **most abundant** and **least abundant** detectable protein species:

$$ R = \frac{C_{\text{max}}}{C_{\text{min}}} $$

### 3.1.2 Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1: Define the maximum and minimum plasma protein concentrations**

$$
C_{\text{max}} = \text{Albumin concentration} = 4.5 \times 10^{1} \; \text{mg/mL}
$$

$$
C_{\text{min}} = \text{IL-6 (interleukin-6) concentration} = 1.0 \times 10^{-9} \; \text{mg/mL}
$$

**Step 2: Substitute into the dynamic range formula**

$$
\begin{aligned}
